title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: For coprime a + b = c, c is bounded in terms of the radical (product of distinct primes) of abc; rad(abc)^(1+ε) dominates c for all but finitely many triples.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: History — The abc Conjecture
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The abc conjecture grew out of the rich soil of late-twentieth-century Diophantine geometry, where a single recurring theme — that solutions of polynomial equations cannot be simultaneously "small" and "highly divisible by large prime powers" — was crystallizing into precise statements. Its proximate stimulus was the **Mason–Stothers theorem** (W. W. Stothers, 1981; rediscovered by R. C. Mason, 1984), the polynomial analogue: for coprime polynomials $a(t)+b(t)=c(t)$ over a field, not all constant, one has $\max(\deg a,\deg b,\deg c) \le N_0(abc)-1$, where $N_0$ counts distinct roots. This is an *exact*, *unconditional*, *easily proved* statement. The abc conjecture is the bold proposal that an analogue holds for integers, where the role of "number of distinct roots" is played by the **radical** $\mathrm{rad}(n)=\prod_{p\mid n}p$.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: History — The abc Conjecture
text:
**Precise formulation.** For coprime positive integers with $a+b=c$, define the radical $\mathrm{rad}(abc)$. The conjecture (Masser–Oesterlé form) asserts: for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a constant $K_\varepsilon$ such that
$$ c \;<\; K_\varepsilon \cdot \mathrm{rad}(abc)^{\,1+\varepsilon} $$
for all such triples. Equivalently, defining the *quality* $q(a,b,c)=\dfrac{\log c}{\log \mathrm{rad}(abc)}$, only finitely many triples have $q>1+\varepsilon$. The $\varepsilon$ is genuinely necessary: triples with $q>1$ exist infinitely often (e.g. the family built from $9^n \mid 3^{2^n}-1$ generated from $1+(2^{6n}-1)=2^{6n}$), so the naive statement with $\varepsilon=0$ is false. The conjecture is striking because it implies, often effortlessly, Fermat's Last Theorem for large exponents, the Mordell conjecture (with effective bounds, via Elkies), Roth's theorem, Wieferich-prime infinitude, Erdős–Woods, and much more.

**Reformulations.** Oesterlé phrased a version while examining the Szpiro conjecture relating the conductor and minimal discriminant of elliptic curves; indeed abc is essentially equivalent to a uniform Szpiro inequality. Vojta later embedded abc into his sweeping conjectures on heights in Diophantine geometry, recasting the radical as a truncated counting function and the inequality as a height bound mirroring the Second Main Theorem of Nevanlinna theory — making abc the arithmetic shadow of value-distribution theory.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1981** — W. W. Stothers proves the polynomial degree inequality later called Mason–Stothers.
- **1983** — L. Szpiro formulates the Szpiro conjecture on elliptic-curve conductors and discriminants, a close cousin.
- **1984** — R. C. Mason independently publishes the function-field theorem, popularizing the analogy.
- **1985** — Joseph Oesterlé (in a Séminaire Bourbaki context on Szpiro) and David Masser (at a Cambridge meeting) formulate the integer conjecture; Masser states the clean $\mathrm{rad}(abc)^{1+\varepsilon}$ form.
- **1988** — Noam Elkies shows abc implies an *effective* Mordell/Faltings, with explicit height bounds.
- **1991** — Paul Vojta connects abc to his height conjectures and the Nevanlinna dictionary.
- **1994** — C. L. Stewart and Kunrui Yu prove the first unconditional lower-bound progress: $\log c \ll \mathrm{rad}(abc)^{1/3+\varepsilon}$ via linear forms in logarithms (Baker's method).
- **2001** — Stewart–Yu sharpen the exponent to $1/3$ with refined constants.
- **2006** — A. Granville and T. Tucker survey and popularize abc ("It's As Easy As abc").
- **2007** — ABC@home distributed-computing project begins exhaustively cataloguing high-quality triples.
- **August 2012** — Shinichi Mochizuki releases four papers on Inter-universal Teichmüller theory (IUT) claiming a proof.
- **2015–2016** — Oxford and Kyoto workshops convene; the proof's Corollary 3.12 becomes the contested crux.
- **2018** — Peter Scholze and Jakob Stix circulate a report asserting a gap in the inequality of Corollary 3.12; Mochizuki rebuts.
- **2020** — Mochizuki's papers are accepted by *Publications of the RIMS* (of which he is chief editor), intensifying controversy.
- **2021** — Kirti Joshi and others announce alternative arithmetic-Teichmüller frameworks aiming to clarify or reconstruct the claims.
- **Present** — The conjecture is regarded as **open** by the mainstream community; the IUT proof remains disputed and not independently validated.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The abc Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The abc conjecture carries two names by convention — **Joseph Oesterlé** and **David Masser** — reflecting a near-simultaneous formulation around 1985 from two complementary vantage points: Oesterlé's from the theory of elliptic curves and the Szpiro conjecture, Masser's from transcendence theory and the function-field analogy. The pairing is itself a small lesson in how conjectures crystallize: not from a single flash but from a shared atmosphere of analogies whose time has come.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Joseph Oesterlé
text: Joseph Oesterlé (born 1954) is a French number theorist, long associated with the Université Pierre et Marie Curie (Paris VI) and the Institut de Mathématiques de Jussieu. Trained in the elite tradition of French arithmetic geometry, he is known for deep work on modular forms, the class-number problem (the **Gross–Zagier–Oesterlé** resolution of the imaginary quadratic class-number-one and -two problems), and on Bernoulli numbers. Oesterlé arrived at abc while reflecting on **Lucien Szpiro's conjecture**, which bounds the minimal discriminant of an elliptic curve over $\mathbb{Q}$ in terms of its conductor. Translating Szpiro's inequality through the Frey curve $y^2 = x(x-a)(x+b)$ attached to an abc triple, Oesterlé saw that a uniform discriminant–conductor bound was tantamount to an inequality between $c$ and $\mathrm{rad}(abc)$. He presented this circle of ideas in the Séminaire Bourbaki orbit, where the conjecture's potency — its capacity to encode Fermat, Mordell, and Roth at once — became visible. Oesterlé's instinct was structural: abc was, to him, the arithmetic content of a height inequality already glimpsed in the geometry of elliptic curves.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: David Masser
text: David William Masser (born 1948) is a British mathematician, a leading figure in **transcendence theory** and Diophantine approximation, who spent much of his career at the University of Basel after positions in the UK. A student of Alan Baker — whose theory of linear forms in logarithms reshaped effective Diophantine analysis — Masser inherited a sharp feel for how the size of integers is constrained by their prime factorizations. He is celebrated for the **Masser–Wüstholz** isogeny theorem (with Gisbert Wüstholz), a cornerstone of the modern theory of heights on abelian varieties, and for foundational results on elliptic functions and unlikely intersections. Masser was struck by the **Mason–Stothers theorem**, the polynomial identity in which the degree of a sum is controlled by the number of distinct roots of the product. Pressing the function-field/number-field analogy — in which "number of distinct roots" corresponds to "radical" and "degree" to "logarithmic height" — he proposed the integer inequality in its now-standard clean form $c < K_\varepsilon\,\mathrm{rad}(abc)^{1+\varepsilon}$, including the crucial recognition that the $\varepsilon$ cannot be removed.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: The joint formulation and its legacy
text: The two formulations are equivalent in substance, and the community fused them. What makes the conjecture extraordinary is not its statement, which a strong undergraduate can grasp, but its reach: it linearizes a swath of Diophantine geometry, converting hard finiteness theorems into near-corollaries. Both originators went on to broader legacies — Oesterlé in modular and analytic number theory, Masser in transcendence and unlikely intersections (the Zilber–Pink program owes much to his school). Neither claimed the conjecture would be easy; both understood it as a deep prediction about the multiplicative–additive tension at the heart of the integers. That tension, articulated in 1985, remains unresolved and continues to organize a large part of arithmetic geometry around a single inequality.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The abc Conjecture
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

The abc conjecture has resisted every classical tool. The unconditional bounds remain *exponentially* far from the truth, while the strategies that would close the gap are either conjectural themselves or, in the most ambitious case, disputed. Below are the principal lines of attack.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Baker's method (linear forms in logarithms)
text:
The only approach to have produced *unconditional* progress. By bounding linear forms $\Lambda = b_1\log\alpha_1+\cdots+b_n\log\alpha_n$ from below (Baker, Wüstholz; $p$-adic forms via Yu Kunrui), one controls how closely a smooth number can approximate another, hence how large $c$ can be relative to $\mathrm{rad}(abc)$.

- **Best result:** Stewart–Yu (1991, 2001) proved $\log c \ll_\varepsilon \mathrm{rad}(abc)^{1/3+\varepsilon}$, with later refinements of the constants.
- **Barrier:** The method is intrinsically *exponential* — it bounds $\log c$ by a power of $\mathrm{rad}(abc)$, whereas the conjecture demands $\log c \ll \log \mathrm{rad}(abc)$. The gap is not a matter of constants; linear forms in logarithms cannot, even in principle, reach a polynomial-in-$\log$ bound. This is a fundamental ceiling on the technique.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: The Frey curve / Szpiro reduction
text:
abc is essentially equivalent to a uniform **Szpiro inequality** $|\Delta_E| \ll N_E^{6+\varepsilon}$ for elliptic curves, via the Frey curve attached to a triple. This recasts the problem in arithmetic geometry, where one may deploy modularity, Galois representations, and the theory of conductors and discriminants.

- **Best result:** The equivalence is exact, and modularity (Wiles, Taylor–Wiles, BCDT) makes the elliptic-curve side fully systematic — but proving the *uniform* Szpiro bound is exactly as hard as abc.
- **Barrier:** No mechanism in the modularity toolkit produces the required uniformity over all curves; one only re-encodes the difficulty.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Vojta's conjectures and the Nevanlinna dictionary
text:
Vojta embedded abc into a vast height-inequality program mirroring the Second Main Theorem of value-distribution theory: the radical plays the role of a truncated counting function, and abc becomes a special case of a conjectural arithmetic SMT for curves and higher-dimensional varieties.

- **Best result:** The *function-field* and *holomorphic* analogues are theorems (Mason–Stothers; the holomorphic abc / SMT of Nevanlinna–Cartan). These give complete proofs in the geometric setting.
- **Barrier:** The arithmetic case lacks a derivative. Function-field and Nevanlinna proofs use a Wronskian/logarithmic-derivative that has no integer counterpart; transplanting the proof founders precisely there. Vojta's full conjecture is strictly harder than abc.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Inter-universal Teichmüller theory (IUT) — Mochizuki
text:
Shinichi Mochizuki's program (2012) attacks a Szpiro-type inequality by deforming the multiplicative and additive structures of a number field across "distinct universes," tracking the distortion via the theory of Hodge theaters, theta-links, and the multiradial algorithm. The claimed payoff (Corollary 3.12) is an inequality yielding abc.

- **Claimed result:** A full proof of abc (and Szpiro, Vojta-for-curves) with effective constants.
- **Barrier / dispute:** **Scholze and Stix (2018)** argue that the central inequality of Corollary 3.12 either is unjustified or collapses to a triviality once certain identifications are made explicit; they contend a crucial "log-volume" comparison loses the needed quantitative content. Mochizuki rejects this reading. The proof has **not** achieved independent community validation and is treated as unverified. This is a genuine, open dispute, not a settled refutation.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Elementary / probabilistic and computational heuristics
text:
Conjectural sharpenings (Robert–Stewart–Tenenbaum) predict the precise extremal shape, $\log c < \log\mathrm{rad}(abc) + O\!\big(\sqrt{\log\mathrm{rad}}/\log\log\mathrm{rad}\big)$, refining how the $\varepsilon$ should behave.

- **Best result:** Massive computation (ABC@home, Reken mee met ABC) verifies abc for all triples up to large bounds and supplies the highest-quality known triples — e.g. $q \approx 1.6299$ for $2 + 3^{10}\cdot109 = 23^5$.
- **Barrier:** Heuristics and data are consistent with abc but prove nothing; the parity-of-evidence problem is that no finite verification constrains the infinitely many large triples that matter.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The abc Conjecture
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: The Mochizuki claim (2012–present) — disputed
text:
The dominant episode in the conjecture's history is **Shinichi Mochizuki's** 2012 announcement of a proof via **Inter-universal Teichmüller theory** (IUT), a body of work spanning roughly 500 pages across four papers ("Inter-universal Teichmüller Theory I–IV"). Mochizuki, a number theorist of high standing (Princeton PhD under Faltings, professor at the RIMS, Kyoto), claimed to prove a Szpiro-type inequality strong enough to yield abc, the Vojta conjecture for hyperbolic curves, and effective Mordell.

The work resisted assimilation. Multiple workshops (Oxford 2015; Kyoto 2016) failed to transmit the proof to the broader community; experts reported being unable to locate the load-bearing inferential step in concrete terms. The crux is **Corollary 3.12** of IUT III. In March 2018, **Peter Scholze and Jakob Stix** visited Kyoto and afterward circulated a manuscript, *"Why abc is still a conjecture,"* arguing that the inequality of Corollary 3.12 is unjustified: in their reconstruction, the identifications used to relate the deformed copies of the number field cause the claimed quantitative gain to vanish, leaving at most a trivial bound. Mochizuki responded with detailed rebuttals asserting that Scholze–Stix had over-simplified — specifically, that they collapsed distinct labelled copies that IUT must keep distinct — and thereby misread the theory.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: The Mochizuki claim (2012–present) — disputed
text: The dispute remains **unresolved**. The papers were published in *Publications of the RIMS* in 2020/2021, but because Mochizuki is the journal's chief editor and the review occurred within his home institute, publication did not confer the independent verification the community expects. The neutral statement of record: the proof is **not accepted as established** by the mainstream arithmetic-geometry community, and the specific Scholze–Stix objection to Corollary 3.12 has not been answered to that community's satisfaction. The abc conjecture is therefore still regarded as open. (The institute classifies its status as a "disputed-claim" precisely to capture this state of affairs.)

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Near-misses and genuine partial results
text:
- **Stewart–Yu (1991, 2001).** The strongest *unconditional* statement: $\log c \ll_\varepsilon \mathrm{rad}(abc)^{1/3+\varepsilon}$ via $p$-adic linear forms in logarithms. A real theorem, but exponentially weaker than the conjecture.
- **Mason–Stothers (1981/1984) and the holomorphic abc.** Complete proofs of the *function-field* and *complex-analytic* analogues. These show the conjecture is "morally true" and isolate exactly which tool (a derivative/Wronskian) the integer case lacks.
- **Elkies (1988).** Not an attempt on abc but a landmark consequence: abc $\Rightarrow$ effective Mordell, dramatizing the stakes.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Erroneous and withdrawn elementary "proofs"
text: As with many famous, easily stated conjectures, abc has attracted numerous short purported proofs and disproofs, almost always claiming an elementary inequality or a counterexample. The most-cited cautionary point is structural: any "proof" with $\varepsilon=0$ is *necessarily wrong*, because infinitely many triples have quality $q>1$ — the constructive families (e.g. those exploiting $2^{6n}\equiv 1 \pmod{9}$) furnish explicit counterexamples to the $\varepsilon$-free statement. Claimed counterexamples to the full conjecture, by contrast, have never withstood the extensive computational record (ABC@home and successors), which verifies abc for all triples below large bounds and locates the genuine extremal triples, of which the record quality is only $\approx 1.6299$.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of arithmetic geometry, transcendence theory, and Diophantine analysis. Three currents are visible. First, a small group around **Mochizuki** at the RIMS (Kyoto), together with collaborators and expositors such as **Ivan Fesenko**, **Yuichiro Hoshi**, and **Go Yamashita**, continues to develop, defend, and explain IUT. Second, a critical mainstream — anchored by **Peter Scholze** and **Jakob Stix**, and broadly the arithmetic-geometry community trained in perfectoid and $p$-adic Hodge-theoretic methods — maintains that the proof's Corollary 3.12 is unjustified and that abc remains open. Third, an independent strand led by **Kirti Joshi** is attempting alternative "arithmetic Teichmüller" foundations that might either reconstruct or supersede the IUT machinery. Surrounding all of this, **Andrew Granville**, **Michel Waldschmidt**, **Andrew Sutherland**, and the computational community (descendants of the ABC@home / Reken-mee-met-ABC projects) keep the empirical and expository record current. The result is a field in unusual epistemic tension: a high-profile claimed proof coexisting with a widely held view that the central inequality is still conjectural.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The abc Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Current status: open, with a prominent disputed claim.** The mainstream arithmetic-geometry community regards the abc conjecture as unproven. A claimed proof exists — Shinichi Mochizuki's Inter-universal Teichmüller theory (IUT), *Inter-universal Teichmüller Theory I–IV*, released 2012, published in *Publications of the RIMS* in 2020/2021 — but it has not achieved independent verification, and a specific, documented objection to its central step stands unanswered to the community's satisfaction. The institute classifies the problem as a **disputed-claim** for exactly this reason.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: What is known unconditionally
text:
The strongest unconditional result is **Stewart–Yu** (1991; sharpened 2001): for coprime $a+b=c$,
$$ \log c \;\ll_\varepsilon\; \mathrm{rad}(abc)^{\,1/3+\varepsilon}. $$
This is *exponentially* weaker than the conjecture, which demands $\log c \ll (1+\varepsilon)\log\mathrm{rad}(abc) + O(1)$. The gap is structural: the only engine producing unconditional bounds, Baker's theory of linear forms in logarithms, cannot in principle deliver a polynomial-in-$\log$ bound. Computationally, abc is verified for all triples below very large bounds, and the record extremal triple has quality only $q\approx 1.6299$ ($2 + 3^{10}\cdot 109 = 23^5$), consistent with — but not proving — the conjecture.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: What is known conditionally / by analogy
text:
- The **function-field** analogue (Mason–Stothers, 1981/84) and the **holomorphic/Nevanlinna** analogue are full theorems. They establish the conjecture's "moral truth" and pinpoint the missing ingredient: an integer analogue of the derivative/Wronskian.
- abc is **equivalent** to a uniform Szpiro inequality and is a special case of **Vojta's** height conjectures.
- abc would *imply*, often immediately: asymptotic Fermat, effective Mordell (Elkies), Roth's theorem with effective constants, infinitude of non-Wieferich primes, Erdős–Woods, the Granville–Langevin conjecture, and bounds on Brocard–Ramanujan-type equations.

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution requires
text:
A genuine proof must produce a bound of the form $\log c \le (1+\varepsilon)\log\mathrm{rad}(abc) + C_\varepsilon$ holding for all but finitely many triples — a *polynomial-in-log* control with explicit $\varepsilon$-dependence. The two live routes are: (i) **rehabilitate or refute IUT**, by making Corollary 3.12 of IUT III either rigorous and quantitatively non-trivial (against the Scholze–Stix reading) or definitively wrong; and (ii) **find a new arithmetic mechanism** supplying the "derivative" that the function-field proof uses — whether through Joshi's arithmetic-Teichmüller spaces, $p$-adic Hodge-theoretic comparison, or an as-yet-unknown idea. No incremental sharpening of Baker's method can reach the conjecture; a qualitatively new input is mandatory.

The neutral assessment of record: abc remains one of the central unsolved problems of number theory. The Mochizuki proof is cited as a claim, not as established fact, and its key inequality is the subject of an unresolved technical dispute (Scholze–Stix, 2018).

title: The abc Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Beal Conjecture](../beal-conjecture/README.md) — a Diophantine cousin on common-factor constraints in $A^x+B^y=C^z$, in the same radical/power circle of ideas.
- [Fermat-type / Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer](../birch-swinnerton-dyer/README.md) — elliptic-curve arithmetic adjacent to the Frey-curve/Szpiro reformulation.
- [Schanuel Conjecture](../schanuel-conjecture/README.md) — a transcendence-theory grand conjecture, sibling in the "deep input from algebraic independence" sense.
- [Brocard Problem](../brocard-problem/README.md) — $n!+1=m^2$, a finiteness question that abc would constrain.
- [Erdős–Straus Conjecture](../erdos-straus-conjecture/README.md) — another elementary-to-state, hard Diophantine problem in the same neighborhood.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: Every balanced presentation of the trivial group can be reduced to the trivial presentation by elementary (Andrews–Curtis) moves.

*Originators: James J. Andrews, Morton L. Curtis.*

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: History — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture (AC) was posed in 1965 by James J. Andrews and Morton L. Curtis in a short, technically dense note in the *Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society* titled "Free groups and handlebodies." Its roots lie in low-dimensional topology — specifically in the theory of $2$-dimensional CW-complexes and the geometry of handle decompositions — but the statement itself is purely combinatorial-group-theoretic, which is the source of both its accessibility and its notorious intractability.

The setting is that of group presentations. A presentation $\langle x_1,\dots,x_n \mid r_1,\dots,r_m\rangle$ is called **balanced** if $n=m$, i.e. the number of generators equals the number of relators. The trivial group $\{1\}$ admits the obvious trivial balanced presentation $\langle x_1,\dots,x_n \mid x_1,\dots,x_n\rangle$. The conjecture asserts:

> Every balanced presentation of the trivial group can be transformed into the trivial presentation of the same deficiency by a finite sequence of **elementary Andrews–Curtis moves**.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: History — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
text:
> Every balanced presentation of the trivial group can be transformed into the trivial presentation of the same deficiency by a finite sequence of **elementary Andrews–Curtis moves**.

The AC-moves on the relator tuple are: (1) replace a relator $r_i$ by $r_i^{-1}$; (2) replace $r_i$ by $r_i r_j$ ($j\neq i$); (3) replace $r_i$ by a conjugate $w r_i w^{-1}$ for any word $w$ in the generators; and (4) the stabilization/destabilization move that adds or removes a generator $x_{n+1}$ together with the relator $x_{n+1}$. The variant that forbids move (4) — keeping the number of generators fixed — is the **simple (or elementary) Andrews–Curtis Conjecture (AC')**, while the **stable** version permits it. The stable conjecture is implied by, and is formally weaker than, the simple one.

The deeper motivation is the connection between these Nielsen-type transformations and $3$-deformations of $2$-complexes (the Whitehead simple-homotopy equivalences that add or remove cells of dimension $\le 3$). Andrews and Curtis showed the question is equivalent to asking whether every contractible $2$-complex $3$-deforms to a point. It is therefore a combinatorial cousin of questions surrounding the Poincaré conjecture and is closely tied to the **Zeeman conjecture**. A potential counterexample would be a balanced presentation of the trivial group that is genuinely "knotted" — algebraically trivial yet combinatorially irreducible.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: History — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
text: The history since 1965 is a story of accumulating candidate counterexamples on one side and ever-larger computational verifications on the other. The most famous candidates are the **Akbulut–Kirby presentations** $AK(n) = \langle x,y \mid x^n = y^{n+1},\ xyx = yxy\rangle$ (1985); $AK(2)$ was eventually shown to be AC-trivial, leaving $AK(3)$ as a long-standing open test case. Miller and Schupp introduced another influential family. Modern work is dominated by computer search — breadth-first search, genetic algorithms, and since 2021 deep reinforcement learning — which has trivialized many candidates and thereby reshaped, without resolving, expert belief.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1939** — J. H. C. Whitehead develops simple-homotopy theory and the theory of $3$-deformations of $2$-complexes, the topological backdrop for AC.
- **1965** — J. J. Andrews and M. L. Curtis pose the conjecture in "Free groups and handlebodies" (*Proc. AMS* 16).
- **1966** — Andrews and Curtis extend the framework, linking the problem to handlebody theory and $2$-complexes.
- **1975** — Connections between AC, the Zeeman conjecture, and Whitehead's deformation questions are clarified (Wright and others).
- **1979** — C. F. Miller III and P. E. Schupp produce balanced presentations conjectured to be potential counterexamples.
- **1984–1985** — S. Akbulut and R. Kirby introduce the $AK(n)$ family as candidate counterexamples arising from Dehn-surgery and $4$-manifold considerations.
- **1993** — R. G. Burns and O. Macedońska study AC-moves and balanced transformations algebraically.
- **1999** — A. D. Miasnikov applies genetic algorithms to search for AC-trivializations.
- **2006** — Miasnikov–Myasnikov and collaborators survey computational attacks; $AK(2)$ remains a focus.
- **2016** — M. R. Bridson constructs presentations with explicit (super-exponential) lower bounds on trivialization length, showing AC can be true yet astronomically hard.
- **2019** — D. Panteleev and A. Ushakov trivialize $AK(2)$ and several Miller–Schupp presentations via large-scale search.
- **2021–2024** — Reinforcement-learning approaches (including work by Shehper, Halverson, and collaborators) resolve large swaths of the Miller–Schupp family, shrinking the open candidate set.
- **Present** — $AK(3)$ and certain Miller–Schupp presentations remain open; the conjecture is unresolved in both stable and simple forms.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The conjecture bears the names of two American topologists, **James J. Andrews** and **Morton Landers Curtis**, who formulated it jointly in their 1965 *Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society* paper "Free groups and handlebodies." Both worked at the interface of geometric topology and combinatorial group theory in the postwar American school that grew out of the work of J. H. C. Whitehead and the handle-theoretic methods then transforming manifold topology.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Morton L. Curtis (1921–1989)
text: Morton Curtis is the better-documented of the two. He earned his Ph.D. and built a long career as a topologist, spending the bulk of it at **Rice University** in Houston, where he was a professor of mathematics and served for a period as department chair. Curtis's research ranged widely across algebraic and geometric topology and, later, the theory of Lie groups: he is well known to generations of students for his graduate textbook *Matrix Groups* (Springer), a clean and widely used introduction to the structure of classical Lie groups. He also worked on $4$-manifolds and group actions. Curtis was an institution-builder as much as a researcher, and his influence at Rice helped establish a strong topology presence there. The Andrews–Curtis problem emerged from his sustained interest in how the algebra of group presentations encodes the topology of $2$-complexes and handlebodies.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: James J. Andrews
text: James Andrews was Curtis's collaborator and co-author on the foundational papers. He worked in geometric topology and combinatorial group theory and is associated primarily with the joint Andrews–Curtis line of work on free groups, handlebodies, and the equivalence of presentations under elementary transformations. Beyond the AC papers, Andrews and Curtis co-authored related work on extending automorphisms and on the structure of $2$-dimensional complexes. Andrews is less of a public figure in the historical record than Curtis, but the conjecture's name preserves an equal partnership: both authors are listed on the 1965 note, and the central idea — that the obvious algebraic triviality of a balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ ought to be witnessable by a bounded vocabulary of elementary moves — was a shared insight.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: The idea and its motivation
text:
The intellectual setting was Whitehead's simple-homotopy theory. By the 1960s it was understood that two finite $2$-complexes are simple-homotopy equivalent iff one can pass between them by elementary expansions and collapses of cells. Restricting attention to deformations through dimension $3$ (so-called **$3$-deformations**) gives a more rigid notion. Andrews and Curtis recognized that, for the spines of contractible complexes, this geometric question translates exactly into a question about presentations of the trivial group and the AC-moves. Their conjecture is the assertion that no genuinely new phenomenon hides in the gap between "algebraically trivial" and "AC-reducible to trivial."

What makes the problem enduring is precisely this transparency: the statement can be explained to an undergraduate, the moves are completely explicit, and yet a single small presentation — for instance $AK(3)$ — has resisted both human ingenuity and decades of escalating computer search. The originators' legacy is thus a problem that sits at a crossroads of topology, group theory, and, increasingly, computational mathematics and machine learning, remaining a benchmark for what "elementary" reducibility can and cannot achieve.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

Work on the conjecture splits into two opposed programs: attempts to **prove** AC for ever-larger classes of presentations (chiefly via computation and via topological reformulation), and attempts to **disprove** it by exhibiting a balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ that no sequence of AC-moves trivializes. No approach has resolved the problem in either direction; the candidate-counterexample strategy has repeatedly failed in the sense that every published candidate eventually fell.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Topological reformulation (3-deformations of 2-complexes)
text: The core idea, due to Andrews and Curtis, is to translate the algebraic question into geometry: a balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ corresponds to a contractible (in the stable case, aspherical-quotient) finite $2$-complex, and AC-reducibility corresponds to $3$-deformability to a point. This reframes AC as a special case of the broader question of whether contractible $2$-complexes are collapsible after $3$-deformation, and links it to the **Zeeman conjecture** ($K \times I$ collapsible for contractible $2$-complexes $K$) and to questions in the Whitehead/Wall theory of simple homotopy. **Best result:** the reformulation is an exact equivalence and has clarified what a counterexample must look like (an "exotic" contractible complex). **Barrier:** it converts one hard problem into another equally hard one; Zeeman's conjecture is itself open, and the implication relationships among Zeeman, AC, and Poincaré-type statements are subtle and do not yield AC as a corollary of anything proved.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Candidate counterexamples (Akbulut–Kirby and Miller–Schupp families)
text: The dominant disproof strategy isolates structured families of balanced presentations of $\{1\}$ that "look knotted" and conjectures they are not AC-trivial. The **Akbulut–Kirby** presentations $AK(n)=\langle x,y\mid x^n=y^{n+1},\,xyx=yxy\rangle$ ($n\ge 2$) arose from Dehn-surgery descriptions of homotopy $4$-spheres. The **Miller–Schupp** family $\langle x,y\mid x^{-1}y^n x = y^{n+1},\, x = w\rangle$ (with $w$ a word of zero exponent sum in $x$) is a second well-studied source. **Best result:** these families correctly identify the hardest small presentations and have driven all subsequent progress. **Barrier / negative outcome for the strategy:** the candidates keep falling. $AK(2)$ was shown AC-trivial; large initial segments of the Miller–Schupp family have been trivialized by search and reinforcement learning. Thus the very presentations proposed as counterexamples have instead become evidence *for* the conjecture — though $AK(3)$ remains stubbornly open, neither trivialized nor proven non-trivializable.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Computational search (BFS, genetic, and Monte Carlo)
text: Because AC-moves generate a transformation graph, trivializing a presentation is a path-search problem. Approaches include breadth-first / iterative-deepening search over the move graph (Havas–Ramsay; Bowman–McCaul), genetic algorithms (A. D. Miasnikov, 1999), and large-scale heuristic search with length-bounded relators (Panteleev–Ushakov, 2019). **Best result:** explicit AC-trivializations of $AK(2)$ and of many Miller–Schupp presentations, plus exhaustive verification of AC-triviality for all sufficiently short balanced presentations. **Barrier:** the search space grows super-exponentially with relator length, and Bridson's lower bounds (below) show that even a *true* instance may require trivialization sequences and intermediate relator lengths far beyond any feasible search horizon. A failed search therefore can never certify non-triviality.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Reinforcement learning and modern AI search
text: Since 2021, the path-search has been recast as a Markov decision process and attacked with deep reinforcement learning (policy/value networks, AlphaZero-style self-play, and proximal-policy-optimization agents). The work of Shehper, Halverson, and collaborators (and related independent efforts) resolved substantial new portions of the Miller–Schupp family that classical search could not reach. **Best result:** trivialization of many previously open Miller–Schupp instances and improved understanding of the "energy landscape" of AC-moves. **Barrier:** the same length-explosion obstruction applies; learned agents extend the feasible horizon but do not bound it, and they cannot prove a negative.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Algebraic invariants and obstructions
text: A complementary program seeks computable invariants that are preserved by AC-moves and could certify non-triviality. Candidates have included representation-variety and group-ring (e.g. $K$-theoretic, Fox-calculus / Alexander-type) invariants, and the second homotopy module $\pi_2$. **Best result:** various necessary conditions for AC-equivalence and a sharper picture of what data the moves preserve. **Barrier — the central obstruction of the whole field:** *no invariant is known that distinguishes any balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ from the trivial one.* Many natural invariants (abelianization, Euler characteristic, Whitehead torsion in the relevant setting) vanish identically on balanced presentations of the trivial group, so they cannot separate a counterexample from the trivial presentation. This is the analogue, in this field, of a "barrier result": the absence of a separating invariant is exactly why a counterexample, if it exists, is so hard to *recognize*, and why disproof cannot proceed by invariant computation alone.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Hardness and lower-bound results
text: Bridson (2015–2016) constructed balanced presentations that *are* AC-trivial but for which any trivialization must pass through relators of enormous length, giving non-elementary (tower-type) lower bounds on trivialization cost. **Significance:** this is a genuine negative-flavored theorem — it proves that brute-force feasibility is hopeless in general and that "we searched and found nothing" is evidentially weak. **Barrier:** it does not decide AC; it shows the decision problem, whatever its truth value, is computationally severe and that intuition calibrated on small cases is unreliable.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

The literature on AC is unusual in that its most celebrated results are the *fall* of proposed counterexamples rather than partial proofs of the conjecture itself. The pattern has been: a structured family is proposed as "probably not AC-trivial," resists for years, and is then trivialized by an explicit sequence — leaving the conjecture standing but its candidate counterexamples diminished.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Akbulut–Kirby $AK(2)$: the canonical near-miss for disproof
text: The presentation $AK(2)=\langle x,y\mid x^2=y^3,\,xyx=yxy\rangle$ was for decades the most prominent suspected counterexample, originating in Akbulut and Kirby's 1985 work on potential exotic homotopy $4$-spheres. It was widely believed to be non-AC-trivial. This expectation was overturned: explicit AC-trivializations were found by large-scale computational search (notably by **Panteleev and Ushakov, 2019**, building on earlier searches), demonstrating $AK(2)$ *is* AC-trivial in the stable sense. The episode is the field's clearest cautionary tale — a candidate counterexample that survived for over thirty years was simply a hard *positive* instance. $AK(3)=\langle x,y\mid x^3=y^4,\,xyx=yxy\rangle$ remains open and is now the canonical open test case.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Miller–Schupp family: progressive trivialization
text: The Miller–Schupp presentations $\langle x,y\mid x^{-1}y^nx=y^{n+1},\,x=w\rangle$ supplied a large, parameterized testbed. Classical search trivialized many small members; **reinforcement-learning agents (Shehper, Halverson, and collaborators, 2024)** then trivialized a much larger range of $(n,w)$ that had been beyond reach, again confirming AC for those instances and reducing — but not eliminating — the set of open candidates. As with $AK(2)$, the proposed counterexamples became evidence for the conjecture.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Bridson's hardness construction (a near-miss in the other direction)
text: Martin Bridson's construction of AC-trivial presentations requiring tower-type (non-elementary) trivialization length is not an attempt to prove or disprove AC, but it sharply constrains both programs. It shows that a search-based "we found no trivialization, so it is a counterexample" inference is invalid in principle: a true instance can lie arbitrarily far beyond any search horizon. This is the closest thing the field has to a barrier theorem and is frequently cited as the reason no negative result can come from finite search alone.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Claimed proofs and disputes
text: To date there is **no widely accepted proof or disproof** of the Andrews–Curtis Conjecture, in either the stable or simple form. Occasional preprints and announcements claiming resolution have circulated, but none has gained acceptance in the geometric-group-theory community, and the conjecture is uniformly listed as open in current surveys and problem lists. Accordingly, this dossier records no established resolution. Any future claim should be assessed against two recurring failure modes documented above: (1) confusing an unverifiable *failed search* for a genuine non-triviality proof, and (2) overlooking the stable-vs-simple distinction (a proof for the stable conjecture would not settle the simple one, and vice versa). Because the relevant claims here are about the *absence* of an accepted resolution rather than the details of a specific disputed manuscript, readers are advised to consult the latest survey literature before treating any announcement as settled. Reports in this dossier of specific trivializations (e.g. $AK(2)$, Miller–Schupp members) reflect computational results that are checkable in principle by replaying the published move sequences, and are flagged as needing independent verification where their provenance is a single source.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Key Papers — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture

_The 25 most important papers. Each carries a verification flag._

Identifiers are given only where confidently known; "—" means no identifier is asserted (the work is real but its DOI/arXiv id is not reproduced here from memory). The **Verify** column is honest about confidence: "high-confidence" marks canonical, certainly-real works; "needs-verification" marks plausible real works whose bibliographic details should be checked; "ai-suggested" marks leads that should be confirmed to exist before citation.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The current active community spans three overlapping camps. **Topologists** (in the lineage of Whitehead, Metzler, and the *Two-Dimensional Homotopy* school edited by Hog-Angeloni, Metzler, and Sieradski) maintain the geometric reformulation in terms of $2$-complexes, $3$-deformations, and the Zeeman conjecture. **Computational and combinatorial group theorists** — the Miasnikov/Myasnikov circle, Panteleev, Ushakov, and adjacent workers using GAP/KBMAG-style tools and term-rewriting (e.g. Lisitsa) — push the explicit-trivialization frontier and maintain the catalogue of open candidates. **Machine-learning researchers** (Shehper, Halverson, and collaborators since roughly 2021) have recast trivialization as a reinforcement-learning problem and absorbed large parts of the Miller–Schupp family. Bridson's hardness theorems anchor all three by bounding what any of these methods can hope to certify. The shared frontier today is concrete: settle $AK(3)$, and either prove the conjecture for a structurally complete class or exhibit the first genuine counterexample.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open (active progress).** As of mid-2026 the Andrews–Curtis Conjecture is unresolved in both its **simple** form (AC', fixed generator count) and its **stable** form (with allowed stabilizations). There is no accepted proof and no accepted counterexample. What has changed over the last decade is not the truth value but the evidence base: a long parade of suspected counterexamples has been computationally trivialized, while a hardness theorem has shown that this evidence cannot, by itself, settle anything.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **The reformulation is exact.** AC is equivalent to the statement that every contractible finite $2$-complex $3$-deforms to a point; equivalently, every balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ is AC-reducible to the trivial presentation. This ties AC to the Zeeman conjecture and to simple-homotopy theory.
- **All sufficiently short balanced presentations of $\{1\}$ are AC-trivial**, verified by exhaustive search. Small cases offer no counterexample.
- **$AK(2)$ is AC-trivial.** The decades-long flagship candidate $\langle x,y\mid x^2=y^3,\,xyx=yxy\rangle$ was explicitly trivialized by large-scale search (Panteleev–Ushakov, 2019), overturning the prior expectation that it was a counterexample.
- **Large portions of the Miller–Schupp family are AC-trivial**, with reinforcement-learning agents (Shehper, Halverson, et al., 2024) extending the trivialized range well beyond classical search.
- **Hardness (Bridson).** There exist AC-trivial balanced presentations whose every trivialization passes through relators of non-elementary (tower-type) length. Consequently a finite failed search can never certify a counterexample.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional / structural)
text: No deep conditional theorems of the form "if $X$ then AC" carry the field; the principal structural fact is negative — **no invariant is known that separates any balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ from the trivial one.** Standard invariants (abelianization, Euler characteristic, relevant torsion) vanish identically on this class, which is exactly why a counterexample, if it exists, would be so hard to recognize.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A **proof** would need a uniform reduction algorithm — or a non-constructive argument — guaranteeing that *every* balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ reduces to trivial under AC-moves, robust against Bridson's length explosion (so it cannot merely be a smarter search). A **disproof** would require either (i) an explicit balanced presentation of $\{1\}$ together with an invariant or rigorous combinatorial argument proving no AC-move sequence trivializes it, or (ii) a topological construction of a contractible $2$-complex that provably does not $3$-deform to a point. Note that the simple and stable conjectures could have different answers: a stabilization-allowing proof would not settle AC', and a counterexample to AC' might still be stably trivial.

title: The Andrews–Curtis Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Resolve $AK(3)$** $\langle x,y\mid x^3=y^4,\,xyx=yxy\rangle$ — the canonical open test case — either by trivialization (further evidence for AC) or by a non-triviality obstruction (a counterexample).
2. **Find a separating invariant**, perhaps from representation/character varieties, $\pi_2$-module structure, or $K$-theoretic data, that is provably AC-invariant and non-trivial on some candidate.
3. **Topological resolution of Zeeman-type questions** that forces, or refutes, $3$-deformability for the relevant contractible complexes.
4. **AI-assisted discovery** of either very long trivializations (confirming AC on new families) or, conversely, structural patterns suggesting genuine irreducibility.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: If A^x + B^y = C^z with x,y,z > 2 and A,B,C positive integers, then A, B, and C share a common prime factor.

*Originators: Andrew Beal.*

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: History — The Beal Conjecture
text:
The Beal Conjecture asserts that the Diophantine equation
$$A^x + B^y = C^z,\qquad x,y,z>2,\ A,B,C\in\mathbb{Z}^{+},$$
has no solution in which $A$, $B$, and $C$ are pairwise coprime — equivalently, that any solution forces $A$, $B$, $C$ to share a common prime factor. It generalizes Fermat's Last Theorem (FLT): setting $x=y=z=n\ge 3$ recovers the FLT equation, and FLT (proved by Andrew Wiles, 1995) is exactly the equal-exponent, coprime case.

The conjecture grew out of computational number theory rather than from inside academia. Around 1993, **Andrew Beal**, a Dallas banker and self-taught number theorist, was experimenting with the FLT equation on computers. He observed that whenever $A^x+B^y=C^z$ held with all exponents exceeding 2, the bases shared a factor — the canonical example being $3^3 + 6^3 = 3^5$ (since $27+216=243$), where $3$ divides every base. He conjectured that the coprime case is impossible. Beal communicated the problem to several number theorists, including **R. Daniel Mauldin** of the University of North Texas, who published it for the wider community in a 1997 *Notices of the AMS* article, "A Generalization of Fermat's Last Theorem: The Beal Conjecture and Prize Problem," which also announced a cash prize Beal had endowed.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: History — The Beal Conjecture
text:
The conjecture is tied to the family of generalized Fermat equations $A^p+B^q=C^r$, organized by the inverse-exponent sum $\chi=\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r$. When $\chi>1$ (the "spherical" case) there are infinitely many coprime solutions; when $\chi=1$ (the "Euclidean" case, e.g. $(3,3,3),(2,3,6),(2,4,4)$) finitely many; and when $\chi<1$ (the "hyperbolic" case) the Darmon–Granville theorem (1995), via Faltings' theorem, gives finitely many coprime solutions for each fixed triple. Beal's condition $x,y,z>2$ forces $\chi\le 1$, with equality only at $(3,3,3)$, so the conjecture lives entirely in the hyperbolic regime plus that single Euclidean triple. A more general statement — the **Fermat–Catalan conjecture** of Darmon and Granville — predicts only finitely many coprime solutions across all triples with $\chi<1$; the ten known such solutions all involve an exponent equal to 2, and so all violate Beal's $x,y,z>2$ hypothesis, consistent with Beal.

The prize history made the problem unusually public. Beal initially offered \$5{,}000 (1997), raised it in stages, and in **2013** set it at **\$1{,}000{,}000**, administered by the American Mathematical Society, which holds the funds in trust and adjudicates submissions. The large prize drew amateur submissions in volume; none has survived refereeing.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1637** — Pierre de Fermat states FLT in the margin of his Diophantus, the historical root of the equal-exponent case.
- **1993** — Andrew Beal, experimenting computationally on FLT-type equations, formulates the coprimality conjecture and begins circulating it.
- **1994–95** — Wiles, with Taylor, completes the proof of FLT, settling the equal-exponent coprime case $x=y=z=n\ge 3$.
- **1995** — Darmon and Granville prove that $A^p+B^q=C^r$ with $\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r<1$ has finitely many coprime solutions for each fixed $(p,q,r)$, via Faltings.
- **1997** — Mauldin publishes the conjecture and Beal's prize in the *Notices of the AMS*, fixing the name "Beal Conjecture."
- **1997** — Darmon and Loïc Merel resolve $A^n+B^n=2C^n$ and related ternary equations, extending the modular method.
- **2000s** — Modular-method case studies (Bennett, Skinner, Kraus, Bruin, Poonen, Siksek, and others) resolve many individual signatures $(p,q,r)$, including infinite families such as $(n,n,2)$ and $(n,n,3)$.
- **2013** — Beal raises the prize to \$1{,}000{,}000, administered by the AMS; press coverage renews public attention.
- **2010s–2020s** — Frey-curve and level-lowering methods plus large-scale computation extend coverage of small exponent signatures; the general coprime case remains open.
- **present** — No general proof or counterexample; the conjecture stands as a flagship open problem in Diophantine number theory, with progress confined to specific exponent families.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Beal Conjecture
text:
**Daniel Andrew Beal** (born 29 November 1952, Lansing, Michigan) is an American banker, businessman, and amateur number theorist. He is the founder and chairman of Beal Bank and Beal Financial Corporation, headquartered in Texas, and one of the wealthiest self-made financiers in the United States. He is widely known outside finance for his recreational mathematics, high-stakes poker, and his interest in space launch ventures (Beal Aerospace, active 1997–2000). His mathematical work is that of a gifted autodidact rather than a credentialed academic: he has no doctorate in mathematics and pursued number theory as an avocation.

Beal's path to the conjecture ran through Fermat's Last Theorem. In the early 1990s, against the backdrop of intense public interest in FLT (Wiles announced his proof in 1993), Beal undertook his own computer experiments on the equation $A^x+B^y=C^z$. Rather than restrict to the equal-exponent FLT case, he allowed the three exponents to vary independently, subject to all exceeding 2. His central empirical observation was structural: every solution he could find had bases sharing a common prime factor. Examples such as $3^3+6^3=3^5$, $2^7+2^7=2^8$ (after rescaling), and the family generated by Mauldin's later catalogue all carry this feature. From this pattern Beal abstracted the bold claim that coprimality of $A,B,C$ is incompatible with $A^x+B^y=C^z$ once $x,y,z>2$.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Beal Conjecture
text: Beal recognized that the conjecture is a natural generalization of FLT and that, if true, it would imply FLT for exponents $\ge 3$ (the equal-exponent case with coprime bases). He also understood that the converse fails — Wiles's proof of FLT does not settle Beal, because the unequal-exponent cases require fundamentally more than the modularity of a single elliptic curve. Lacking the apparatus of modern arithmetic geometry, Beal sought out professional mathematicians to vet and disseminate the problem. The most consequential of these contacts was **R. Daniel Mauldin**, a distinguished analyst and set theorist at the University of North Texas, who recognized the problem's coherence and importance and published it, with Beal's blessing and prize, in the *Notices of the AMS* (1997). Mauldin's article is the canonical reference and the source of the name.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Beal Conjecture
text:
Beal's broader legacy in mathematics is twofold. First, he is an unusual example of an amateur whose conjecture entered the professional literature as a named, seriously studied problem — a status earned partly by the problem's intrinsic interest and partly by Mauldin's imprimatur. Second, through the **Beal Prize** (currently \$1{,}000{,}000, held and administered by the American Mathematical Society), he has institutionalized a standing incentive, on the model of earlier prize problems. Beal himself frames the prize as a way to inspire young people toward mathematics, and the AMS uses the associated funds partly to support research and outreach. Beal has stated that he formulated the conjecture independently; equivalent or closely related statements were noted around the same period by others (see the history of the generalized Fermat equation), but the modern name and the prize are firmly attached to Beal.

It should be stressed that the **modern formulation** is exactly Beal's: the coprime-bases impossibility for $x,y,z>2$. There is no divergence between a "historical root" and a "modern version" in the way some conjectures have one — what has evolved is the surrounding theory (the generalized Fermat equation, the Fermat–Catalan framework, the modular method), not the statement itself. Beal's contribution is the precise, falsifiable claim; the professional community has supplied the context in which it sits and the partial results that bound it.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Beal Conjecture
text: The Beal Conjecture is the coprime case of the generalized Fermat equation $A^x+B^y=C^z$ with $x,y,z>2$. No single technique reaches the full statement; progress is organized by exponent signature $(p,q,r)$ and by the inverse-sum $\chi=\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r$. The principal lines of attack follow.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: The modular method (Frey curves, level-lowering, modularity)
text:
The dominant tool, descended directly from the proof of FLT. To a putative coprime solution of $A^p+B^q=C^r$ one attaches a **Frey–Hellegouarch elliptic curve** whose discriminant encodes the solution. Ribet's level-lowering and the modularity of elliptic curves over $\mathbb{Q}$ (Wiles; Breuil–Conrad–Diamond–Taylor) then force the associated mod-$\ell$ Galois representation to match a modular form of very small level, which one shows cannot exist — a contradiction. This program, developed by Darmon, Merel, Bennett, Skinner, Kraus, Siksek and others, has resolved many specific signatures.

*Best results.* Equal-exponent families and many mixed cases are settled: $(n,n,n)$ is FLT; $(n,n,2)$ and $(n,n,3)$ are handled by Darmon–Merel (1997) and Bennett–Skinner-type arguments; numerous individual triples such as $(2,3,n)$ (Catalan-type, partly via Poonen–Schaefer–Stoll and Chen, Siksek, Dahmen) and small fixed exponents are known. Within the strict Beal range $x,y,z>2$, every signature with two equal exponents in the resolved families is covered.

*Barrier.* The method is essentially **one signature at a time**. Each new triple typically demands its own Frey curve, its own modularity input (often over a real quadratic or other number field, where modularity is harder), and its own elimination of residual newforms. There is no uniform Frey curve covering all $(p,q,r)$ with $\chi<1$, so the method does not, as presently understood, reach the infinitely many exponent triples that Beal requires.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Finiteness via Faltings (Darmon–Granville)
text:
For each *fixed* signature $(p,q,r)$ with $\chi<1$, the equation $A^p+B^q=C^r$ defines (after passage to a suitable cover) a curve of genus $\ge 2$; **Faltings' theorem** then gives finitely many coprime solutions. This is the Darmon–Granville theorem (1995).

*Best result.* Unconditional finiteness for every fixed hyperbolic triple, hence for Beal restricted to any bounded exponent set.

*Barrier.* Faltings is **ineffective**: it bounds the *number* of solutions but yields no computable height bound, so one cannot in general enumerate or exclude them. Worse, finiteness per triple says nothing across the infinitely many triples; it does not even bound the bases uniformly.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: The $abc$ conjecture and Diophantine inequalities
text:
The $abc$ conjecture of Masser and Oesterlé implies the Beal Conjecture **for all but finitely many** solutions, and with an explicit-enough form of $abc$ would settle it entirely. The mechanism: a coprime solution with large exponents has very smooth (high-power) terms, contradicting the radical bound $c \ll_\varepsilon \operatorname{rad}(abc)^{1+\varepsilon}$.

*Best result.* Conditional on $abc$, Beal holds up to finitely many exceptions; combined with the known finiteness per triple this is close to a conditional proof.

*Barrier.* The $abc$ conjecture is itself open. Shinichi Mochizuki's claimed proof via inter-universal Teichmüller theory remains **disputed**: it was published in *PRIMS* (2021) but is not accepted by the wider community, with a specific gap identified by Scholze and Stix (2018). No unconditional, effective $abc$ bound strong enough for Beal exists.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Descent and explicit Chabauty–Coleman
text:
For individual signatures where the relevant curve has small genus or special structure, **descent** and **Chabauty–Coleman** (and its quotient/elliptic-curve Chabauty refinements) can provably determine *all* rational points, hence all solutions. Bruin, Poonen, Schaefer, Stoll, Siksek and collaborators used these to finish signatures such as $(2,3,8)$, $(2,3,7)$ (the Klein quartic), $(2,4,5)$, and others.

*Best result.* Complete resolution of several otherwise-intractable individual cases, often where the modular method stalls on residual forms.

*Barrier.* These methods require the rank of the relevant Jacobian (or a quotient) to be strictly less than its dimension, and substantial case-specific computation. They do not scale to families with growing exponent.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Computation and verification
text:
Large-scale search has verified no coprime counterexample exists within wide bounds (historically, bases up to $1000$ and exponents up to large limits in independent searches by Norvig and others).

*Best result.* Strong empirical support and confirmation that no small counterexample exists; useful for ruling out specific residual signatures.

*Barrier.* Computation can only refute, never prove, the conjecture; the search space is infinite in both bases and exponents.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Summary of obstructions
text: The recurring obstruction is **uniformity**: every effective method is anchored to a fixed exponent signature, while Beal ranges over infinitely many. Bridging that gap appears to require either an unconditional effective $abc$, a uniform Frey-type construction, or a genuinely new idea. The ineffectivity of Faltings and the unresolved status of $abc$ together explain why a problem so close to FLT has resisted a general proof.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Genuine partial results (near-misses by signature)
text:
The substantive progress on Beal consists of complete resolutions of individual exponent signatures $(p,q,r)$ of the generalized Fermat equation $A^p+B^q=C^r$, each a near-miss in that it disposes of one slice of the infinite problem.

- **Fermat's Last Theorem (Wiles, with Taylor, 1995).** Settles the equal-exponent case $A^n+B^n=C^n$, $n\ge 3$ — the diagonal of the Beal range. This is the deepest established sub-result and the template for all that follow.
- **Darmon–Merel (1997).** Resolved $x^n+y^n=2z^n$ and the signatures $(n,n,2)$ and $(n,n,3)$ for $n\ge$ small bounds, extending the modular method to non-diagonal exponents.
- **Bennett, Skinner, Kraus, and collaborators (2000s).** A long sequence of papers eliminating mixed signatures via Frey curves and level-lowering, e.g. families $(2,4,n)$, $(2,n,4)$, and assorted $(3,3,n)$-type cases.
- **Poonen–Schaefer–Stoll (2007), Bruin, Siksek, Chen, Dahmen (2000s–2010s).** Resolved hard individual signatures including $(2,3,7)$, $(2,3,8)$, $(2,3,9)$, $(2,4,5)$, $(3,4,5)$, and others, often by elliptic-curve Chabauty or descent where the modular method left residual newforms. Note that signatures involving an exponent equal to 2 lie just *outside* the strict Beal hypothesis $x,y,z>2$ but bound the surrounding Fermat–Catalan landscape.

These collectively confirm Beal on an expanding but still finite catalogue of signatures. No method among them generalizes to all triples.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: The $abc$ route and the Mochizuki dispute
text: Because $abc$ implies Beal up to finitely many exceptions, any accepted proof of the $abc$ conjecture would be a decisive near-resolution. **Shinichi Mochizuki** announced a proof of $abc$ via inter-universal Teichmüller theory in 2012, published in *Publications of RIMS* in 2021. The claim is **disputed**: in 2018 **Peter Scholze and Jakob Stix** circulated a detailed objection ("Why $abc$ is still a conjecture"), arguing that a specific identification in the proof of a key inequality (around "Corollary 3.12") collapses a quantity that must be tracked, invalidating the argument. Mochizuki rejected the objection; the broader number-theory community has not accepted the proof. Stated neutrally: $abc$ remains, by mainstream consensus, open, and hence the $abc$-conditional path to Beal is not currently available.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Claimed direct proofs (retracted, withdrawn, or unrefereed)
text:
The Beal Prize (now \$1{,}000{,}000, administered by the AMS) has attracted a steady stream of purported elementary proofs, almost all from outside the research community. None has passed peer review. Common failure modes, documented by referees and in expository commentary, include:

- **Illegitimate modular-arithmetic reductions** that implicitly assume coprimality conclusions, or that handle only finitely many residues.
- **Errors in infinite descent** that silently require an exponent equal to 2 (where descent genuinely works) and break for $x,y,z>2$.
- **Confusing necessary with sufficient conditions**, e.g. deriving a common-factor relation that holds for *known* solutions and asserting it forbids coprime ones.

In keeping with house policy, no specific amateur preprint is endorsed or refuted here beyond noting that, as of the present, the AMS has not awarded the prize and no claimed elementary proof is accepted. Notably, an elementary proof would be surprising precisely because the nearest established result (FLT) required modularity; a short proof would imply an unexpectedly elementary route to FLT as a corollary, which the community regards as strong prior evidence against any brief argument.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Status of the "near-misses" taken together
text: The honest summary: Beal is **confirmed on infinitely many but not all** specific configurations (every fully resolved signature, plus the diagonal via FLT), is **conditionally near-settled** under $abc$, and has **no accepted general proof or counterexample**. The gap between "every fixed exponent triple" and "all exponent triples uniformly" is exactly where every attempt has stalled.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Key Papers — The Beal Conjecture
text: **Notes on reliability.** Rows 1–5, 7, 17, and 20 are canonical and certainly real; identifiers given there are high-confidence. For the modular-method and generalized-Fermat case studies (rows 6, 8–16, 18, 21–24) the *works and authors are real research in this area*, but exact titles, years, and especially DOIs vary across versions (journal vs. arXiv) and are marked "needs-verification"; identifiers are withheld ("—") rather than guessed. Row 19 (Norvig) is a well-known online computational essay on Beal counterexample search; its publication metadata is informal, hence no DOI. Row 25 is Mochizuki's published IUT paper underlying the *disputed* $abc$ claim relevant to Beal's conditional resolution; the Scholze–Stix objection (2018, unpublished note) is discussed in `attempts.md` and is not listed as a separate row because it has no formal identifier. Rows 16–17 (Tijdeman, Mihăilescu) concern Catalan's conjecture — included because Catalan ($A^x - B^y = 1$) is the companion exponential Diophantine problem and Mihăilescu's 2004 proof is a landmark of the surrounding theory. Human source-checking is required before any "needs-verification" or "ai-suggested" row is treated as established.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community on Beal is essentially the **generalized Fermat / Fermat–Catalan** community, since Beal is the coprime $x,y,z>2$ slice of that problem. The center of gravity is the modular-method school — Darmon's program and its descendants — pursued by groups around Michael Bennett (UBC), Samir Siksek and Nuno Freitas (Warwick / and collaborators), Imin Chen, Sander Dahmen, Bartosz Naskręcki, Nicolas Billerey, Luis Dieulefait, and Michael Stoll, among others. Their work proceeds signature by signature, combining Frey curves, modularity over real and imaginary quadratic fields, level-lowering, and explicit (elliptic-curve) Chabauty. A second, looser community connects Beal to the $abc$ conjecture (Masser, Oesterlé; and the disputed Mochizuki program), since an effective $abc$ would essentially settle Beal. The conjecture also draws sustained, mostly fruitless, attention from amateurs because of the seven-figure prize; the refereeing burden falls on the AMS. No single individual is presently "the" Beal expert — progress is distributed across the modular-method network, and a uniform resolution is widely expected to require an idea beyond the current toolkit.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Beal Conjecture
text: **Status: open.** There is no accepted proof and no known counterexample. The conjecture states that $A^x+B^y=C^z$ with $x,y,z>2$ and $A,B,C$ positive integers forces $\gcd(A,B,C)>1$.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **The diagonal case is closed.** For $x=y=z=n\ge 3$, coprime solutions are impossible by Fermat's Last Theorem (Wiles 1995). This is the deepest established sub-statement.
- **Finiteness per signature.** For every fixed exponent triple $(p,q,r)$ with $\tfrac1p+\tfrac1q+\tfrac1r<1$ — which includes all Beal signatures except $(3,3,3)$ — there are only finitely many coprime solutions (Darmon–Granville 1995, via Faltings). This bound is **ineffective**: it gives no way to enumerate or exclude the solutions.
- **Many individual signatures fully resolved.** A large catalogue of triples $(p,q,r)$ has been completely settled by the modular method and by descent/Chabauty: the families $(n,n,2)$, $(n,n,3)$, and numerous mixed and small-exponent cases (Darmon–Merel, Bennett–Skinner, Kraus, Bruin, Poonen–Schaefer–Stoll, Siksek, Chen, Dahmen, Freitas, and others). Within the strict Beal range these confirm the conjecture for infinitely many — but not all — exponent configurations.
- **Computation.** No coprime counterexample exists within the (large) ranges of bases and exponents that have been searched.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional)
text: - **Under the $abc$ conjecture**, Beal holds for all but finitely many solutions; with a sufficiently effective form of $abc$ it would follow entirely. The $abc$ conjecture itself is open: Mochizuki's claimed proof (published 2021) is **disputed**, with the Scholze–Stix objection (2018) unresolved by community consensus.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text:
The essential gap is **uniformity across infinitely many exponent triples**. Every effective method is tied to a single signature $(p,q,r)$; Faltings' finiteness is ineffective and non-uniform. A complete proof would need one of:

1. an **unconditional, effective $abc$** (or a Beal-tailored Diophantine inequality) strong enough to bound the smooth terms;
2. a **uniform Frey-curve / modularity construction** covering all hyperbolic signatures at once, rather than case by case; or
3. a genuinely new structural idea linking the coprimality hypothesis to a global obstruction.

A short, elementary proof is regarded as unlikely: it would imply an unexpectedly elementary route to FLT (a corollary of Beal), contrary to the depth FLT is known to require. The most plausible near-term routes are incremental — extending the modular method over number fields (asymptotic-Fermat techniques) to swallow ever-larger families of signatures — while a clean general theorem awaits progress on $abc$ or a new method.

title: The Beal Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [The abc Conjecture](../abc-conjecture/README.md) — implies Beal up to finitely many exceptions; the natural conditional route.
- [Fermat's Last Theorem context: Goldbach Conjecture](../goldbach-conjecture/README.md) — companion additive/multiplicative number-theory problem of comparable fame.
- [The Erdős–Straus Conjecture](../erdos-straus-conjecture/README.md) — another elementary-to-state Diophantine equation resistant to general proof.
- [The Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — the central conditional-results hub of analytic number theory bordering this area.
- [Odd Perfect Numbers](../odd-perfect-numbers/README.md) — a sibling "elementary statement, deep obstruction" problem in multiplicative number theory.

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture

title: The Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: The rank of the group of rational points of an elliptic curve equals the order of vanishing of its L-function at s = 1.
