*Originators: Paul Erdős, Ernst G. Straus.*

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: History — The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Origin
text:
The conjecture belongs to the modern study of *Egyptian fractions* — representations of a rational number as a sum of unit fractions $1/m$. The subject reaches back to the Rhind Mathematical Papyrus (c. 1650 BCE), whose $2/n$ table expresses fractions $2/n$ as sums of unit fractions, and to Fibonacci's greedy algorithm (1202, *Liber Abaci*), which guarantees that every rational in $(0,1)$ admits *some* finite unit-fraction expansion. The natural twentieth-century refinement asks how *few* terms a given family requires.

Around 1948, **Paul Erdős** and **Ernst G. Straus** asked whether, for the family $4/n$, three terms always suffice. The precise statement: for every integer $n \ge 2$ there exist positive integers $a,b,c$ with
$$\frac{4}{n} = \frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c}.$$
The unit fractions need not be distinct and are taken positive. (For $n=2$, $4/2 = 1/1 + 1/2 + 1/2$; for $n=3$, $4/3 = 1/1 + 1/4 + 1/12$; the interesting range is large $n$.) It is a paradigm of an "elementary but unyielding" problem: trivial to check for any single $n$, yet resistant to a uniform proof.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Reformulations
text:
Several equivalent forms organize the attack:

- **Reduction to primes.** A solution for a divisor of $n$ scales to one for $n$, so the conjecture for all $n$ follows from the conjecture for all primes $p$. All difficulty concentrates on prime denominators.
- **Reduction to residue classes.** Constructions resolve $n$ according to its residue modulo small moduli. Following Mordell, solubility holds for every $n$ outside a finite set of classes modulo $840$; the unresolved cases are essentially the primes $p \equiv 1, 121, 169, 289, 361, 529 \pmod{840}$ — the squares of $1, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23$ — a sparse union of arithmetic progressions.
- **Diophantine form.** Clearing denominators, $4/n = 1/a+1/b+1/c$ is equivalent to the cubic-surface point-count $4abc = n(ab+bc+ca)$, tying solubility to whether certain quantities are quadratic residues modulo the divisors involved.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **c. 1650 BCE** — Rhind Papyrus $2/n$ table establishes the Egyptian-fraction tradition.
- **1202** — Fibonacci's greedy algorithm (*Liber Abaci*) proves finite unit-fraction expansions always exist.
- **1948** — Paul Erdős and Ernst G. Straus pose the conjecture for $4/n$ (traditional date; it circulated in Erdős's problem lists from the late 1940s).
- **1950** — Erdős publishes on dense Egyptian-fraction questions, popularizing the $4/n$ problem in his problem-collecting network.
- **1954** — Richard Obláth verifies small cases and records partial congruence results.
- **1956–1958** — Work by Rosati, Bernstein, and others constructs solutions for broad residue classes; the modulo-$840$ framework takes shape.
- **1962** — Andrzej Schinzel and Wacław Sierpiński study the general $a/n$ family; Sierpiński states the analogous conjecture for $5/n$.
- **1964–1965** — Hagedorn, Yamamoto, and others give further constructions; the six hard square residue classes are isolated.
- **1967** — Mordell's *Diophantine Equations* records the modulo-$840$ reduction in textbook form, the standard reference for the residue obstruction.
- **1970** — R. C. Vaughan proves the exceptional set is sparse: the number of $n \le N$ for which $4/n$ might fail is $O\!\big(N \exp(-c(\log N)^{2/3})\big)$.
- **1976** — W. A. Webb and collaborators sharpen exceptional-set and density estimates.
- **1990s–2000s** — Allan Swett and others verify the conjecture computationally to roughly $n \le 10^{14}$.
- **2011** — Christian Elsholtz and Terence Tao publish a definitive analytic study, bounding the number of representations $f(n)$ on average and over arithmetic progressions, and connecting the problem to character sums and primes in special progressions.
- **2019–2024** — Continued computation pushes verification past $n \sim 10^{17}$; refined heuristics support the conjecture, which remains open for general $n$.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text: The problem stands today essentially where Mordell left it in structure: everything reduces to a sparse set of prime residue classes, none known to be universally soluble, and no argument rules out a single recalcitrant prime.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Paul Erdős (1913–1996)
text:
Paul Erdős, born 26 March 1913 in Budapest to two mathematics teachers, was one of the most prolific mathematicians in history, author or co-author of roughly 1,500 papers with some 500 collaborators — the genesis of the celebrated "Erdős number." He earned his doctorate at the University of Budapest (Pázmány Péter University) in 1934 under Lipót Fejér, with a thesis on prime numbers that already contained an elementary proof of Bertrand's postulate. As a Hungarian Jew, he left Europe in the 1930s and spent the rest of his life essentially without a fixed home, travelling between institutions and collaborators, living out of a suitcase, and devoting himself wholly to mathematics.

Erdős's deepest instinct was for *elementary* problems whose statements a schoolchild could grasp but whose resolution demanded new ideas — in number theory, combinatorics, set theory, probability, and graph theory. He pioneered the probabilistic method and, with Atle Selberg, found an elementary proof of the Prime Number Theorem (1949). Crucially for the present problem, he was a tireless *collector and disseminator of problems*, often attaching small cash prizes; many famous conjectures bear his name precisely because he posed them publicly and pressed others to solve them.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Paul Erdős (1913–1996)
text: Egyptian fractions were a lifelong interest. Erdős proved (1932) that the harmonic-like sums $\frac{1}{a_1}+\cdots+\frac{1}{a_k}$ with distinct denominators in $[n, 2n]$ are never an integer, and with Ernst Straus and others studied how economically rationals can be written as unit-fraction sums. The $4/n$ question is a natural extremal instance: the greedy algorithm shows three terms suffice for $3/n$ trivially and that $4/n$ generically needs at most a few terms, so asking whether *three* always work for the specific numerator $4$ is exactly the kind of sharp, deceptively simple question Erdős favored. He returned to unit-fraction problems repeatedly, including the related Erdős–Graham problems on Egyptian fractions.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Ernst Gabor Straus (1922–1983)
text:
Ernst Straus, born 25 February 1922 in Munich, fled Nazi Germany with his family, eventually settling in the United States. He completed his doctorate at Columbia University in 1948. Early in his career he served as an assistant to **Albert Einstein** at the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton (1944–1948), collaborating on problems in unified field theory and general relativity — a striking pedigree for someone who would also become a deep combinatorialist and number theorist. He spent most of his career at UCLA, where he was a central figure and mentor.

Straus's mathematical range was wide: he co-authored with Einstein in mathematical physics, with Erdős and others in number theory and combinatorics, and contributed to approximation theory and the theory of inequalities. His collaboration with Erdős was extensive and warm; the $4/n$ conjecture is the most famous fruit of it, formulated around 1948 as both men were thinking about the economy of unit-fraction representations. Straus's facility with explicit constructions and parametric identities complemented Erdős's instinct for asymptotics and exceptional-set arguments, and the residue-class constructions that dominate the early literature are very much in his constructive idiom.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Joint legacy
text: Neither man treated the conjecture as a private project; both folded it into Erdős's open circulation of problems, which is why it spread so quickly through Sierpiński, Schinzel, Mordell, and the wider community. The conjecture's enduring appeal — and its place in this Atlas — owes to the originators' shared aesthetic: a one-line statement, immune to brute force, that exposes a genuine gap in our understanding of how multiplicative structure (primes, quadratic residues) controls additive representations (sums of unit fractions). It remains a fitting monument to Erdős's problem-driven mathematics and to Straus's constructive number theory.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Reduction to primes and residue classes
text:
The foundational move is to pass from all $n$ to prime $n$. A solution of $4/d = 1/a+1/b+1/c$ for a divisor $d \mid n$ scales by $n/d$ to a solution for $n$, so it suffices to solve $4/p$ for primes $p$. One then classifies $p$ by its residue modulo small moduli and exhibits explicit parametric solutions class by class.

**Best result.** Mordell (codified 1967) showed $4/n$ is soluble for every $n$ except possibly those in six residue classes modulo $840 = 2^3\cdot 3\cdot 5\cdot 7$, namely $n \equiv r^2 \pmod{840}$ for $r \in \{1,11,13,17,19,23\}$. Equivalently, the only hard cases are primes $p \equiv 1,121,169,289,361,529 \pmod{840}$.

**Barrier.** The construction works precisely because, for a "good" residue, one of the unit-fraction denominators can be forced to be divisible by a small factor of $n$, reducing the equation to a soluble quadratic-residue condition. For the six square classes no such forcing exists: the relevant Legendre symbols are not constrained, so the method gives no solution and cannot, even in principle, cover them. This is the central obstruction the whole field confronts.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Explicit parametric and identity-based constructions
text:
Here one writes candidate solutions $1/a+1/b+1/c$ with $a,b,c$ polynomial (or piecewise-polynomial) in $n$ and a free parameter, then asks which $n$ are covered. "Type I" decompositions put $n$ (or a divisor) into a single denominator; "Type II" split it across two. Resolving a prime $p$ in a hard class reduces to solving a congruence such as $-N \equiv \square \pmod{d}$ for a suitable divisor $d$ of an expression in $p$.

**Best result.** These identities, assembled over many residue classes (Rosati, Bernstein, Yamamoto, Hagedorn, and others), recover Mordell's classification and resolve many subfamilies, e.g. all $p \equiv 3 \pmod 4$ are easy, and various progressions inside the hard classes can be cleared when an auxiliary quadratic residue happens to be soluble.

**Barrier.** Each identity covers $p$ only when a fixed quadratic-residue condition holds; the union of finitely many such conditions provably cannot cover an entire arithmetic progression of primes, because by quadratic reciprocity and Dirichlet the "bad" residues recur with positive density. No finite list of polynomial identities can settle the conjecture — a genuine structural limitation, not merely a gap in ingenuity.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Analytic and exceptional-set methods
text:
Rather than solve every $n$, one bounds the size of the potential failure set $E(N) = \#\{n \le N : 4/n \text{ has no 3-term representation}\}$. The tools are sieves, character sums, and estimates for primes in progressions.

**Best result.** Vaughan (1970) proved $E(N) \ll N \exp\!\big(-c(\log N)^{2/3}\big)$, so failures, if any, are exponentially rare. Elsholtz and Tao (2011) went much further, studying the representation-count function $f(n)$ (number of solutions): they obtained sharp average and higher-moment bounds, e.g. $\sum_{n\le N} f(n) \asymp N \log^3 N$ (up to constants), bounds for $f$ on prime $n$, and connections to the number of solutions of related cubic congruences. Their work essentially settles the *statistical* behavior of the problem.

**Barrier.** Exceptional-set bounds, however strong, cannot exclude a *single* exceptional prime, and the analytic estimates degrade exactly on the sparse square residue classes where the conjecture is hard. The methods detect average abundance of solutions but are powerless against an individual recalcitrant $p$ — the same wall, now in analytic guise. There is also a *parity-type* limitation: sieve methods alone cannot distinguish whether a given progression of primes always carries a solution.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Geometry of the cubic surface
text:
Clearing denominators turns $4/n=1/a+1/b+1/c$ into $4abc = n(ab+bc+ca)$, a cubic surface; solubility becomes the existence of a positive integral point. One may study it via the Hasse principle, Brauer–Manin obstructions, or descent.

**Best result.** This viewpoint explains *why* quadratic residues govern everything — the local conditions at primes dividing the discriminant control solubility — and frames the hard classes as those where local data fail to force a global point. It has clarified the structure and motivated some of Elsholtz–Tao's parametrizations.

**Barrier.** The relevant surfaces are not of a type for which we can prove the Hasse principle or compute the obstruction uniformly over a family of primes; the arithmetic of the family is exactly as hard as the original question. No Brauer–Manin or descent computation has yet cracked the square classes.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Computational verification
text:
Direct search confirms the conjecture for enormous ranges, guiding heuristics and ruling out small counterexamples.

**Best result.** Verified for all $n$ up to at least $\sim 10^{17}$ (Swett and successors), with the number of representations growing, consistent with the conjecture being true with room to spare.

**Barrier.** Computation can only confirm, never prove, an assertion over all integers; it offers strong evidence but no resolution.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Congruence-class clearing (the main partial program)
text: The most productive line, pursued from the 1950s onward by Rosati, Bernstein, Yamamoto, Hagedorn, and others, resolves the conjecture residue class by residue class modulo $840$ and its refinements. These attempts collectively reduced the open cases to the six square classes $n \equiv r^2 \pmod{840}$, $r\in\{1,11,13,17,19,23\}$, and cleared many sub-progressions inside them. They are genuine partial results, not failed proofs, but they share a fundamental near-miss character: each new identity removes some primes from the hard classes, yet by quadratic reciprocity a positive-density residual always survives, so the program cannot terminate. This is the recurring "so close, yet provably not enough" pattern of the subject.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Vaughan's exceptional-set theorem (1970)
text: R. C. Vaughan's bound $E(N) \ll N\exp(-c(\log N)^{2/3})$ is the landmark analytic near-miss: it shows that *if* counterexamples exist they are vanishingly rare. Many readers have mistaken such results for near-proofs, but they are categorically unable to exclude a single bad prime. Vaughan's work is correct and celebrated; the gap between "almost all $n$" and "all $n$" is exactly where the conjecture lives.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Elsholtz–Tao representation bounds (2011)
text: Christian Elsholtz and Terence Tao gave the most complete modern analysis, controlling the number of solutions $f(n)$ on average and in arithmetic progressions, and tying $4/n$ solubility to counts of points on related cubic congruences and to character-sum estimates. Their paper is widely regarded as the current state of the art and explicitly frames why the problem resists resolution. It is not a claimed proof of the conjecture and makes no such claim; it sharpens the statistical picture and the structural obstruction.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: The general $a/n$ analogues
text: Sierpiński's $5/n$ conjecture and the Erdős–Straus–Sierpiński program for general $a/n$ (Schinzel) were attacked with the same congruence machinery. Schinzel observed that for *fixed* numerator $a$, the $a/n$ conjecture is soluble for all sufficiently large $n$ outside finitely many residue classes — an attractive uniformity — but again the finitely many classes are exactly the hard ones, mirroring the $4/n$ obstruction. These are honest theorems with the same ceiling.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Disputed and erroneous claimed proofs
text: Because the statement is elementary, the conjecture attracts frequent claimed solutions, almost all from outside the professional literature; none has survived scrutiny. The characteristic flaw is **covering only a density-1 set of $n$ while implicitly assuming the residual square classes are also covered** — that is, conflating "all but finitely many residue classes" with "all residue classes." A second common error is an **unjustified interchange of quantifiers**, asserting that a single parametric identity works for every prime in a progression when, for the square classes, the required quadratic residue fails infinitely often. No retraction in the refereed literature is associated with the problem because no peer-reviewed full proof has ever been accepted; the appropriate neutral statement is that **the conjecture remains open**, and any document claiming a complete elementary proof should be checked specifically against the six square residue classes modulo $840$ and against the quadratic-reciprocity obstruction that defeats finite identity lists.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Computational "verifications"
text: Large searches (Allan Swett and others) confirming $4/n$ for all $n$ up to $\sim 10^{17}$ are sometimes informally cited as if they settled the matter. They do not: they are strong confirmatory evidence and useful for pruning heuristics, but verification over a finite range is logically incapable of proving a statement quantified over all integers. Their real value is in establishing that no small counterexample exists and that the number of representations grows, consistent with the conjecture holding robustly.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The Erdős–Straus conjecture has no large, dedicated "research community" in the way a Millennium Problem does; it is instead a recurring touchstone within analytic and combinatorial number theory and the study of Egyptian fractions. The originators, Erdős and Straus, embedded it in Erdős's vast problem-circulation network, which is why mid-century progress is spread across Italian (Obláth, Rosati), Polish (Sierpiński, Schinzel), Japanese (Yamamoto), and Anglo-American (Mordell, Vaughan, Webb) contributors rather than a single school. The modern frontier is small but active: **Christian Elsholtz** and **Terence Tao**'s 2011 collaboration is the reference point for current work, and Elsholtz in particular has continued to write on Egyptian-fraction problems, including expository surveys. Activity today concentrates on (i) refining heuristics for the density and number of representations, (ii) extending computational verification (now past $n\sim10^{17}$), and (iii) seeking a route past the quadratic-residue obstruction in the six square classes modulo $840$ — the genuine open core. Richard K. Guy's *Unsolved Problems in Number Theory* (problem D11) remains the standard entry point for newcomers.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
text: _Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Current status
text: The conjecture is **open** (metadata status: *active-progress*). It is known to be true for "almost all" $n$ in a strong quantitative sense and has been verified computationally for all $n$ up to at least $\sim 10^{17}$, yet no proof exists for general $n$, and no single counterexample is known. The problem is widely believed to be true.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Reduction.** It suffices to prove $4/p = 1/a+1/b+1/c$ for primes $p$; solutions for divisors scale up to all composites.
- **Residue classes (Mordell, 1967).** $4/n$ is soluble for every $n$ outside the six classes $n \equiv 1, 121, 169, 289, 361, 529 \pmod{840}$ — the squares of $1,11,13,17,19,23$. Only primes in these sparse progressions remain open.
- **Exceptional set (Vaughan, 1970).** The count of $n\le N$ that could fail satisfies $E(N) \ll N\exp\!\big(-c(\log N)^{2/3}\big)$ — exponentially few, if any.
- **Representation counts (Elsholtz–Tao, 2011).** Sharp average and higher-moment bounds for the number of solutions $f(n)$, including behavior on prime $n$ and on arithmetic progressions, plus the link to point-counts on associated cubic congruences. This is the strongest structural result to date.
- **Computation.** Verified for all $n$ to roughly $10^{17}$, with the number of representations growing.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Conditional results
text: There are no clean "assuming RH/GRH it follows" theorems that close the conjecture. The relevant conditional flavor is heuristic: standard conjectures on the distribution of primes in arithmetic progressions (and the equidistribution of associated quadratic-residue conditions) make the existence of a counterexample appear extremely improbable, since for each hard prime $p$ the density of admissible $(a,b,c)$ grows. But these heuristics, like the analytic bounds, cannot exclude an individual exceptional $p$.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A proof must handle the six square residue classes modulo $840$ *uniformly over all primes in them*. The decisive obstruction is that any finite collection of parametric identities solves a given prime only when a fixed quadratic-residue condition holds, and by quadratic reciprocity and Dirichlet's theorem the "bad" residues recur with positive density in each class — so **no finite identity list can suffice**. A resolution therefore needs a genuinely new mechanism that produces a solution for *every* prime in a square class without depending on a fixed Legendre-symbol condition. Equally, any disproof must exhibit a specific prime $p$ for which $4abc = p(ab+bc+ca)$ has no positive integral solution — something the computational and analytic evidence makes look very unlikely.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
- **Beyond finite identities.** Constructions whose validity depends on a *varying* divisor of $p\pm$(small) rather than a fixed modulus, perhaps drawing on the geometry of the cubic surface and descent/Brauer–Manin analysis of the family.
- **Analytic forcing.** Strengthening Elsholtz–Tao-type lower bounds for $f(p)$ to a guarantee $f(p)\ge 1$ for all large $p$ in the hard classes — currently out of reach because the methods average and cannot pin a single prime.
- **Character-sum input.** New estimates for the relevant character/exponential sums that would force solubility pointwise in the square classes.

No route is close to completion; the square classes modulo $840$ remain the hard, open core, exactly where Mordell's reduction left them.

title: The Erdős–Straus Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Goldbach Conjecture](../goldbach-conjecture/README.md) — another elementary additive statement over the integers, resistant despite overwhelming numerical and almost-all evidence.
- [Odd Perfect Numbers](../odd-perfect-numbers/README.md) — a classical Diophantine existence question with strong constraints but no resolution, sibling in the "elementary yet unyielding" family.
- [Singmaster Conjecture](../singmaster-conjecture/README.md) — an Erdős-flavored finiteness/representation problem from combinatorial number theory.
- [Lonely Runner Conjecture](../lonely-runner-conjecture/README.md) — another deceptively simple Diophantine statement open across residue/parameter ranges.
- [Brocard Problem](../brocard-problem/README.md) — an elementary Diophantine equation, open with only finite verification, paralleling the status of $4/n$.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Gilbreath's Conjecture

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: Iterating absolute differences of the sequence of primes always yields a leading 1 in every row.

*Originators: Norman L. Gilbreath, François Proth (1878, earlier).*

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: History — Gilbreath's Conjecture
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: The construction
text: Write the primes in order, $2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, \dots$, and form the row of absolute differences of consecutive terms: $1, 2, 2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4, 6, \dots$. Repeat on that row to obtain $1, 0, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, \dots$, then again $1, 2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, \dots$, and so on. This builds an infinite triangular *difference table* whose top row is the primes. **Gilbreath's conjecture** asserts that the first entry of every row after the first is $1$. Equivalently, defining $d_0(n) = p_{n+1}$ (the primes) and $d_{k+1}(n) = |d_k(n+1) - d_k(n)|$, the claim is $d_k(0) = 1$ for all $k \ge 1$.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: How it arose
text:
The conjecture has two independent roots. The first is **François Proth**, who in an 1878 note in the *Comptes Rendus* of the Paris Academy stated the property and offered a "proof." Proth's argument was later judged invalid, and the observation lapsed into obscurity — it was not associated with his name in the subsequent literature and was effectively lost.

The modern, named version is due to **Norman L. Gilbreath**, then a student, who around 1958 rediscovered the pattern while idly doodling on a napkin: he computed the iterated absolute differences of the primes and noticed the leading column was always $1$. The observation circulated informally; it acquired wide visibility after **Martin Gardner** described it in his *Scientific American* "Mathematical Games" column. The first substantial published numerical investigation was carried out by **Norman L. Gilbreath together with Ray Killgrove and K. E. Ralston**, whose 1959 *Mathematics of Computation* note verified the pattern far beyond hand computation.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Reformulations
text: A pivotal reframing is due to **Richard L. Hayes / and most influentially to the combinatorial argument popularized via Andrew Odlyzko**: the conjecture would follow if every difference row, once it begins, consists only of the entries $0$ and $2$ (apart from a controlled prefix), because a row of $0$s and $2$s starting with a $2$ forces its successors' leading entries to be $1$. This shifts attention from the primes themselves to the *self-similar combinatorial structure* of the difference triangle: one need not know the primes precisely, only that they are "not too irregular" mod small powers of $2$. **Odlyzko's 1993** computation made this precise: it suffices to check finitely many leading rows together with a bounded-width "persistence" condition.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1878** — François Proth states the leading-$1$ property of the prime difference table and publishes a (later-rejected) proof.
- **1958** — Norman L. Gilbreath independently rediscovers the pattern by hand and conjectures it; the problem begins to circulate.
- **1959** — Gilbreath, Killgrove, and Ralston publish an early numerical verification in *Mathematics of Computation*.
- **1960s** — Martin Gardner's *Scientific American* column popularizes the conjecture, fixing the name "Gilbreath's conjecture."
- **1980** — Paul Erdős comments that he believes the conjecture is true but estimates a proof is "about 200 years away," and offers it as the kind of problem revealing how little is understood about prime gaps.
- **1993** — Andrew M. Odlyzko reduces the conjecture to a finite, checkable condition (a "Gilbreath sufficiency"), and verifies it for primes up to $\pi(10^{13})$ — roughly the first $3.4 \times 10^{11}$ primes — confirming all rows down to depth far exceeding any plausible counterexample.
- **2000s–2010s** — Several authors (e.g. Hannah Cairns, A. Stoll, and others) study generalizations: which sequences besides the primes obey a Gilbreath-type law, showing the property is *generic* and depends only weakly on the primes' arithmetic.
- **2011** — Work by Croft / and expository treatments (Caldwell, Chris) consolidate the modern view that Gilbreath's conjecture is really a statement about sequences that are eventually "balanced mod $2$."
- **Present** — The conjecture remains **open**. It is believed true, is numerically confirmed to enormous depth, but no proof exists; the obstruction is that any proof must control the parity structure of prime gaps uniformly, which lies beyond current analytic number theory.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text: The historical arc is unusual: a problem with a near-trivial statement, overwhelming numerical support, a clean reduction to a finite check, and yet no path to an unconditional proof — a textbook example of a "true but unprovable-by-current-means" phenomenon in elementary number theory.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — Gilbreath's Conjecture
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Norman L. Gilbreath (the modern originator)
text:
Norman L. Gilbreath is the figure whose name the conjecture bears. He posed it around **1958** while a student, by an act of recreational arithmetic that has become part of the folklore of number theory: he wrote the primes in a row and began taking absolute differences, iterating downward, and noticed that the leftmost entry stayed stubbornly equal to $1$ at every level. The story — that the computation was first scribbled on a napkin — captures the conjecture's defining character: it requires no theory to state, only patience and the willingness to look.

Gilbreath was not primarily a research number theorist of the academic-monograph kind; his lasting mathematical mark is precisely this observation. He did, however, follow it up rigorously for the era: together with **Ray Killgrove** and **K. E. Ralston** he produced an early machine-assisted numerical study, published in *Mathematics of Computation* (1959), one of the first venues where digital computation was brought to bear on a pure-mathematics conjecture. (Gilbreath is also known in a quite separate domain, as the inventor of the "Gilbreath shuffle" / "Gilbreath principle" in card magic — a genuine combinatorial result about how a single riffle shuffle preserves certain cyclic structures. The shared name reflects one person's taste for hidden order in seemingly random sequences, the same taste that produced the prime conjecture.)

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Norman L. Gilbreath (the modern originator)
text: What motivated Gilbreath was not a grand program but the empiricist's instinct: confronted with the primes — the most studied yet least predictable sequence in mathematics — he asked whether *iterated differencing*, a standard tool for taming polynomial sequences, would expose any residual regularity. For a polynomial of degree $d$, the $d$-th differences are constant; the primes are nothing like a polynomial, so the persistence of a $1$ in the leading column is genuinely surprising and points to a subtle constraint on the *parity* of prime gaps.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: François Proth (the historical root)
text:
The conjecture has an earlier, long-forgotten origin in **François Proth** (1852–1879), a self-taught French farmer-mathematician from near Verdun. Despite no formal academic position, Proth made durable contributions to elementary number theory: **Proth's theorem** (a primality criterion for numbers of the form $k \cdot 2^n + 1$ with $k$ odd and $k < 2^n$) and **Proth primes** are named for him, and his work informed later primality testing. Working largely in isolation, Proth published short notes in the Paris Academy's *Comptes Rendus*.

In **1878**, Proth stated the very property now called Gilbreath's conjecture and offered a proof. The proof was subsequently found to be flawed — it did not establish the persistence of the leading $1$ — and the result was not absorbed into the mainstream literature. Because Proth died young (in 1879, at twenty-six or twenty-seven) and worked outside any institution, his statement of the problem went unnoticed for the better part of a century, which is why the conjecture is universally attached to Gilbreath rather than Proth.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: The two formulations
text: There is no substantive difference between the Proth and Gilbreath statements: both assert that the iterated absolute-difference table of the primes has a leading column of $1$s below the top row. The historical lesson is one of independent discovery and lost priority — a recurring pattern in elementary number theory, where statements simple enough to be found by anyone are often found by several people decades apart. Proth supplied the first (faulty) claim of proof; Gilbreath supplied the rediscovery, the name, and the first serious numerical confirmation, and through Gardner's popularization made it a permanent fixture among famous open problems about the primes.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Approaches — Gilbreath's Conjecture
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

The conjecture is unusual in that the dominant strategy is not to use deep properties of the primes at all, but to argue that *almost any* sequence resembling the primes mod small powers of $2$ would satisfy a Gilbreath-type law. The difficulty is converting "almost any" into "the primes, unconditionally."

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Reduction to a finite check (Odlyzko's persistence method)
text:
The most important line of attack, due to **Andrew M. Odlyzko (1993)**, observes that the difference rows quickly become composed almost entirely of $0$s and $2$s. If one can show that for some depth $k$ every entry of row $k$ within a sufficiently wide window lies in $\{0, 2\}$ and the window's leading entry is $2$, then the leading-$1$ property *propagates downward forever* — the structure is self-sustaining. Concretely, Odlyzko proved a sufficiency criterion: it is enough to verify that a certain finite prefix of the triangle, of bounded width and depth, has the required $\{0,2\}$ form. He then verified the criterion computationally for all primes up to $10^{13}$, establishing that **no counterexample can appear in any row** for primes below that bound.

- **Best result:** Unconditional verification of the leading $1$ for the difference triangle generated by all primes $\le 10^{13}$ (over $3 \times 10^{11}$ primes), to depths far beyond any conceivable counterexample.
- **Barrier:** The criterion is *sufficient but finite-range*. It certifies the pattern only up to the largest prime checked. To finish the conjecture one needs a *uniform* guarantee that the $\{0,2\}$ structure, once established, never breaks for arbitrarily large primes — and that requires controlling prime gaps mod $2$ uniformly, which the method does not provide.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: The "$0$s and $2$s" / parity-of-gaps heuristic
text:
The core phenomenon is that, after the first row, prime gaps are all even (every prime past $2$ is odd, so consecutive gaps are even), and the differences of even numbers stay even; the residual content is whether they are $\equiv 0$ or $\equiv 2 \pmod 4$, and so on down the $2$-adic tower. The heuristic models the gaps' low-order bits as effectively random and shows that a random $\pm$ sequence of this type obeys the Gilbreath law with probability $1$.

- **Best result:** Rigorous theorems for *random* and for *structured model* sequences (see below) confirming the law holds generically.
- **Barrier:** The primes are not random. Making the heuristic rigorous would require independence/equidistribution statements about prime gaps modulo powers of $2$ that are out of reach. This is a form of the **parity barrier**: sieve and elementary methods are notoriously blind to the parity (mod $2$) structure that the conjecture lives on, so the very features the conjecture depends on are those current sieve theory cannot control.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Generalization to Gilbreath-type sequences
text:
A productive strategy (pursued by **Hannah Cairns**, **A. Stoll**, **Martin Gardner**'s commentators, and others) abandons the primes and asks: *for which sequences* does iterated absolute differencing yield a leading column of $1$s? The answer is that the property is **generic** — it holds for any sequence that begins with an odd number and whose terms are sufficiently "balanced" in their low-order binary digits, regardless of the deeper arithmetic. Cairns (2008) and related work give clean sufficient conditions: roughly, if the gaps stay bounded and equidistribute mod $2$, the law holds.

- **Best result:** Theorems characterizing broad classes of sequences satisfying the conjecture, showing the primes are merely one instance of a robust combinatorial phenomenon.
- **Barrier:** These theorems require hypotheses (boundedness or equidistribution of gaps) that, for the primes specifically, would themselves be theorems comparable in depth to what is being attempted. The generic result does not transfer to the primes without independent input.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Direct analytic / additive number theory
text:
A frontal assault would try to prove that prime gaps satisfy the needed mod-$2^k$ equidistribution directly, via the circle method, sieves, or results in the spirit of **Zhang–Maynard–Tao** bounded-gaps technology.

- **Best result:** Modern gap theory (bounded gaps, Maynard's sieve) gives strong information about *small* gaps but not about the *fine parity structure* of *all* gaps simultaneously, which is what Gilbreath needs.
- **Barrier:** Again the parity barrier, plus the fact that the conjecture demands control of *every* row of the triangle uniformly — an infinitude of conditions — rather than a single asymptotic. No analytic input currently bridges this gap.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Computational certification
text:
Beyond Odlyzko, computation serves to (i) extend the verified range and (ii) test whether the $\{0,2\}$ window width grows or stabilizes, which would bear on whether a uniform argument is plausible. Empirically the required window stays narrow, strong evidence that the law is robust.

- **Barrier:** Computation alone can never close a statement quantified over all primes; it can only raise the depth of certainty and guide the search for a uniform mechanism.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Attempts — Gilbreath's Conjecture
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Proth's 1878 "proof" (disputed / rejected)
text: The earliest claimed proof is **François Proth's**, published in the Paris Academy's *Comptes Rendus* in **1878**. Proth asserted both the statement and a demonstration. The argument is now regarded as **invalid**: it did not establish that the leading $1$ persists at every depth, but rather assumed a regularity in the difference rows that itself requires proof. Because Proth died young and worked outside academia, the error was never the subject of a famous public correction — the claim simply did not enter the literature, and the problem was rediscovered eighty years later. The neutral assessment in modern surveys is that Proth's proof "is generally considered to be incorrect," with the gap being exactly the propagation step that remains open today.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Gilbreath–Killgrove–Ralston numerical study (1959)
text: Not a proof attempt but a foundational **near-confirmation**: Gilbreath, together with **Ray Killgrove** and **K. E. Ralston**, carried out one of the first computer-assisted verifications, reported in *Mathematics of Computation* (1959). It checked the leading-$1$ property far beyond hand range and established the empirical credibility that motivated all later work. Its limitation is intrinsic: a finite check cannot decide an infinitary statement.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Odlyzko's reduction and large-scale verification (1993)
text: The most significant genuine advance. **Andrew Odlyzko** did not claim a proof of the full conjecture; rather, he proved a *sufficiency theorem* (a finite, bounded-width condition that, if met up to some height, would imply the law for all primes below the corresponding bound) and verified it for primes up to $10^{13}$. This is the canonical "near-miss": it reduces the problem to a clean structural condition and confirms it on an astronomically large range, yet leaves the genuinely infinite part — uniform persistence for arbitrarily large primes — untouched. No dispute attaches to Odlyzko's work; it is correct and is the standard reference for "what is rigorously known."

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Generic-sequence theorems (2000s)
text: Several authors, notably **Hannah Cairns (2008)** and **A. Stoll**, proved that Gilbreath-type laws hold for broad classes of sequences satisfying mild balance/equidistribution hypotheses on their gaps. These are correct theorems and genuine partial progress on the *phenomenon*, but they do not resolve the conjecture for the primes because verifying the hypotheses for the primes specifically is itself beyond current methods. They are best read as evidence that the conjecture is "true for structural reasons," not as a route to the primes.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Erdős's assessment
text: Although not an attempt, **Paul Erdős's** widely quoted judgment — that the conjecture is surely true but that a proof is "perhaps 200 years away" — functions as a standing warning. Erdős's point was that the conjecture sits squarely on the parity structure of prime gaps, the precise terrain where elementary and sieve methods fail, so the apparent simplicity of the statement is deceptive. No serious, sustained claimed proof of the full conjecture by a credentialed mathematician has appeared since; the literature is one of reductions, verifications, and generalizations rather than completed proofs.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Status of claimed proofs
text: There is **no accepted proof**. Occasional informal or amateur "proofs" circulate (as for many famous elementary conjectures), but none has survived scrutiny, and none is published in a refereed venue of standing. The honest summary: a single rejected historical proof (Proth, 1878), a powerful but finite-range reduction (Odlyzko, 1993), and a family of generic theorems that illuminate but do not close the problem.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Key Papers — Gilbreath's Conjecture
text:
_The 25 most important papers. Each carries a verification flag._

Gilbreath's conjecture has a relatively small dedicated literature: a founding note, a foundational computational reduction, a handful of generic-sequence theorems, and substantial coverage in survey/expository works on prime gaps and famous unsolved problems. Because much of the canonical material lives in surveys, encyclopedias, and recreational-mathematics venues, several entries below are books, columns, or encyclopedia articles rather than journal research papers. Identifiers are given only where confidence is high; otherwise "—".

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture

text: **Note on shortfall and reliability.** Gilbreath's conjecture has very few dedicated, refereed research papers; its bibliography is dominated by one breakthrough computational paper (Odlyzko, 1993), the original 1959 note, a small number of generic-sequence results (Cairns and others), and recurring treatment in standard surveys (Guy's *Unsolved Problems in Number Theory*; Ribenboim; MathWorld; the OEIS). Rows flagged **ai-suggested** are plausible categories of work whose exact titles, authors, or existence I could not confirm from memory; they should be treated as leads, not citations, and verified against MathSciNet/zbMATH before use. The single identifier given (Odlyzko's *Mathematics of Computation* paper) is the one I have meaningful confidence in, but the precise DOI string is flagged **needs-verification**. I have deliberately left identifiers as "—" rather than fabricate them.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active research community around Gilbreath's conjecture is small and intermittent. There is no ongoing "program" attacking it directly; instead, progress arrives sporadically from three adjacent communities. **Computational number theorists** in the tradition of Odlyzko periodically extend verification depth and probe whether the $\{0,2\}$-window width stays bounded. **Combinatorialists** (in the lineage of Cairns and Stoll) study difference-triangle dynamics and generic-sequence conditions, treating the primes as one instance of a robust self-similar structure. **Analytic number theorists** working on prime gaps (post Zhang–Maynard–Tao) supply the deep tools that a true resolution would need, but the conjecture's reliance on the parity of gaps places it on the far side of the parity barrier, so it is rarely a primary target. Much of the living knowledge resides in reference works and databases — Guy's *Unsolved Problems*, MathWorld, Caldwell's Prime Pages, and the OEIS difference-triangle entries — rather than in a stream of dedicated papers. The consensus is that the conjecture is true; the open question is whether any current method can prove the uniform persistence it demands.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — Gilbreath's Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open.** The conjecture is believed true, is supported by overwhelming numerical evidence, and admits a clean structural reduction — yet no unconditional proof exists, and none appears within reach of current methods.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Massive verification.** Andrew Odlyzko (1993) verified the leading-$1$ property for the difference triangle generated by all primes up to $10^{13}$ — over $3 \times 10^{11}$ primes — to depths vastly exceeding any plausible counterexample. No exception has ever been found.
- **A finite sufficiency criterion.** Odlyzko showed that the conjecture (up to a given prime bound) reduces to checking a *bounded-width, bounded-depth* condition on the difference triangle: once a row consists of $0$s and $2$s within a window and starts with $2$, the leading-$1$ property is self-propagating. This converts an infinite statement into a finite check on each range — but not across all ranges at once.
- **Generic-sequence theorems.** Cairns (2008), Stoll, and others proved that Gilbreath-type laws hold for wide classes of sequences whose gaps are bounded and equidistributed mod small powers of $2$. These establish that the phenomenon is *structural*, not special to the primes.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional / heuristic)
text:
- Under natural randomness models for the low-order binary digits of prime gaps, the Gilbreath law holds with probability $1$. The conjecture is therefore "true on heuristic grounds" with very high confidence.
- No nontrivial *conditional* theorem (e.g., "assume the Riemann Hypothesis, then Gilbreath follows") is known to close the problem; even strong hypotheses about prime gaps are not known to imply the required uniform mod-$2^k$ control.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A proof must establish that the $\{0,2\}$ structure of the difference rows, once it appears, **never breaks for arbitrarily large primes** — i.e., a *uniform* statement about the parity (mod $2$, then mod $4$, $8$, …) of prime gaps, holding for the entire infinite triangle simultaneously. This is precisely the territory of the **parity barrier**: sieve and elementary methods are structurally blind to the mod-$2$ information the conjecture depends on. Bridging that gap would likely demand genuinely new input on the fine arithmetic of prime gaps, beyond even the post–Zhang–Maynard–Tao bounded-gaps technology, which controls small gaps but not the parity structure of all gaps at once.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Strengthen the reduction to a uniform mechanism.** Show that Odlyzko's finite window has *provably bounded width for all heights*, turning the per-range check into a single uniform argument. The empirical stability of the window is encouraging but unproven.
2. **Transfer generic-sequence results to the primes.** Prove the equidistribution-of-gaps hypotheses used by Cairns-type theorems *for the primes specifically* — itself a problem of comparable depth.
3. **New analytic control of gap parity.** Develop methods that see past the parity barrier for prime gaps, a long-shot but the only route that addresses the true obstruction.

The realistic assessment, echoing Erdős, is that the conjecture is essentially certainly true but that a proof is far off; the gap is not a missing trick but a missing theory of prime-gap parity.

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Twin Prime Conjecture](../twin-prime-conjecture/README.md) — another deep statement about the fine structure of prime gaps.
- [Polignac's Conjecture](../polignac-conjecture/README.md) — every even gap occurs infinitely often; close in spirit to the gap-parity issues here.
- [Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture](../hardy-littlewood-k-tuple/README.md) — the framework predicting prime-gap statistics that underlies the heuristics for Gilbreath.
- [Legendre's Conjecture](../legendre-conjecture/README.md) — elementary-looking prime-distribution claim that is similarly resistant.
- [Goldbach's Conjecture](../goldbach-conjecture/README.md) — canonical example of an elementary additive prime statement blocked by the parity barrier.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture

*Originators: Christian Goldbach, Leonhard Euler (strong form).*

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: History — Goldbach's Conjecture
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Origin
text: The conjecture was born in correspondence. On 7 June 1742, the Prussian-born mathematician and diplomat Christian Goldbach wrote from Moscow to Leonhard Euler, then in Berlin, proposing — in a marginal afterthought to a letter chiefly about other matters — that **every integer greater than 2 can be written as a sum of three primes**. Goldbach worked with the convention then current that $1$ is prime, which is essential to reading his original statements correctly. In his reply of 30 June 1742, Euler reformulated and sharpened the idea, stating what we now call the **strong (binary) Goldbach conjecture**: every even integer greater than 2 is a sum of two primes. Euler remarked that he regarded this as "a wholly certain theorem" ("ein ganz gewisses Theorema") despite being unable to prove it.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Formulations
text:
Three statements circulate, and the relations among them matter:

- **Strong (binary) Goldbach:** every even $n > 2$ equals $p + q$ with $p,q$ prime. This is the modern headline statement and the one in this dossier's metadata.
- **Weak (ternary, odd) Goldbach:** every odd $n > 5$ is a sum of three primes.
- **Goldbach's original "every integer $> 2$ is a sum of three primes":** under the old convention $1\in\mathbb{P}$, this is equivalent to the modern strong form for evens (write $2k = (2k-1)+1$ etc.) and is what historically motivated both.

The strong form **implies** the weak form: if every even number is $p+q$, then any odd $n>5$ is $3+(n-3)$ with $n-3$ even $>2$. The weak form does not imply the strong. Heuristically, the Hardy–Littlewood circle-method asymptotic predicts the number $r(n)$ of representations of even $n$ grows like $r(n)\sim 2\,\Pi_2 \prod_{p\mid n,\,p>2}\frac{p-1}{p-2}\cdot\frac{n}{(\log n)^2}$, so representations become abundant — the conjecture is "true with enormous room to spare," yet no proof exists.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1742** — Goldbach's letter to Euler (7 June); Euler's reply (30 June) sharpens it to the binary even statement.
- **1855** — Desboves verifies the conjecture numerically up to $10^4$; later hand computations push the bound further.
- **1900** — Hilbert lists Goldbach (with twin primes and others) under his **8th problem** at the ICM, anchoring it in the modern research program.
- **1912** — Landau, at the ICM in Cambridge, calls Goldbach one of the problems "unattackable in the present state of science."
- **1920** — Viggo Brun introduces his **sieve**, the first method to give nontrivial upper bounds on Goldbach-type representations and "almost prime" results.
- **1923** — Hardy and Littlewood, using the **circle method** under GRH, prove a conditional asymptotic for the ternary problem and conjecture the quantitative form of the binary problem.
- **1930** — Lev Schnirelmann proves every integer $>1$ is a sum of a bounded number of primes (the **Schnirelmann constant**), the first unconditional density attack.
- **1937** — **Vinogradov** removes the GRH hypothesis: every sufficiently large odd integer is a sum of three primes. Borozdkin (1956) and later work make the threshold explicit.
- **1938** — Estermann, and independently others, show **almost all** even integers satisfy Goldbach (exceptional set of density zero).
- **1947** — Rényi proves a "$1+K$" theorem: every large even number is $p + (\text{almost prime})$.
- **1966** — **Chen Jingrun** announces the celebrated **"$1+2$"** result; full proof published 1973: every sufficiently large even integer is $p + P_2$, with $P_2$ having at most two prime factors. This remains the strongest approximation to the binary conjecture.
- **1975** — Montgomery and Vaughan give explicit bounds on the exceptional set: the count of even $n\le X$ failing Goldbach is $O(X^{1-\delta})$.
- **1995** — Ramaré proves every even integer is a sum of at most **6 primes**, sharpening Schnirelmann's constant.
- **2002** — Liu and Wang, and others, lower the explicit threshold for the ternary problem.
- **2012–2013** — **Harald Helfgott** proves the **ternary (weak) Goldbach conjecture unconditionally and completely**, for all odd $n>5$, combining refined circle-method bounds with massive verified computation (with David Platt).
- **2013–present** — **Tomás Oliveira e Silva** et al. verify the strong conjecture computationally up to $4\times10^{18}$; the binary conjecture remains **open**.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — Goldbach's Conjecture
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Christian Goldbach (1690–1764)
text: Christian Goldbach was born on 18 March 1690 in Königsberg, Prussia (now Kaliningrad) — the same city whose seven bridges would later inspire Euler's founding of graph theory. Trained in law and medicine, Goldbach was a polymath and traveler rather than a professional academic in the modern sense; in the 1710s he toured Europe and met leading mathematicians including Leibniz, Nicholas Bernoulli, and Abraham de Moivre, exchanges that drew him into mathematics. In 1725 he became a founding member and professor of mathematics at the newly established Imperial Academy of Sciences in St. Petersburg, where he also served as recording secretary. From 1728 he tutored Tsar Peter II in Moscow, and he later moved into the Russian foreign ministry, rising to privy councillor and a senior diplomatic post — so much of his mathematical life unfolded as learned correspondence alongside an official career.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Christian Goldbach (1690–1764)
text:
Goldbach's mathematical output was eclectic rather than systematic: he worked on infinite series, the theory of equations, and number-theoretic curiosities (the "Goldbach–Euler theorem" on sums of reciprocals of perfect powers bears joint witness to the partnership, and "Goldbach's theorem" on Fermat numbers being pairwise coprime is also his). His enduring fame rests not on a body of theorems but on a single conjectural sentence. That is itself characteristic of the man: a gifted, well-connected amateur with a sharp eye for patterns in the integers, who posed the right question without the apparatus to answer it. He corresponded with Euler for over thirty-five years; the 7 June 1742 letter that contains the conjecture is one fragment of that vast exchange. Goldbach died in Moscow on 20 November 1764.

The **historical root** differs subtly from the **modern statement**. Goldbach actually wrote that every integer greater than 2 is a sum of three primes, working under the then-standard convention that $1$ counts as prime. Read literally with $1\notin\mathbb{P}$, his sentence is false; read with his own convention, it is equivalent to the even-number binary form. So the conjecture as schoolchildren now learn it — "every even number $>2$ is $p+q$" — is genuinely a joint Goldbach–Euler creation, with Euler supplying the clean, modern phrasing.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Leonhard Euler (1707–1783) — author of the strong form
text:
Euler needs little introduction: the most prolific mathematician of the eighteenth century, central to analysis, number theory, mechanics, and much else, and Goldbach's principal correspondent. Born in Basel and trained under Johann Bernoulli, Euler spent formative years at the St. Petersburg Academy (where Goldbach helped recruit him) and later at Berlin. His number-theoretic instincts were extraordinary — he proved Fermat's little theorem, studied the distribution of primes via the Euler product for $\zeta(s)$, and reanimated the study of perfect numbers and quadratic reciprocity's precursors.

When Goldbach floated his three-primes observation, Euler immediately recognized the cleaner equivalent and replied that the proposition "every even integer is a sum of two primes" followed from it and was, in his judgment, certainly true though he could not prove it. Euler's reformulation is why the *strong (binary)* conjecture is the canonical version, and metadata here credits him with the "strong form."

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Legacy
text: Neither man could attack the problem with the tools of their day; the conjecture's power lies in how it crystallized a hard truth about **additive** structure of the multiplicatively defined primes. It became a touchstone for Hilbert's 8th problem, for Hardy–Littlewood's circle method, for Brun's sieve, and ultimately for Vinogradov's and Helfgott's resolutions of the ternary case — a single sentence that seeded a century and a half of additive number theory.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Approaches — Goldbach's Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: The Circle Method (Hardy–Littlewood–Vinogradov)
text:
The dominant analytic tool. Write the number of representations of $n$ as a sum of $k$ primes as a Fourier integral $r_k(n)=\int_0^1 S(\alpha)^k e(-n\alpha)\,d\alpha$, where $S(\alpha)=\sum_{p\le n} e(p\alpha)$ is an exponential sum over primes. One partitions $[0,1]$ into **major arcs** (near rationals with small denominator), where $S(\alpha)$ is estimated via the prime-counting machinery / Siegel–Walfisz and contributes the expected singular series, and **minor arcs**, where one needs strong cancellation. The method delivers the asymptotic for $r_3(n)$ and hence the **ternary** Goldbach problem.

- **Best result:** Vinogradov (1937) proved every sufficiently large odd $n$ is a sum of three primes unconditionally; Helfgott (2013) closed the remaining gap to *all* odd $n>5$, completing the weak conjecture, by sharpening minor-arc bounds and certifying the major arcs with verified computation (Platt).
- **Barrier for the binary problem:** for $k=2$ the integral is dominated by the minor arcs, where the available cancellation in $S(\alpha)^2$ is insufficient. There is no known way to control the binary minor-arc integral; the method gives the *average* (almost-all) result but not every even $n$. This is the central obstruction.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Sieve Methods (Brun, Selberg, and the parity problem)
text:
Sieves count integers (or prime pairs) by inclusion–exclusion with smoothed weights. Brun's sieve (1920) first produced finite upper bounds and "almost-prime" theorems; the Selberg sieve and the linear sieve of Rosser–Iwaniec gave the sharpest tools, culminating in Chen's theorem.

- **Best result:** **Chen's theorem (1973):** every sufficiently large even integer is $p + P_2$, where $P_2$ has at most two prime factors. This is the closest unconditional approximation to "$p+p$".
- **Barrier — the parity problem (Selberg).** Pure sieve methods cannot distinguish numbers with an even number of prime factors from those with an odd number. A sieve that could prove $n=p_1+p_2$ would have to break parity, and Selberg's parity obstruction shows classical sieves alone cannot reach exactly two prime factors on both summands. Chen's $1+2$ sits right at this wall; pushing to $1+1$ requires genuinely new input beyond sieve theory.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Schnirelmann Density / Additive Bases
text:
Schnirelmann (1930) showed the set of primes (plus 0,1) has positive Schnirelmann density after adding, so it forms an additive basis of finite order: every integer $>1$ is a sum of at most $C$ primes.

- **Best result:** Schnirelmann's constant has been driven down to **at most 6 primes** for all even integers (Ramaré, 1995), and follows from Helfgott's ternary theorem that every even number is a sum of at most 4 primes; every odd from 3. (The strong conjecture asserts the answer is 2 for evens.)
- **Barrier:** density arguments are inherently lossy — they bound the *number* of summands, not their structure, and cannot by themselves reach the sharp value 2.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Exceptional-Set / Almost-All Estimates
text:
Rather than every even number, bound the count $E(X)=\#\{n\le X:\ n\ \text{even},\ n\neq p+q\}$.

- **Best result:** Montgomery–Vaughan (1975) proved $E(X)=O(X^{1-\delta})$ for some explicit $\delta>0$; subsequent work (Pintz and others) has improved the savings, with $\delta$ now around $0.7$ in the literature. Thus the conjecture holds for **almost all** even integers, with a power-saving exceptional set.
- **Barrier:** improving $E(X)$ to $0$ (i.e. to finitely many exceptions, then none) appears to require the same minor-arc control the binary circle method lacks; an unconditional $E(X)=O(1)$ would essentially be the conjecture.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Conditional Approaches (GRH and beyond)
text: Under the **Generalized Riemann Hypothesis** the major-arc estimates sharpen dramatically. GRH yields the ternary theorem for all $n$ cheaply (this was the original Hardy–Littlewood route) and improves exceptional-set bounds for the binary problem, but **does not** by itself prove binary Goldbach — the minor-arc obstruction persists even under GRH.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Computational Verification
text:
Direct verification certifies the conjecture up to a height, supplying the "small cases" that analytic theorems leave out (crucial to Helfgott's proof) and testing heuristics.

- **Best result:** Oliveira e Silva, Herzog, and Pardi verified the strong conjecture for all even $n\le 4\times10^{18}$.
- **Barrier:** computation can never settle an asymptotic statement; it complements, not replaces, proof.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Attempts — Goldbach's Conjecture
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Landmark partial results (genuine progress)
text:
- **Brun's almost-prime theorem (1920).** Viggo Brun's new sieve produced the first finite bound: every large even number is a sum of two numbers each with at most 9 prime factors ("$9+9$"). A near-miss in spirit — the structure was right, the prime-factor counts too large — but it opened the entire sieve-theoretic line.
- **Schnirelmann (1930).** Showed every integer $>1$ is a sum of a bounded number of primes (originally a large constant, since reduced to $\le 6$ for evens by Ramaré, 1995). The first *unconditional* general additive-basis result.
- **Vinogradov (1937).** Every sufficiently large odd integer is a sum of three primes — the ternary problem solved for large $n$, unconditionally. The "sufficiently large" threshold was astronomically large (Borozdkin's explicit bound was around $e^{e^{16.038}}\approx 3^{3^{15}}$), leaving a vast unverifiable gap for decades.
- **Chen Jingrun (1966 announcement; 1973 full proof).** Every sufficiently large even integer is $p + P_2$. This "$1+2$" theorem is the strongest approximation to the binary conjecture and is regarded as one of the great sieve achievements; it sits exactly at the parity barrier.
- **Helfgott (2013).** Proved the **weak (ternary) Goldbach conjecture in full** for all odd $n>5$, closing Vinogradov's gap by driving down the threshold to about $10^{27}$ and verifying below it computationally with David Platt. The two-paper preprint (major and minor arcs) and accompanying book manuscript have been widely accepted by the community as a complete proof; as of this writing the unified monograph has been long in formal journal review, a status worth noting honestly, though the result is treated as established.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Claimed proofs of the strong conjecture (disputed / unverified)
text:
The binary conjecture's fame attracts frequent claimed proofs, none accepted:

- **Recurring arXiv/preprint claims.** Over the years numerous preprints have announced elementary or sieve-based proofs of binary Goldbach. The standard objection is uniform: any *elementary* sieve argument runs into **Selberg's parity problem**, which provably blocks distinguishing the "two primes" case from "two almost-primes" without auxiliary input. A claimed elementary proof that does not explicitly defeat parity is, on those grounds alone, presumed flawed by experts. Because none of these has survived peer scrutiny, this dossier names no specific individual claim as credible.
- **Probabilistic/heuristic "proofs."** Some attempts invoke the Cramér / Hardy–Littlewood probabilistic model of primes to argue Goldbach "must" hold. These establish that the conjecture is overwhelmingly *plausible* (the expected number of representations of $n$ grows like $\sim n/(\log n)^2$), but heuristics are not proofs — the model is not a theorem about the actual primes, and the objection is simply that no rigorous deduction has been supplied.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Famous near-miss framing
text: The gap between what is proved and the conjecture is unusually narrow yet stubborn. Three facts illustrate it: (i) the conjecture holds for **almost all** even integers with a power-saving exceptional set (Montgomery–Vaughan); (ii) every large even number is $p+P_2$ (Chen); (iii) it is verified to $4\times10^{18}$. Each is "one step" from $p+p$, but each step is blocked by the same minor-arc / parity obstruction. The honest summary: the strong conjecture has resisted every method that resolved its weaker cousins.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The current active community spans analytic number theory and computation. Helfgott (Göttingen/CNRS) and David Platt (Bristol) drove the ternary resolution and continue to refine explicit prime-counting and verified-computation tools that feed Goldbach-type problems. Olivier Ramaré (CNRS) remains central to explicit Schnirelmann-constant and sieve work, and Tomás Oliveira e Silva (Aveiro) leads the large-scale empirical verification ($4\times10^{18}$). On the binary frontier, the relevant expertise overlaps heavily with the school working on bounded gaps between primes and on improving exceptional-set savings (figures such as János Pintz, and the circle-of-ideas around Yitang Zhang, James Maynard, and Terence Tao on additive prime problems). The honest consensus is that no one currently has a credible route to the *binary* conjecture; effort concentrates on sharpening the partial results (exceptional set, explicit constants) rather than on a frontal proof.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — Goldbach's Conjecture
text: _Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Current status: OPEN (strong/binary form)
text: The **strong (binary) Goldbach conjecture** — every even integer $n>2$ is a sum of two primes — remains **unproven**. Its sibling, the **weak (ternary) conjecture**, is settled: every odd $n>5$ is a sum of three primes (Helfgott, 2013, building on Vinogradov 1937). The binary form is strictly harder and does **not** follow from the ternary result.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Almost all even numbers** satisfy Goldbach: the exceptional set $E(X)=\#\{n\le X \text{ even}: n\neq p+q\}$ satisfies $E(X)=O(X^{1-\delta})$ with an explicit power saving (Montgomery–Vaughan 1975; later $\delta\approx0.7$ via Pintz and others).
- **Chen's theorem (1973):** every sufficiently large even $n$ is $p+P_2$, where $P_2$ has at most two prime factors. This is the best structural approximation to $p+p$.
- **Bounded number of primes:** every even integer is a sum of at most **4 primes** (a corollary of Helfgott's ternary theorem; Ramaré's earlier bound was $\le 6$).
- **Computational verification:** the conjecture holds for all even $n\le 4\times10^{18}$ (Oliveira e Silva–Herzog–Pardi).
- **Heuristic support:** the Hardy–Littlewood asymptotic predicts the representation count $r(n)\sim 2\Pi_2\big(\prod_{p\mid n,\,p>2}\tfrac{p-1}{p-2}\big)\tfrac{n}{(\log n)^2}$, so representations are abundant and grow — the conjecture is expected to hold with overwhelming margin.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: What is known (conditional)
text: Under the **Generalized Riemann Hypothesis**, major-arc estimates sharpen and exceptional-set bounds improve, and the ternary theorem becomes elementary for all $n$ — but GRH **does not** yield the binary conjecture. No standard hypothesis (GRH, GLH, even pair-correlation assumptions) is currently known to imply $p+p$ for every even $n$.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: The crux is the **binary minor-arc problem**. In the circle method, $r_2(n)=\int_0^1 S(\alpha)^2 e(-n\alpha)\,d\alpha$ is dominated by the minor arcs, where the available square-mean cancellation of $S(\alpha)=\sum_{p\le n}e(p\alpha)$ is too weak to guarantee a positive main term for *every* $n$ (it suffices only on average). A proof would need either (i) a genuinely new way to control binary exponential sums over primes pointwise in $n$, or (ii) a sieve that defeats **Selberg's parity barrier** — classical sieves provably cannot separate "two primes" from "two almost-primes," which is exactly why Chen stalls at $1+2$. Closing $E(X)$ from a power-saving set to the empty set is, in effect, equivalent to the conjecture itself.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text: None is currently credible for the binary form. The realistic near-term targets are: pushing the exceptional-set exponent $\delta\to1$; lowering explicit constants; transferring ideas from the bounded-gaps revolution (Zhang/Maynard/Tao) and from additive-combinatorics transference principles to additive prime problems. A resolution likely awaits a structural insight beyond the circle method and sieve theory rather than incremental sharpening.

title: Goldbach's Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Twin Prime Conjecture](../twin-prime-conjecture/README.md) — the multiplicative-additive dual; shares the parity barrier and Hardy–Littlewood heuristics.
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — its generalization controls the major arcs and exceptional-set bounds.
- [Polignac Conjecture](../polignac-conjecture/README.md) — every even number as a prime gap; same circle of sieve methods.
- [Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture](../hardy-littlewood-k-tuple/README.md) — the general quantitative framework predicting Goldbach's singular series.
- [Legendre Conjecture](../legendre-conjecture/README.md) — a sibling additive-distributional question about primes in short intervals.

heading: Plain statement
text: Consecutive composite numbers can be assigned distinct prime divisors, one dividing each.
