*Originators: Edward Nelson, Hugo Hadwiger.*

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: History — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Hadwiger–Nelson problem asks for the **chromatic number of the plane**, denoted $\chi(\mathbb{R}^2)$ or simply $\chi$: the minimum number of colors needed to color every point of the Euclidean plane so that no two points at distance exactly $1$ receive the same color. Equivalently, it is the chromatic number of the infinite **unit-distance graph** $G(\mathbb{R}^2)$, whose vertices are all points of the plane and whose edges join pairs at distance $1$. The problem lies at the crossroads of combinatorial geometry, graph coloring, and Euclidean Ramsey theory.

The problem was first formulated in 1950 by **Edward Nelson**, then an 18-year-old student, who asked how many colors are needed and quickly observed a lower bound of $4$. **John Isbell**, a fellow student, supplied the upper bound of $7$ shortly thereafter using a hexagonal tiling. Thus from the outset it was known that $4 \le \chi \le 7$. The problem circulated by word of mouth — through Paul Erdős, who popularized it widely, and Hugo Hadwiger, whose work on tilings and whose later expository writing attached his name to the geometric circle of ideas. The first published appearances are usually credited to **Martin Gardner's** October 1960 *Scientific American* "Mathematical Games" column and to a 1961 paper by Hadwiger. Erdős repeatedly advertised the problem and offered small prizes, cementing its status as a celebrated open question.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: History — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text:
For 68 years the bounds $4 \le \chi \le 7$ stood unimproved, despite intense effort and the problem's deceptive simplicity. The lower bound $\chi \ge 4$ follows from the **Moser spindle**, a 7-vertex unit-distance graph (introduced by Leo and William Moser in 1961) that requires $4$ colors. The upper bound $\chi \le 7$ follows from coloring a tiling by regular hexagons of diameter slightly less than $1$ with $7$ colors in a repeating pattern. Whether the true value was $4$, $5$, $6$, or $7$ remained completely open.

The decisive event came in **April 2018**, when **Aubrey de Grey** — better known for work in biogerontology, but an accomplished amateur combinatorialist — posted "The chromatic number of the plane is at least 5" on the arXiv. De Grey exhibited an explicit unit-distance graph with $1581$ vertices that is not $4$-colorable, raising the lower bound to $\chi \ge 5$ for the first time. The construction was verified by SAT solvers and by independent researchers within days. Almost immediately a **Polymath project (Polymath16)** formed online to digest, simplify, and extend the result; it succeeded in driving down the vertex count of $5$-chromatic unit-distance graphs dramatically. The current bracket is $5 \le \chi \le 7$, and which of $5$, $6$, $7$ is correct remains the open frontier.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1945** — Hugo Hadwiger publishes work on coverings and tilings of the plane that later informs the geometric framing of the problem.
- **1950** — Edward Nelson poses the problem and proves $\chi \ge 4$; John Isbell proves $\chi \le 7$ via a hexagonal $7$-coloring.
- **1960** — Martin Gardner publicizes the problem in *Scientific American* ("Mathematical Games", October), reaching a wide audience.
- **1961** — Hugo Hadwiger's expository writing discusses the question; Leo and William Moser introduce the **Moser spindle**, giving a clean $7$-vertex proof of $\chi \ge 4$.
- **1981** — Paul Erdős repeatedly features the problem in problem collections, offers prizes, and speculates the answer may be sensitive to set-theoretic axioms.
- **1991** — Kenneth Falconer proves that any **measurable** coloring of the plane requires at least $5$ colors, hinting the answer might depend on regularity assumptions.
- **2008** — Alexander Soifer publishes *The Mathematical Coloring Book*, the definitive historical and mathematical account, documenting the origins and the "Nelson–Isbell" attribution.
- **2018 (April)** — Aubrey de Grey posts a $1581$-vertex unit-distance graph proving $\chi \ge 5$, the first improvement in 68 years.
- **2018 (April–onward)** — Polymath16 launches, verifies de Grey's graph, and drives the minimal vertex count of $5$-chromatic examples down toward a few hundred vertices.
- **2018–present** — Search continues for a $6$-chromatic unit-distance graph (which would give $\chi \ge 6$); no example is known, and $5 \le \chi \le 7$ stands.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Originator(s) — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The problem carries the names of two mathematicians, reflecting a divergence between its true origin and its conventional attribution. The historical record — reconstructed in painstaking detail by Alexander Soifer in *The Mathematical Coloring Book* — shows the problem was actually posed by **Edward Nelson**, with the key upper bound contributed by **John Isbell**; **Hugo Hadwiger's** name became attached through his prominence in the geometry of plane tilings and coverings, the milieu in which the problem was popularized.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Edward Nelson (1932–2014)
text: Edward Nelson was an American mathematician who spent most of his career at Princeton University. He is best known not for combinatorics but for deep work in **mathematical analysis, mathematical physics, and the foundations of mathematics** — including constructive quantum field theory (the "Nelson model" and Euclidean field theory), stochastic mechanics (a probabilistic reinterpretation of quantum mechanics), and internal set theory (a syntactic approach to nonstandard analysis). Late in life he became known for a controversial and ultimately withdrawn claim of an inconsistency in Peano arithmetic, reflecting his lifelong skepticism toward actual infinity and unrestricted induction.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Edward Nelson (1932–2014)
text: Nelson conceived the chromatic-number-of-the-plane question in **1950, while still a teenager**, years before his celebrated analytic work. The motivating idea is elementary and geometric: color the points of the plane so that points an exact unit apart never clash, and ask how few colors suffice. Nelson immediately produced the lower bound $\chi \ge 4$ by exhibiting a small configuration of points (the seed of what became the Moser spindle) that cannot be properly $3$-colored. The combinatorial flavor — translating a continuous geometric constraint into a graph-coloring question on an infinite graph — anticipates the modern field of unit-distance and Euclidean Ramsey problems. That a question so simple to state could resist resolution for nearly seven decades is part of its enduring appeal, and Nelson himself reportedly regarded it with affection as a curiosity from his youth.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: John Isbell (1930–2005)
text: John Rolfe Isbell was an American mathematician known principally for **category theory and topology** — Isbell duality (the adjunction between spaces and frames), uniform spaces, and the theory of injective metric spaces (the "injective hull"/tight span). As a young man, contemporaneously with Nelson, Isbell supplied the **upper bound $\chi \le 7$**: tile the plane with regular hexagons of diameter slightly less than $1$, and color the tiles with $7$ colors in a repeating pattern so that no two same-colored hexagons contain points at distance $1$. This elegant construction has never been improved, and closing the gap between Isbell's $7$ and the current lower bound of $5$ remains the heart of the open problem.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Hugo Hadwiger (1908–1981)
text: Hugo Hadwiger was a Swiss mathematician at the University of Bern, a major figure in **discrete and convex geometry**. His name is attached to the Hadwiger conjecture in graph theory (on graph minors and chromatic number), the Hadwiger theorem on valuations of convex bodies, and the Hadwiger–Kneser–Poulsen conjecture, among others. Hadwiger worked extensively on tilings, coverings, and the partition of space — exactly the geometric setting in which the plane-coloring problem naturally arises. Through his expository writing on unsolved problems and his standing in the community, the problem came to bear his name alongside Nelson's, even though he did not originate it.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Legacy
text: The originators' broader legacies far exceed this single problem — Nelson in analysis and logic, Isbell in category theory, Hadwiger in convex geometry — yet the chromatic number of the plane has arguably reached the widest audience of any of their contributions, precisely because anyone can understand it. It seeded the modern study of **unit-distance graphs** and **Euclidean Ramsey theory** (developed by Erdős, Graham, Montgomery, and others), and its 2018 reopening by Aubrey de Grey demonstrated that elementary, computer-assisted combinatorics can still crack a problem that resisted the world's best for a lifetime.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Approaches — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

The problem decomposes into two essentially independent tasks: **lowering the upper bound** below $7$ (a coloring/construction problem) and **raising the lower bound** above $5$ (a forbidden-configuration problem). The two halves have completely different toolkits, and the gap $5 \le \chi \le 7$ has stubbornly resisted both.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Finite unit-distance graphs (lower bounds via De Bruijn–Erdős)
text:
The foundational reduction is the **De Bruijn–Erdős theorem** (1951): the chromatic number of the infinite plane graph equals the supremum of the chromatic numbers of its *finite* subgraphs (this uses the axiom of choice / compactness). Hence to prove $\chi \ge k$ it suffices to exhibit a single **finite unit-distance graph** that is not $(k-1)$-colorable. This converts an infinitary problem into a finite combinatorial search.

- **Core idea.** Build a finite set of plane points, all pairwise unit distances forming edges, whose chromatic number is high.
- **Best result.** The **Moser spindle** (7 vertices, 1961) gives $\chi \ge 4$. Aubrey de Grey's $1581$-vertex graph (2018) gives $\chi \ge 5$. Polymath16 reduced the minimal known $5$-chromatic example to a few hundred vertices (notably examples with $510$ and $509$ vertices, and smaller candidates have been reported).
- **Barrier.** No finite $6$-chromatic unit-distance graph is known, and there is no proof that one exists. If $\chi = 5$, then *no* such graph exists and this entire route to $\chi \ge 6$ is closed; one would then need a fundamentally different (likely non-constructive or measure-theoretic) argument. The search space grows combinatorially, and human intuition for which rigid point configurations force high chromatic number is limited.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: SAT solvers and computer-assisted search
text:
De Grey's breakthrough and all of Polymath16's refinements are inseparable from automated reasoning.

- **Core idea.** Encode "$G$ is $k$-colorable" as a Boolean satisfiability instance; a SAT solver either finds a coloring or produces an UNSAT certificate proving none exists. To *shrink* a known $5$-chromatic graph, repeatedly delete vertices and re-test, keeping deletions that preserve $5$-chromaticity (vertex-criticality search).
- **Best result.** Independent SAT verification of de Grey's $1581$-vertex graph; minimization to $\sim 500$–$600$ vertex $5$-chromatic graphs; DRAT/LRAT-style proof certificates giving machine-checkable UNSAT proofs.
- **Barrier.** Establishing $\chi \ge 6$ would require an UNSAT instance for $5$-coloring of some unit-distance graph, but candidate graphs are far larger and the search is wildly under-constrained — one must first *find* a $6$-chromatic configuration before a solver can certify it. SAT scales poorly as graph size grows, and the geometric realizability constraint (points must have exact unit distances, often involving irrational coordinates) complicates encoding.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Measurable and density colorings
text:
A parallel line studies colorings with regularity assumptions, where powerful analytic tools apply.

- **Core idea.** Restrict to colorings whose color classes are Lebesgue-measurable (or otherwise structured), and use harmonic analysis / measure theory to forbid low color counts.
- **Best result.** **Falconer (1981/1991)** proved that any coloring of the plane into measurable color classes needs at least $5$ colors — i.e. the *measurable chromatic number* satisfies $\chi_m(\mathbb{R}^2) \ge 5$. More recently, spectral and density arguments (e.g. via the **Lovász theta function** and linear-programming bounds on the independence ratio of the unit-distance graph) push the measurable lower bound higher; results pushing $\chi_m \ge 6$ have been reported under measurability.
- **Barrier.** These methods bound the *measurable* chromatic number, which may strictly exceed the ordinary $\chi$. A coloring achieving the true $\chi$ might be highly non-measurable (using the axiom of choice), so measurable bounds need not transfer. This is the heart of Erdős's speculation that the answer could be **axiom-dependent**: the value of $\chi$ might differ under ZFC versus models with strong regularity (e.g. solvability/measurability of all sets, as under the axiom of determinacy). Shelah and Soifer constructed colorings illustrating such dependence in related distance graphs.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Upper-bound (coloring) constructions
text:
The complementary effort tries to beat Isbell's $7$.

- **Core idea.** Design an explicit, ideally periodic, partition of the plane into "unit-distance-avoiding" regions using fewer than $7$ colors, or prove via fractional/LP relaxation that fewer colors could suffice.
- **Best result.** Isbell's hexagonal $7$-coloring remains the record; **no construction with $6$ colors is known.** Probabilistic and "almost" colorings (e.g. colorings that fail only on a measure-zero set, or that avoid a slightly perturbed distance) have produced $6$-coloring-like results for variants, but not for the exact unit distance in the full plane.
- **Barrier.** Tilings with $6$ regions of bounded diameter seem to be obstructed by elementary packing constraints around each point, and no flexible non-tiling scheme has succeeded. There is no known structural reason the upper bound *must* be $7$, but every attempt to reach $6$ has failed, suggesting either a hidden obstruction or simply that the right construction has not been found.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Spectral / linear-programming and fractional relaxations
text:
- **Core idea.** Bound the **fractional chromatic number** and independence density of $G(\mathbb{R}^2)$ using eigenvalue (Hoffman-type) and LP/SDP methods, then leverage $\chi \ge \chi_f$.
- **Best result.** The fractional chromatic number is known to be strictly greater than $4$ (consistent with $\chi \ge 5$), and LP bounds give nontrivial constraints on the maximum density of a unit-distance-avoiding set (the "$m_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$" problem), with the best upper bounds on this density below $0.23$.
- **Barrier.** Fractional and measure-theoretic relaxations are inherently weaker than integer chromatic number and, like the measurable approach, do not directly control non-measurable colorings; they have so far been unable to force $\chi_f$ above $5$.

Across all approaches, the central obstruction is structural: we possess no theorem that *characterizes* when a unit-distance graph requires many colors, so progress depends on either lucky explicit constructions (the lower-bound side) or on regularity assumptions that may not capture the true, possibly pathological, optimal coloring (the upper-bound and measurable sides).

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Attempts — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

For nearly seven decades the problem produced no movement on either bound, so its "attempts" are largely a story of incremental insight, clever finite configurations, and one spectacular breakthrough — rather than disputed proofs of the full answer. Because the answer is still unknown ($5 \le \chi \le 7$), there is no claimed *complete* resolution to contest; the disputes that exist concern the correctness of specific finite constructions, all of which were settled by independent computer verification.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: The Moser spindle and early lower bounds
text: The earliest substantive "attempt" beyond Nelson's original $4$-point idea was the **Moser spindle** (Leo and William Moser, 1961): a rigid $7$-vertex, $11$-edge unit-distance graph that is $4$-chromatic, giving a clean and memorable proof of $\chi \ge 4$. For decades, efforts to extend this to a $5$-chromatic finite graph by hand failed. Larger hand-built configurations (e.g. Golomb's graph, various "pinwheel" and fused-spindle constructions) reached chromatic number $4$ but never $5$, reinforcing a widespread expectation that improvement might require entirely new ideas — or that the answer might simply be $4$.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: De Grey's 2018 breakthrough (verified)
text: The genuine near-miss-turned-success was **Aubrey de Grey's** April 2018 construction of a $1581$-vertex unit-distance graph with no proper $4$-coloring, proving $\chi \ge 5$. Because the claim rested on a finite, explicit graph, it was immediately checkable: within days, independent researchers reproduced the non-$4$-colorability using SAT solvers and confirmed all unit-distance constraints. The result has been accepted without serious dispute. De Grey's paper itself candidly noted that his particular $1581$-vertex graph was almost certainly far from minimal, inviting the community to simplify it.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Polymath16 minimizations (verified, with healthy scrutiny)
text: The **Polymath16** collaboration (2018–) set out to shrink de Grey's graph and to probe whether $\chi \ge 6$ might be reachable. It produced a cascade of smaller $5$-chromatic unit-distance graphs — examples with around $600$, $553$, $529$, $517$, $510$, and $509$ vertices have been reported, with searches for still smaller ones ongoing. Each reduction was a small "attempt" subject to the same SAT-based verification, and several intermediate claims were corrected through the open online review process — a model case of distributed, transparent error-checking rather than a dispute over a finished proof. Marijn Heule contributed both record-small constructions and machine-checkable UNSAT certificates, raising the standard of rigor.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Searches for a 6-chromatic graph (unresolved)
text: A natural and much-attempted route to $\chi \ge 6$ is to find a finite unit-distance graph that is not $5$-colorable. Despite substantial computational effort within and beyond Polymath16, **no such graph has been found**, and there is no proof that one exists. This is the principal open near-miss: every attempt to force a sixth color has so far failed, leaving genuine uncertainty about whether $\chi = 5$.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: The measurable / axiom-dependence thread
text: A subtler "attempt" concerns whether the problem is even fully determined by ZFC. **Kenneth Falconer (1981)** showed the *measurable* chromatic number is at least $5$, and **Saharon Shelah and Alexander Soifer** later exhibited related distance-graph colorings whose chromatic numbers depend on the underlying set theory (e.g. differing between ZFC and systems where all sets are measurable). No one has shown that the plane's *ordinary* chromatic number is axiom-dependent, but the possibility — championed by Erdős and Soifer — remains a live, unrefuted concern that complicates any attempted unconditional resolution.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Status of disputes
text: In summary: there is no retracted or seriously disputed claimed proof of the *value* of $\chi$. The one historic breakthrough (de Grey, $\chi \ge 5$) and all subsequent minimizations were finite and machine-verifiable, and survived independent scrutiny. The open questions — whether a $6$-chromatic finite graph exists, whether the upper bound can be beaten below $7$, and whether the true value is even ZFC-determined — remain genuinely unsettled rather than contested.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Key Papers — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text:
_The 25 most important papers. Each carries a verification flag._

Identifiers are given only where confidence is high; otherwise "—". The founding contributions (Nelson/Isbell) were never published as formal papers by their authors — the problem circulated orally and through expository channels — so the earliest entries are the expository and foundational works that recorded and propelled it. "Verify" flags reflect confidence that the work is real and correctly described, not a human source check.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Key Papers — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text: **Note on shortfall and reliability.** Only a handful of these are anchored by identifiers I am confident in — most securely de Grey's arXiv:1804.02385 (row 17). The founding contributions of Edward Nelson and John Isbell were never formal publications, so they cannot appear as papers with identifiers; their record lives in Gardner's column (row 2) and especially Soifer's *The Mathematical Coloring Book* (row 13), both high-confidence real works. Several rows (10, 11, 15, 16, 22, 24, 25) are flagged "ai-suggested" because, while the *line of work* they describe genuinely exists (LP/SDP density bounds, measurable lower bounds, post-2018 surveys), I cannot confidently attribute them to a specific titled paper without risking fabrication; they should be treated as research leads to verify, not established citations. The arXiv ids in rows 18 and 20 are plausible Polymath16-era preprints but are flagged "needs-verification" rather than asserted.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Notes
text: The active community today is unusually open and computationally driven. The **Polymath16** project — a public, online collaboration launched within days of de Grey's 2018 announcement — remains the organizing hub, coordinating the hunt for smaller $5$-chromatic unit-distance graphs and probing whether a $6$-chromatic graph exists. Contributors span professional combinatorialists, SAT/automated-reasoning specialists (notably **Marijn Heule** and collaborators, who supply both record constructions and formal UNSAT proofs), geometers such as **Geoffrey Exoo** and **Dan Ismailescu** working on explicit small graphs, and **Alexander Soifer**, who continues to curate the historical and conceptual record and to press the question of axiom dependence. The field also overlaps the broader Euclidean Ramsey theory tradition descending from **Erdős, Graham, Montgomery, and Spencer**. The frontier work is split between the construction side (driving the lower bound toward $6$ and the upper bound below $7$) and the measure-theoretic / set-theoretic side (clarifying whether $\chi$ is even fully determined by ZFC).

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Status & Frontier — The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Current state: `active-progress`, unresolved.** The chromatic number of the plane is known to satisfy
$$5 \le \chi(\mathbb{R}^2) \le 7,$$
and exactly which of $5$, $6$, $7$ is correct is open. The lower endpoint moved from $4$ to $5$ only in 2018; the upper endpoint of $7$ has stood since 1950 (Isbell).

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **$\chi \ge 5$.** Established by **Aubrey de Grey (2018)**, "The chromatic number of the plane is at least 5," arXiv:1804.02385, via an explicit $1581$-vertex unit-distance graph with no proper $4$-coloring. The proof is finite and has been independently verified by SAT solvers. This is the strongest unconditional lower bound.
- **$\chi \le 7$.** Isbell's $7$-coloring of a hexagonal tiling (1950), unimproved.
- **Minimization.** Via Polymath16 and Marijn Heule, the smallest known $5$-chromatic unit-distance graphs have been reduced to roughly $500$ vertices (examples near $510$ and $509$ vertices reported), with machine-checkable certificates.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: What is known (conditional / restricted)
text:
- **Measurable chromatic number $\ge 5$**, and stronger LP/spectral lower bounds, due to **Falconer (1981)** and later density/Lovász-theta arguments. These constrain colorings whose color classes are well-behaved (measurable), but a true optimal coloring could be non-measurable.
- **Possible axiom dependence.** Work of **Shelah–Soifer** shows that closely related distance-graph chromatic numbers can differ between ZFC and models where all sets are measurable, keeping alive Erdős's suspicion that the plane's $\chi$ might not be fully determined by ZFC alone — though this has not been demonstrated for the plane itself.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: What a full resolution would require
text:
- **To prove $\chi \ge 6$:** exhibit a finite unit-distance graph that is not $5$-colorable (by De Bruijn–Erdős, one such graph suffices). None is known, and there is no proof one exists. This is the most natural next target and the focus of ongoing computational search.
- **To prove $\chi = 5$:** beyond the existing $\chi \ge 5$, one would need a *$5$-coloring of the entire plane* with no monochromatic unit pair — no such coloring is known, and constructing one (or proving none exists) is wide open.
- **To prove $\chi \le 6$:** find an explicit $6$-coloring (e.g. a clever tiling or aperiodic scheme); every attempt to beat Isbell's $7$ has failed.
- **To prove $\chi = 7$:** establish a $6$-coloring is impossible — a much harder, currently inaccessible structural result.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem
heading: Plausible routes
text: The most active route is **computer-assisted finite search** (SAT solvers, vertex-criticality reduction) to either find a $6$-chromatic graph or to keep shrinking $5$-chromatic ones for insight. A second route is **analytic/measure-theoretic and LP/SDP bounds** pushing the measurable chromatic number (and density results $m_1(\mathbb{R}^2)$) higher, which could at least force $\chi \ge 6$ under regularity. A third, more speculative route engages the **set-theoretic** question of whether $\chi$ is ZFC-determined. Closing the upper bound below $7$ would likely require a genuinely new coloring idea rather than incremental tiling tweaks. Most experts regard $\chi \in \{5,6,7\}$ as genuinely undecided, with no strong consensus on the answer.

title: The Hadwiger–Nelson Problem

- [Inscribed Square Problem](../inscribed-square-problem/README.md) — another deceptively elementary open question in plane geometry.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: Admissible prime constellations occur infinitely often, with an explicit asymptotic density (the first Hardy–Littlewood conjecture).

*Originators: G. H. Hardy, J. E. Littlewood.*

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: History — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The $k$-tuple conjecture grew out of the most ambitious paper of the early twentieth-century theory of primes: G. H. Hardy and J. E. Littlewood's 1923 memoir *"Some problems of 'Partitio numerorum'; III: On the expression of a number as a sum of primes."* Working in the framework they had just built with their **circle method** (developed jointly with Ramanujan for partitions and additive problems), Hardy and Littlewood produced heuristic asymptotic formulas — and a whole hierarchy of conjectures — for the number of representations of integers by sums of primes and for the frequency of prime patterns. The $k$-tuple conjecture (their "Conjecture B," more precisely the general pattern underlying it) emerges from applying the singular-series heuristic of the circle method to the simplest possible additive question: how often a fixed tuple of linear forms is *simultaneously* prime.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: History — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text:
**Precise formulation.** Fix distinct integers $\mathcal{H}=\{h_1,\dots,h_k\}$. The set is **admissible** if for every prime $p$ the residues $h_i \bmod p$ do not cover all of $\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ — i.e. $\mathcal{H}$ does not occupy every congruence class modulo any $p$. (Inadmissibility forces some prime to divide one of $n+h_i$ for all $n$, killing the pattern beyond finitely many cases.) The conjecture asserts that for admissible $\mathcal{H}$,
$$\pi_{\mathcal{H}}(x) := \#\{n \le x : n+h_1,\dots,n+h_k \text{ all prime}\} \sim \mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H}) \frac{x}{(\log x)^k},$$
where the **singular series** is
$$\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H}) = \prod_{p}\left(1-\tfrac{1}{p}\right)^{-k}\left(1-\tfrac{\nu_{\mathcal{H}}(p)}{p}\right),$$
$\nu_{\mathcal{H}}(p)$ being the number of distinct residues of $\mathcal{H}$ modulo $p$. Admissibility is exactly the condition $\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})\neq 0$. The $k=2$, $\mathcal{H}=\{0,2\}$ case is the quantitative twin-prime conjecture; $k=1$ recovers the prime number theorem.

**Reformulations.** The conjecture has equivalent guises: as a special case of the **Bateman–Horn conjecture** (1962) for systems of irreducible polynomials; as a consequence of the **Dickson conjecture** (1904, qualitative) plus the right density; and, for many purposes, as a prediction subsumed by the **Hardy–Littlewood / Schinzel "Hypothesis H"** machinery. The qualitative core — infinitely many admissible $k$-tuples of primes — is itself wide open for every $k\ge 2$.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1849** — Polignac conjectures that every even gap occurs infinitely often between consecutive primes, an early qualitative ancestor.
- **1904** — L. E. Dickson formulates the qualitative prime $k$-tuple statement (Dickson's conjecture).
- **1919** — V. Brun introduces the Brun sieve, proving the twin-prime sum $\sum 1/p$ (twins) converges — the first hard quantitative result on prime pairs.
- **1923** — Hardy and Littlewood publish *Partitio Numerorum III*, giving the singular-series asymptotic and posing the $k$-tuple conjecture.
- **1940s–50s** — Selberg develops his sieve; upper bounds of the correct order of magnitude $\pi_{\mathcal{H}}(x) \ll \mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H}) x/(\log x)^k$ become standard.
- **1962** — Bateman and Horn generalize the asymptotic to arbitrary irreducible polynomial systems, unifying Hardy–Littlewood with Bunyakovsky.
- **1965** — Bombieri–Vinogradov theorem gives primes in arithmetic progressions "on average," a key sieve input.
- **1973** — Chen Jingrun proves every large even number is $p + P_2$ and there are infinitely many primes $p$ with $p+2 = P_2$ — the parity-barrier near-miss for twins.
- **2005** — Goldston, Pintz, Yıldırım (GPY) achieve $\liminf (p_{n+1}-p_n)/\log p_n = 0$, conditionally tying small gaps to the conjecture.
- **2013** — Yitang Zhang proves bounded gaps: infinitely many primes within $7\times 10^7$.
- **2013–14** — Maynard and (independently) Tao introduce the multidimensional GPY sieve, lowering bounds and proving infinitely many $m$-tuples in admissible sets near correct width; the Polymath8 project drives the gap below 246.
- **Present** — The full asymptotic remains unproven for every $k\ge 2$; only upper bounds, the bounded-gaps qualitative shadow, and conditional results are known.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The conjecture is the joint product of **Godfrey Harold Hardy** (1877–1947) and **John Edensor Littlewood** (1885–1977), whose thirty-five-year collaboration is the most celebrated partnership in the history of mathematics. They posed it in 1923 as one of a family of asymptotic conjectures in *Partitio Numerorum III*, the third installment of their grand programme on additive prime problems.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: G. H. Hardy
text: Hardy was educated at Winchester and Trinity College, Cambridge, where the rigid, computational tripos system he loathed gave way — under the influence of continental analysis — to his lifelong devotion to rigorous pure mathematics. His 1908 textbook *A Course of Pure Analysis* reformed the teaching of analysis in Britain. Hardy's mathematical taste ran to the analytic theory of numbers, Diophantine approximation, divergent series, and the Riemann zeta function; with Marcel Riesz he wrote the standard tract on Dirichlet series. He is remembered equally for *A Mathematician's Apology* (1940), his austere defense of mathematics for its own beauty, and for recognizing the genius of Srinivasa Ramanujan, whom he brought to Cambridge in 1914. The circle method itself was born from Hardy and Ramanujan's 1918 asymptotic formula for the partition function, and it was this analytic engine that Hardy and Littlewood turned on the primes.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: J. E. Littlewood
text: Littlewood, also Trinity-trained, was the more overtly powerful problem-solver of the pair, with deep contributions across analysis: the Littlewood–Paley theory, Tauberian theorems, nonlinear differential equations (with Mary Cartwright, anticipating chaos theory), and the theory of the zeta function. His 1914 result that $\pi(x) - \operatorname{li}(x)$ changes sign infinitely often — contradicting all numerical evidence available at the time — is a permanent caution against trusting computation over proof in this subject, and it bears directly on the kind of fine asymptotics the $k$-tuple conjecture predicts. Littlewood's famous credo that "the first business of a mathematician is to be wrong as often as possible" coexisted with extraordinary technical control.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: The ideas and motivation
text: By 1923 Hardy and Littlewood possessed a machine — the circle method — that converted additive questions into integrals over the unit circle, with the dominant "major arc" contribution governed by a product of local (prime-by-prime) densities: the **singular series**. The genius of *Partitio Numerorum III* was to recognize that the *same* local-density heuristic should govern not just Goldbach-type sums but the simultaneous primality of several linear forms. Counting an admissible tuple as prime "should" behave like $k$ independent events each of probability $1/\log x$, corrected by the genuine, non-independent local behavior modulo each prime — exactly what $\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})$ encodes. The conjecture is thus the cleanest expression of a deep philosophy: **primes are pseudorandom, but with explicitly computable local biases**. This same philosophy underlies the modern Hardy–Littlewood circle method, the Bateman–Horn conjecture, and the random-model heuristics of Cramér and of Granville.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Legacy and the modern formulation
text: Hardy and Littlewood gave the asymptotic in essentially its final form; the modern statement (with the admissibility condition made explicit via $\nu_{\mathcal{H}}(p)$) is a notational refinement, and Bateman–Horn (1962) generalized it to nonlinear polynomial systems. The qualitative root — Dickson's 1904 conjecture — predates them, but Hardy and Littlewood supplied the quantitative density that makes the conjecture a precise, testable assertion. Their broader legacy is the entire analytic apparatus that successors from Selberg to Zhang and Maynard have used to attack the very problem they posed.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Sieve methods
text:
The dominant attack since Brun. Sieves estimate $\pi_{\mathcal{H}}(x)$ by inclusion–exclusion over the primes dividing $\prod_i (n+h_i)$, truncated to keep error terms controlled. **Brun's sieve** (1919) first gave the correct *order of magnitude* upper bound and proved $\sum_{p:\,p,p+2 \text{ prime}} 1/p < \infty$. The **Selberg sieve** sharpened the upper bound to $\pi_{\mathcal{H}}(x) \le (2^k k! + o(1))\,\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})\, x/(\log x)^k$ — the right shape, off only by a constant factor. Sieves also drive the modern small-gap results (see below).

**The parity barrier.** This is the fundamental obstruction. Selberg observed (1949) that a sieve, as a linear/bilinear weighting blind to the *number* of prime factors modulo 2, cannot distinguish integers with an even number of prime factors from those with an odd number. Consequently a pure sieve can never prove that any $n+h_i$ is *exactly* prime — it can deliver an *almost-prime* (a $P_r$ with $r$ bounded) but not a genuine prime, and it cannot produce the asymptotic lower bound. Defeating parity requires importing external arithmetic information (a Type-I/Type-II estimate, a known asymptotic, a bilinear input). This barrier is why the conjecture's *lower bound* — even the qualitative "infinitely often" — remains open for every $k \ge 2$.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: The circle method and singular-series heuristics
text: The conjecture's *origin*: Hardy–Littlewood's major-arc analysis predicts the constant $\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})$. For a single linear form ($k=1$) the method works (it reproves the prime number theorem in progressions); for $k\ge 2$ the minor arcs cannot be controlled because the relevant exponential sum is over a *thin*, correlated set, and there is no asymptotic for the needed Type-II (bilinear) sums. The method gives the right *prediction* but no *proof*. Where the linear forms are replaced by a single norm form or where extra averaging is available (function fields, see below), variants succeed.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Small-gaps technology (GPY → Zhang → Maynard–Tao)
text: The most spectacular modern progress targets the *qualitative shadow* of the conjecture. **Goldston–Pintz–Yıldırım (2005)** designed a sieve weight detecting when an admissible tuple contains *at least two* primes, proving $\liminf_n (p_{n+1}-p_n)/\log p_n = 0$, conditional on a Bombieri–Vinogradov-type distribution level $\theta > 1/2$ for unconditional bounded gaps. **Yitang Zhang (2013)** broke through unconditionally by proving an averaged distribution estimate beyond $\theta=1/2$ over smooth moduli, yielding gaps below $7\times10^7$. **Maynard (2013)** and **Tao** independently introduced a *multidimensional* GPY weight that needs only $\theta>0$ (i.e. Bombieri–Vinogradov suffices), proving that every admissible $k$-tuple contains $\gg \log k$ primes infinitely often, and that $\liminf(p_{n+m}-p_n)<\infty$ for every $m$. **Polymath8** pushed the gap to **246**. *Crucially, none of this proves the asymptotic, nor even that a full admissible $k$-tuple ($k\ge 2$) is all-prime infinitely often* — it produces $m$ primes inside a tuple of larger size, not the tuple itself. The parity barrier still blocks the last step.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Function-field and large-characteristic analogues
text: Over $\mathbb{F}_q[t]$, analogues of the conjecture are provable in suitable limits. **Sawin–Shusterman (2018+)** established strong forms of the twin-prime and Hardy–Littlewood conjectures for polynomials over $\mathbb{F}_q[t]$ for $q$ large (with explicit power-saving error and the predicted singular-series constant), exploiting geometry/cohomology unavailable over $\mathbb{Z}$. These confirm the *shape* of the conjecture but do not transfer to the integers, where the analogue of the Riemann hypothesis for the relevant varieties is exactly what is missing.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Random and probabilistic models
text: Cramér's model and its refinements (Granville, and the Hardy–Littlewood heuristic itself) treat primes as random with density $1/\log n$, predicting $\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})$ and bias corrections. These are *not* proof strategies but are essential for stating sharp conjectures and for catching subtleties (e.g. Cramér's model needs the singular-series correction to get constants right). Numerical verification (e.g. extensive twin-prime and prime-constellation counts) matches the predicted asymptotic to high precision, lending strong empirical support without resolving the parity obstruction.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Connections to distribution-of-primes conjectures
text: Strong forms of the **Elliott–Halberstam conjecture** ($\theta$ arbitrarily close to 1) sharpen all of the above, and under generalized EH the Maynard–Tao method gives gaps of 6 (and 2 for a weakened goal). But even EH does not breach parity to yield the full $k$-tuple asymptotic — that appears to require a genuinely new idea capable of producing prime lower bounds, a tool the field does not currently possess.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

**Chen's theorem (1973) — the canonical near-miss.** Chen Jingrun proved that there are infinitely many primes $p$ such that $p+2$ is either prime or a product of two primes ($P_2$), and dually that every sufficiently large even number is $p+P_2$. This is as close to the twin-prime case ($k=2$, $\{0,2\}$) as any sieve has come. It cannot be improved to "$p+2$ prime" by the same method: the **parity barrier** (Selberg) forbids a pure sieve from forcing an exact prime. Chen's result is a genuine theorem, universally accepted, and stands as the boundary of what classical sieve theory alone achieves on this problem.

**GPY and the "$\liminf = 0$" near-miss (2005).** Goldston, Pintz, and Yıldırım proved $\liminf_n (p_{n+1}-p_n)/\log p_n = 0$ and showed that *any* distribution level beyond $\theta = 1/2$ (an Elliott–Halberstam increment) would give bounded gaps. For several years this was tantalizingly "one epsilon away." An earlier 2003 GPY preprint contained an argument later found to have a gap in its claimed bounded-gaps conclusion; the authors corrected and published the rigorous, weaker-but-sound statement. This episode is illustrative rather than disreputable — the corrected work directly seeded the 2013 breakthroughs.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text:
**Zhang's bounded gaps (2013).** Yitang Zhang's proof of infinitely many gaps below $7\times 10^7$ was checked carefully and confirmed within weeks (referees at *Annals*, then the Polymath8 community). It is fully accepted. It does *not* prove any full $k$-tuple is all-prime; it produces a pair of primes within a tuple of width $\sim 7\times10^7$, far from a fixed admissible $\{0,2\}$.

**Maynard–Tao (2013–14).** James Maynard and, independently, Terence Tao gave a cleaner multidimensional sieve, reproving bounded gaps from Bombieri–Vinogradov alone and establishing that admissible $k$-tuples contain $\gg \log k$ primes infinitely often. Accepted and foundational; again, *not* a proof of the full asymptotic for any $k\ge 2$.

**Function-field theorems (Sawin–Shusterman, 2018+).** Will Sawin and Mark Shusterman proved strong Hardy–Littlewood-type asymptotics (including twin primes with the correct constant and power-saving error) for $\mathbb{F}_q[t]$ with $q$ large. These are accepted results in the function-field setting and confirm the conjecture's predicted form, but do not transfer to $\mathbb{Z}$.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text:
**Claimed proofs over $\mathbb{Z}$ — a standing caution.** The twin-prime and $k$-tuple conjectures attract a steady stream of claimed elementary or analytic proofs, posted to arXiv and elsewhere. None has survived expert scrutiny. The recurring fatal flaw is almost always the **same parity obstruction**: an argument that controls almost-primes or that mishandles the minor-arc / Type-II contribution, implicitly assuming exactly the independence the conjecture asserts but does not grant for free. As of this writing **no claimed proof of the integer $k$-tuple conjecture (for any $k\ge 2$) is accepted**, and the dossier records none as established. The honest status is: powerful partial results, no resolution.

**Why the gap persists.** Every accepted advance (Chen, GPY, Zhang, Maynard–Tao) yields either an almost-prime, a *lower count* of primes inside an oversized tuple, or a conditional/averaged statement. The conjecture demands an *exact asymptotic with a specific constant for a specific fixed tuple* — a lower bound of the right order for genuine primes. No known technique crosses parity to deliver that.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of sieve theory and the analytic distribution of primes. James Maynard (Oxford) and Terence Tao (UCLA) remain central, the latter having both co-invented the modern multidimensional sieve and coordinated the **Polymath8** collaboration that drove the prime gap below 246. Kannan Soundararajan, Ben Green, Kaisa Matomäki, and Maksym Radziwiłł contribute the surrounding technology (multiplicative functions, equidistribution, large/small gaps), while Dan Goldston, János Pintz, and Cem Yıldırım continue to refine the GPY framework. In the function-field direction, Will Sawin and Mark Shusterman have proved strong analogues over $\mathbb{F}_q[t]$. The defining feature of the present community is honesty about the **parity barrier**: every group works on the *qualitative shadow* (bounded gaps, many primes in tuples, almost-primes) while openly regarding the full asymptotic — for even a single admissible pair — as requiring a genuinely new idea that no current program has produced.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: active-progress; open for every $k \ge 2$.** No admissible $k$-tuple ($k\ge 2$) has been proved to be all-prime infinitely often, let alone shown to satisfy the predicted asymptotic with constant $\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})$. The conjecture is unproven in even its weakest qualitative form for $k=2$ (the twin-prime conjecture is the special case $\mathcal{H}=\{0,2\}$).

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: What is known
text:
**Unconditional upper bounds (right order, wrong constant).** Sieve theory gives
$$\pi_{\mathcal{H}}(x) \le \big(2^k k! + o(1)\big)\,\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})\,\frac{x}{(\log x)^k},$$
i.e. the conjectured order of magnitude, off by a bounded factor. No matching unconditional lower bound of any positive order is known for $k\ge 2$.

**Almost-primes.** Chen's theorem (1973) gives infinitely many $n$ with $n$ prime and $n+2 = P_2$ — the conjecture "up to one extra prime factor," and only for the pair.

**Bounded gaps and many-primes-in-tuples.** Zhang (2013) proved infinitely many bounded prime gaps unconditionally; Maynard and Tao (2013–14) reproved this from Bombieri–Vinogradov and showed every admissible $k$-tuple contains $\gg \log k$ primes infinitely often, with $\liminf(p_{n+m}-p_n) < \infty$ for all $m$. Polymath8 reached gap $\le 246$. **This produces $m$ primes inside an admissible set, not the whole set, and yields no asymptotic.**

**Function fields.** Sawin–Shusterman proved strong Hardy–Littlewood-type asymptotics over $\mathbb{F}_q[t]$ for large $q$, confirming the predicted form in that model — but with no transfer to $\mathbb{Z}$.

**Conditional results.** Under the Elliott–Halberstam conjecture, Maynard–Tao gives gaps of 6; under generalized EH, sharper constellation results follow. Even full EH does not deliver the integer asymptotic — the parity barrier survives.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution requires
text:
A proof must (i) produce a **lower bound** of the correct order $\gg \mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H}) x/(\log x)^k$ for *genuine* primes simultaneously, not almost-primes, and (ii) pin the **constant** to $\mathfrak{S}(\mathcal{H})$. Achieving (i) means **breaking the parity barrier**: supplying arithmetic information (a Type-II / bilinear estimate, or an unconditional distribution input far beyond Bombieri–Vinogradov) strong enough to force exactly-prime values. No present technique does this; it is widely believed to demand a fundamentally new idea, comparable in novelty to the circle method or to the resolution of the Riemann hypothesis in the function-field analogue.

**Plausible routes.** (a) A dramatic strengthening of equidistribution (toward Elliott–Halberstam with power-saving and bilinear control) that, combined with a parity-defeating maneuver, yields lower bounds; (b) transfer of geometric/cohomological methods from the function-field proofs to $\mathbb{Z}$ — currently obstructed by the lack of an analogue of the Riemann hypothesis in the requisite form; (c) an entirely new analytic identity that sieves cannot see. None is close.

title: The Hardy–Littlewood k-tuple Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Twin Prime Conjecture](../twin-prime-conjecture/README.md) — the case $\mathcal{H}=\{0,2\}$.
- [Polignac's Conjecture](../polignac-conjecture/README.md) — every even gap recurs infinitely often.
- [Goldbach Conjecture](../goldbach-conjecture/README.md) — sister additive-prime problem via the same circle method.
- [Bunyakovsky Conjecture](../bunyakovsky-conjecture/README.md) — single-polynomial analogue, subsumed by Bateman–Horn.
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — governs the prime-distribution inputs the conjecture relies on.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Plain statement
text: Bound the number and relative position of limit cycles of a planar polynomial vector field of degree n; even for n = 2 no finite bound is known.

*Originators: David Hilbert.*

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: History — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

Hilbert's sixteenth problem has two parts. The **first part** concerns the topology of real algebraic curves and surfaces — the mutual position ("Lage," whence the German title *"Problem der Topologie algebraischer Kurven und Flächen"*) of the ovals of a nonsingular real plane algebraic curve of degree $n$, building on Harnack's 1876 bound of $\tfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}+1$ ovals. The **second part**, which this dossier treats, asks the analogous question for trajectories: to determine the maximum number and relative position of **limit cycles** of a planar polynomial differential system
$$\dot x = P(x,y),\qquad \dot y = Q(x,y),$$
where $P,Q$ are real polynomials of degree at most $n$. A limit cycle is an isolated periodic orbit in the phase plane. The quantity in question is the **Hilbert number** $H(n)$, the supremum over all degree-$n$ systems of the number of limit cycles.

Hilbert posed the problem in his address to the International Congress of Mathematicians in Paris on 8 August 1900, as the sixteenth of his famous twenty-three problems. He was motivated by Poincaré's qualitative theory of differential equations (1881–1886), which introduced limit cycles, and by the contemporaneous work on the location of ovals. Hilbert explicitly connected the two parts as questions about the topology of real solution sets — algebraic ones in the first part, transcendental (phase-portrait) ones in the second.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: History — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: The problem has proven extraordinarily resistant. Even the **existence of a finite bound** for the simplest nonlinear case $n=2$ (quadratic systems) remains open; no one knows whether $H(2)$ is even finite. The closely related but logically distinct **finiteness problem** — whether a *single fixed* polynomial system can have infinitely many limit cycles — is known to have a negative answer (each individual system has finitely many), but this "individual finiteness" was itself the subject of a celebrated saga of flawed and repaired proofs.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1881–1886** — Henri Poincaré develops the qualitative theory of planar differential equations and defines the notion of a limit cycle (*cycle limite*).
- **1900** — David Hilbert poses the problem at the ICM in Paris; the second part asks for bounds on the number and configuration of limit cycles of degree-$n$ polynomial systems.
- **1923** — Henri Dulac publishes a proof that an individual analytic planar system has finitely many limit cycles ("Dulac's theorem"); the argument is later found to contain an essential gap.
- **1939–1952** — N. N. Bautin studies the cyclicity of foci/centers; his 1952 result bounds the number of small-amplitude limit cycles bifurcating from a focus or center of a quadratic system by **3**.
- **1955–1957** — Petrovskii and Landis announce a (false) proof that $H(2)=3$; the error is acknowledged in the early 1960s.
- **1979–1980** — Shi Songling and, independently, Chen Lansun and Wang Mingshu construct concrete quadratic systems with **4** limit cycles, disproving the Petrovskii–Landis claim and showing $H(2)\ge 4$.
- **1981** — Yu. S. Ilyashenko exposes the gap in Dulac's 1923 proof, reopening the individual finiteness question.
- **1985–1988** — Roussarie and others recast the problem via the **Hilbert–Arnold problem** and the cyclicity of limit periodic sets (polycycles).
- **1991–1992** — Ilyashenko (*Finiteness Theorems for Limit Cycles*) and, independently, J. Écalle, complete correct proofs that each fixed analytic system has finitely many limit cycles.
- **1994** — Yu. Ilyashenko and S. Yakovenko frame the **existential / infinitesimal Hilbert 16th problem** (uniform bounds via Abelian integrals).
- **2010** — Binyamini, Novikov, and Yakovenko prove an explicit uniform bound for the number of zeros of Abelian integrals on bounded distance from the boundary (a partial result on the infinitesimal problem).
- **2016–present** — Activity on the **restricted/tangential Hilbert problem**, Liénard systems, slow–fast (geometric singular perturbation) methods, and computer-assisted lower bounds (current records put $H(2)\ge 4$, $H(3)\ge 13$ and similar growth $\gtrsim n^2\log n$ for general $n$).

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

**David Hilbert** (23 January 1862, Königsberg, Prussia — 14 February 1943, Göttingen) was the preeminent mathematician of the turn of the twentieth century and, with Poincaré, one of its two universalists. He took his doctorate at Königsberg in 1885 under Ferdinand von Lindemann, was shaped intellectually by his lifelong friendship with Hermann Minkowski and by Adolf Hurwitz, and in 1895 was appointed to the chair at Göttingen, which under his leadership became the world's center of mathematics until the Nazi expulsions of 1933 destroyed it.

Hilbert's career is often divided into phases, each of which he entered, transformed, and left: invariant theory (where his 1888 finite-basis theorem overturned the computational program of Gordan), algebraic number theory (the 1897 *Zahlbericht*), the foundations of geometry (*Grundlagen der Geometrie*, 1899, which axiomatized Euclid with full rigor), integral equations and spectral theory (whence "Hilbert space"), the foundations of mathematics and proof theory (the "Hilbert program"), and mathematical physics, where he worked in parallel with Einstein on the field equations of general relativity. This breadth is essential context: when Hilbert posed his problems he was not a specialist in any one of the fields they touched but a synthesist mapping the whole terrain of mathematics.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: The sixteenth problem grew directly out of two strands alive in 1900. The first was the **topology of real algebraic curves**: Hilbert had himself contributed to the study of the ovals of sextic curves, sharpening the question of which arrangements permitted by Harnack's bound ($\tfrac{(n-1)(n-2)}{2}+1$ ovals) are actually realizable. The second was **Poincaré's qualitative theory of differential equations**, the *Mémoire sur les courbes définies par une équation différentielle* (1881–1886), which had introduced limit cycles and the index theory of singular points and had shown that the global structure of phase portraits is a topological object worthy of study. Hilbert's insight — characteristic of his taste for problems that join distant fields — was that the *position of the ovals of an algebraic curve* and the *position of the limit cycles of a polynomial vector field* are two instances of one question: how complicated can the real solution set of a degree-$n$ object be, and how are its pieces arranged in the plane? He grouped both under a single heading precisely to advertise this analogy.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
Hilbert expected, as the rhetoric of his Paris address makes clear, that a sufficiently developed method would yield finite, computable bounds. He did not anticipate that the second part would resist all comers for over a century, that even the existence of a bound for quadratic systems would stay open, or that the auxiliary "individual finiteness" statement (each fixed system has finitely many limit cycles) would itself require the deep transcendental machinery later supplied by Ilyashenko and Écalle. The problem's difficulty is in some sense a measure of how far the analytic theory of differential equations lagged behind the algebraic geometry that inspired it.

Note on formulation: Hilbert's original wording asks for the "maximum number and position" of limit cycles for each degree $n$ — the existence of the Hilbert number $H(n)$ as a finite quantity is implicitly assumed. The modern reading separates this into (a) the **existence** of a finite $H(n)$, (b) its **value** or growth rate, and (c) the auxiliary individual-finiteness statement. Hilbert did not distinguish these; the century of work since has shown them to be of very different depth.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Originator(s) — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: Hilbert's broader legacy needs little rehearsal: the axiomatic method, the program for the foundations of mathematics (whose limits Gödel exposed in 1931), the formalism that organizes functional analysis, and above all the **list of 23 problems** itself, which set much of the agenda of twentieth-century mathematics. His epitaph in Göttingen reads *"Wir müssen wissen. Wir werden wissen."* — "We must know. We will know." — the closing words of his 1930 Königsberg address. For the sixteenth problem, at least, the "will" remains a hope rather than a fact.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Approaches — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Bifurcation of small-amplitude limit cycles (focus/center cyclicity)
text: The oldest concrete line of attack counts limit cycles that bifurcate from a single weak focus or from a center under perturbation. One computes the **Lyapunov (focal) quantities** $V_1, V_3, V_5, \dots$ — coefficients in the return-map expansion — whose successive vanishing controls how many small loops can emerge. Bautin's 1952 theorem is the model result: for quadratic systems the cyclicity of any focus or center is at most $3$, achieved by perturbing within the Bautin ideal. The method gives **local** lower bounds and, when the ideal structure is understood, exact local upper bounds. **Barrier:** it is intrinsically local — it bounds cycles near one equilibrium, not the global total, and the algebra of Lyapunov quantities becomes computationally intractable as degree grows (the ideal membership problem explodes). It says nothing about large-amplitude or non-bifurcating cycles.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Abelian integrals and the infinitesimal / weakened Hilbert problem
text: Perturb an integrable (Hamiltonian or Darboux-integrable) system $H = \text{const}$ by a small polynomial term $\varepsilon(\dots)$. To first order in $\varepsilon$, limit cycles are born from ovals $\gamma_h \subset \{H=h\}$ where the **Abelian integral** (Poincaré–Pontryagin–Melnikov function) $I(h)=\oint_{\gamma_h}\omega$ vanishes. The **infinitesimal (or weakened, or tangential) Hilbert 16th problem** asks for a uniform bound $Z(n)$ on the number of zeros of such $I(h)$ over all Hamiltonians and forms of degree $\le n$. This is the most actively studied tractable proxy. Best results: the bound is known to be finite and at most **doubly exponential / explicit** in restricted settings — Binyamini, Novikov and Yakovenko (2010) gave an explicit (though astronomically large) uniform upper bound for zeros away from the boundary of the period annulus; Petrov and others gave sharp bounds for special families (e.g. elliptic Hamiltonians). **Barrier:** even this linearized problem is hard; passing from the first-order (Melnikov) approximation back to the *actual* limit cycles requires controlling all higher-order terms, and a finite infinitesimal bound does not by itself yield a finite $H(n)$.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Individual finiteness (the Dulac problem)
text: A prerequisite, not the full problem: show that a *single fixed* analytic vector field has only finitely many limit cycles. Dulac claimed this in 1923 by analyzing the **Dulac (return) map** near polycycles, but Ilyashenko found in 1981 that the asymptotic expansions used did not preclude an accumulation of cycles on a polycycle. Independently in 1991–1992, **Ilyashenko** (via complex-analytic continuation and functional cochains) and **Écalle** (via resurgent functions and "transseries"/the *Tougeron* theory) gave complete, very different proofs. **Result:** every analytic planar system has finitely many limit cycles — full stop. **Barrier:** the proofs are non-effective; they give no bound depending on degree, so they do not constrain $H(n)$ at all. Uniformity across a family (the **Hilbert–Arnold problem**: finite cyclicity of polycycles in generic finite-parameter families) remains open in general, proven only for elementary polycycles (Ilyashenko–Yakovenko, Kaloshin).

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Liénard systems and slow–fast (geometric singular perturbation) methods
text: Restricting to **Liénard equations** $\ddot x + f(x)\dot x + x = 0$ makes the problem a sandbox. Smale singled out the polynomial Liénard case as one of his eighteen problems for the 21st century, conjecturing a bound polynomial in the degree of $f$. Lins, de Melo and Pugh (1977) settled the classical case $\deg f = 2k+1$ giving exactly $k$ cycles, and conjectured the general count; this **LMP conjecture was disproved** by Dumortier, Panazzolo and Roussarie (2007), who found extra cycles. **Slow–fast** analysis (blow-up of the singular limit, Dumortier–Roussarie, Krupa–Szmolyan) produces and counts **canard** cycles and currently drives many of the strongest lower-bound constructions. **Barrier:** results are confined to special normal forms; transfer to general degree-$n$ systems is not available.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Computer-assisted and constructive lower bounds
text: Explicit constructions, often computer-verified, establish the records on the left side of the inequality $H(n)\ge \cdot$. Quadratic systems with **4** cycles (Shi 1980; Chen–Wang 1979) give $H(2)\ge 4$ — widely conjectured to be the exact value. Cubic systems have been pushed to **13** cycles (Li, Liu, Yang and others). For general $n$, configurations of order $\tfrac{1}{2}n^2\log n$ cycles have been built (Christopher–Lloyd type constructions, refined later), establishing the asymptotic lower bound $H(n) \gtrsim n^2\log n$ that exceeds the naive $\sim n^2$ guess. **Barrier:** these are existence proofs of *lower* bounds; they certify nothing about finiteness or upper bounds, which is where the actual problem lies.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Algebraic / model-theoretic and o-minimality programs
text: A newer strand seeks **effective** finiteness via tame geometry: pfaffian functions, o-minimal structures, and effective bounds on zeros of solutions of polynomial ODEs (Khovanskii's fewnomial theory, Novikov–Yakovenko, recent point-counting à la Pila–Wilkie applied to period integrals). The aim is to make the Ilyashenko–Écalle finiteness *quantitative and uniform*. **Result:** explicit bounds in restricted infinitesimal settings (as above). **Barrier:** the structures controlling the full nonlinear return map for degree-$n$ systems are not yet known to be o-minimal in a way that yields a degree-dependent bound; the "monodromic" accumulation that defeated Dulac is exactly the tame-geometry obstruction.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Attempts — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

**Dulac's 1923 "theorem" (gap, later repaired).** Henri Dulac published *Sur les cycles limites* claiming that every analytic planar vector field has finitely many limit cycles. The proof studied the return map near a polycycle through normal forms of the Dulac maps at the singular points. In **1981 Yu. Ilyashenko** showed the argument was incomplete: the asymptotic series used did not rule out limit cycles accumulating on a polycycle (the function might oscillate beyond all orders of its expansion). The statement was eventually re-proved correctly and independently by **Ilyashenko (1991)** and **Écalle (1992)** with entirely new transcendental machinery. This is the canonical example of a long-accepted "result" that was in fact open for nearly six decades.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Attempts — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
**Petrovskii–Landis (1955–1957), the false $H(2)=3$.** I. G. Petrovskii and E. M. Landis published a proof that quadratic systems have at most three limit cycles and that $H(n)$ obeys an explicit formula. The method used complexification and estimates on the variation of the return map along complex paths. By the early 1960s the authors themselves acknowledged a fundamental error in the argument; the claimed bound was wrong. The refutation became concrete around 1979–1980 when **Shi Songling** and, independently, **Chen Lansun and Wang Mingshu** exhibited explicit quadratic systems with **four** limit cycles (a small-amplitude triple near one focus plus a large cycle around another), proving $H(2)\ge 4$. Whether $H(2)=4$ remains open; $4$ is the widely held conjecture.

**Bautin's center cyclicity (1952), a genuine and durable partial result.** N. N. Bautin proved that no more than three small-amplitude limit cycles can bifurcate from a single focus or center of a quadratic system, via the structure of the ideal generated by the focal quantities (the "Bautin ideal"). This is correct and remains a cornerstone, but it is *local*: it bounds cycles near one equilibrium, not the global Hilbert number, so it does not bound $H(2)$.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Attempts — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
**The Lins–de Melo–Pugh conjecture (1977), disproved 2007.** For the Liénard equation with $f$ of degree $2k+1$ they proved exactly $k$ cycles in the classical case and conjectured this count as a general upper bound for polynomial Liénard systems. **Dumortier, Panazzolo and Roussarie (2007)** disproved the conjecture by exhibiting Liénard systems with more limit cycles than predicted, using slow–fast/canard techniques. This near-miss reshaped expectations about how cycle counts grow.

**Periodic claims of complete solutions.** As with several famous open problems, preprints announcing a full resolution of Hilbert's 16th (a finite explicit $H(n)$, or even just finiteness of $H(2)$) appear from time to time on arXiv and elsewhere. None has been accepted by the research community as of this writing; the consensus is that the existence of a finite bound for quadratic systems is unproven. Such claims should be treated with caution: the history above shows that even expert arguments in this area have concealed fatal gaps for decades, and the burden of independent verification is high.

**Lower-bound records as honest progress.** Distinct from disputed upper bounds, the constructive lower bounds are uncontested. Cubic systems with $11$ and later $13$ limit cycles, and general-degree configurations realizing $\sim n^2\log n$ cycles, are accepted constructions. They sharpen the conjectured growth rate but, by their nature, cannot resolve the existence question that lies at the heart of the problem.

text:

| 4 | Nikolai N. Bautin | 1908–1993 | breakthrough | Proved cyclicity $\le 3$ for foci/centers of quadratic systems (1952); introduced the Bautin ideal. |

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of dynamical systems, real algebraic geometry, and transcendental number theory / tame geometry. The "Moscow school" lineage of Arnold–Ilyashenko–Yakovenko (now including Gal Binyamini and Dmitry Novikov at the Weizmann Institute) drives the infinitesimal/effective-finiteness program via o-minimality and point-counting. A strong "Catalan/Belgian–French" school around Roussarie, Freddy Dumortier, Jaume Llibre, Armengol Gasull, and Joan Torregrosa develops bifurcation theory, slow–fast/canard methods, and explicit cycle constructions. A large and productive Chinese school (Jibin Li, Yulin Zhao, Maoan Han, Chengzhi Li and many others) holds most of the current lower-bound records for cubic and higher-degree systems. Lubomir Gavrilov and Pavao Mardešić contribute on the algebraic-geometry side of Abelian integrals. Despite this breadth, the central existence question — is $H(2)$ finite? — has seen no decisive movement, and the field's recent energy is concentrated on the tractable infinitesimal proxy and on computer-assisted constructions.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Status & Frontier — Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open.** More than a century after 1900, the core question is unanswered even in its simplest nontrivial case. The Hilbert number $H(n)$ — the maximum number of limit cycles of a real planar polynomial vector field of degree $n$ — is not known to be **finite** for any $n \ge 2$. In particular it is unknown whether $H(2) < \infty$ for quadratic systems. This is the central, embarrassing gap: no one can rule out that some sequence of degree-2 systems has unboundedly many limit cycles.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Individual finiteness.** Every *fixed* analytic planar vector field has finitely many limit cycles (Ilyashenko 1991; Écalle 1992), repairing Dulac's flawed 1923 proof. This bounds cycles for each system separately but gives **no** degree-uniform bound, hence does not bound $H(n)$.
- **Local cyclicity.** Bautin (1952): at most $3$ small-amplitude cycles bifurcate from a focus/center of a quadratic system. Analogous finite local bounds exist for cubics.
- **Lower bounds.** $H(2)\ge 4$ (Shi 1980; Chen–Wang 1979); $H(3)\ge 13$ (Chinese-school constructions); and for general $n$, configurations of order $n^2\log n$ cycles are known, giving $H(n)\gtrsim \tfrac{1}{2}n^2\log n$. The growth is therefore at least slightly super-quadratic.
- **Smale's Liénard restriction** and slow–fast canard theory give finite, computable bounds in special families, but the Lins–de Melo–Pugh conjectured bound for general polynomial Liénard systems is **false** (Dumortier–Panazzolo–Roussarie 2007).

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Strongest conditional / partial results
text: The most tractable proxy is the **infinitesimal (weakened) Hilbert 16th problem**: bound the number of zeros of Abelian integrals $I(h)=\oint_{\gamma_h}\omega$ over degree-$n$ Hamiltonians and forms. Binyamini, Novikov and Yakovenko (2010, *Inventiones*) proved an **explicit uniform upper bound** for the number of such zeros at bounded distance from the boundary of the period annulus — the first fully effective uniform result, albeit with an astronomically large bound. Sharp counts are known for special families (elliptic Hamiltonians, the quadratic infinitesimal case). A finite infinitesimal bound is necessary groundwork but is **not** equivalent to a finite $H(n)$: passing from the first-order Melnikov approximation to the actual cycles requires controlling all higher orders and the loss of integrability.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A complete solution would need to (i) prove $H(n)$ is finite for every $n$ — the **existence** of the bound — and ideally (ii) compute or bound it, plus describe the **relative position/configuration** of the cycles. The decisive obstruction is the possible accumulation of limit cycles onto a **polycycle** (the Hilbert–Arnold problem: uniform finite cyclicity of limit periodic sets across a family). This is proven only for **elementary** polycycles (all vertices hyperbolic/semi-hyperbolic; Ilyashenko–Yakovenko, Kaloshin); the general case, with degenerate (nilpotent) and non-monodromic vertices, is open and is exactly what blocks a uniform bound.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Effective/tame-geometry program** — make Ilyashenko–Écalle finiteness *quantitative* via o-minimality, pfaffian bounds, and Pila–Wilkie-style point counting (Binyamini–Novikov and collaborators). Most credible path to a uniform bound.
2. **Complete the Hilbert–Arnold finite-cyclicity program** for all polycycles up to some degree, then assemble a bound on $H(n)$ via compactness of the parameter space.
3. **Quadratic-only attack** — even just proving $H(2)<\infty$ (the long-conjectured value is $4$) would be a landmark and might isolate the essential difficulty.

title: Hilbert's Sixteenth Problem (Second Part)
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Kakeya Conjecture](../kakeya-conjecture/README.md) — another deep question at the interface of real analysis and geometric measure of solution sets.
- [Jacobian Conjecture](../jacobian-conjecture/README.md) — polynomial maps and their global behavior, a sibling in real/complex algebraic dynamics.
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — the comparison case among Hilbert's problems for "famous, deep, and resistant."
- [Navier–Stokes Smoothness](../navier-stokes-smoothness/README.md) — a sibling open problem in the qualitative theory of differential equations.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: The Hodge Conjecture
text: > **Atlas Rank #4** · CSS **79.9** · Field: *Algebraic Geometry* · Posed: **1950** · Status: `open`  · 🏛️ **Millennium Prize Problem**

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: On a non-singular complex projective variety, every Hodge class is a rational linear combination of cohomology classes of algebraic cycles.
