*Originators: W. V. D. Hodge.*

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: History — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Origin, formulation, and timeline._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: How the problem arose
text:
The Hodge conjecture sits at the junction of three nineteenth- and twentieth-century developments: the theory of algebraic cycles (Severi, Lefschetz), the topology of complex manifolds, and the harmonic theory of differential forms. On a compact Kähler manifold $X$, W. V. D. Hodge's theory of harmonic integrals (developed in the 1930s and codified in his 1941 book *The Theory and Applications of Harmonic Integrals*) produces the **Hodge decomposition** of complex cohomology,
$$H^{n}(X,\mathbb{C}) \;=\; \bigoplus_{p+q=n} H^{p,q}(X), \qquad \overline{H^{p,q}} = H^{q,p},$$
where $H^{p,q}$ consists of classes representable by harmonic forms of type $(p,q)$. An irreducible algebraic subvariety $Z \subset X$ of complex codimension $p$ defines, via its fundamental class, a cohomology class in $H^{2p}(X,\mathbb{Q})$ whose image in $H^{2p}(X,\mathbb{C})$ lies in the middle piece $H^{p,p}(X)$. The question Hodge raised is the **converse**: which rational $(p,p)$-classes arise this way?

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: How the problem arose
text:
A **Hodge class** of codimension $p$ is an element of
$$\mathrm{Hdg}^{p}(X) := H^{2p}(X,\mathbb{Q}) \cap H^{p,p}(X).$$
The conjecture asserts that on a *non-singular complex projective* variety $X$, every Hodge class is a $\mathbb{Q}$-linear combination of fundamental classes of algebraic subvarieties (algebraic cycles). For $p=1$ this is a theorem — the **Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem** (Lefschetz 1924) — which identifies $\mathrm{Hdg}^{1}(X)$ with the image of the Picard group (divisors / line bundles). The Hodge conjecture is the program to extend that theorem to all codimensions.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Formulation and reformulations
text:
Hodge's original 1950 statement was broader and partly *false* as stated: it included an integral version and a statement about the Hodge filtration's lower pieces. Two corrections shaped the modern form:

- **Integral Hodge conjecture is false.** Atiyah and Hirzebruch (1962) used topological $K$-theory and Steenrod operations to produce torsion cohomology classes that are Hodge but not algebraic, forcing the rational coefficients $\mathbb{Q}$.
- **The "general Hodge conjecture" (lower filtration levels) needed amendment.** Grothendieck (1969), in a sharply worded note, showed Hodge's general statement was false and proposed the corrected version now called the **Grothendieck amended general Hodge conjecture**, phrased via coniveau / the Hodge filtration.

The standard reference statement today is the one in the official **Clay Mathematics Institute Millennium Problem description** (Deligne, 2000), which restricts to the codimension-$p$ rational case on smooth projective varieties.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1924** — Lefschetz proves the $(1,1)$-theorem, the codimension-1 case, via normal functions and Poincaré normal functions.
- **1941** — Hodge publishes *The Theory and Applications of Harmonic Integrals*, establishing the Hodge decomposition.
- **1950** — Hodge poses the conjecture in his ICM (Cambridge, MA) address, "The topological invariants of algebraic varieties."
- **1957** — Kodaira's embedding theorem and Hodge theory mature; Weil and others reformulate cycles cohomologically.
- **1961–62** — Atiyah–Hirzebruch disprove the **integral** Hodge conjecture.
- **1969** — Grothendieck publishes "Hodge's general conjecture is false for trivial reasons," giving the amended general conjecture.
- **1969** — Mumford shows $0$-cycles on surfaces with $p_g>0$ are "infinite-dimensional," signalling deep obstructions for related cycle questions.
- **1977** — Deligne, Milne, Ogus, Shih develop **absolute Hodge cycles**; Deligne proves Hodge classes on abelian varieties are absolutely Hodge.
- **1995** — Lewis publishes *A Survey of the Hodge Conjecture*, the standard modern reference.
- **2000** — Clay Mathematics Institute names it one of seven **Millennium Prize Problems** (\$1,000,000), with Deligne's official problem description.
- **2013** — Voisin's monograph and survey articles map the obstruction landscape (Kähler counterexamples, defect of the integral conjecture).
- **Present** — The conjecture remains **open** in every codimension $p\ge 2$; verified only in special families (abelian varieties of low dimension, hypersurfaces, certain $K3$ and Calabi–Yau cases) and via the unconditional Lefschetz $(1,1)$ result.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Originator(s) — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: William Vallance Douglas Hodge (1903–1975)
text: W. V. D. Hodge was a Scottish mathematician, born 17 June 1903 in Edinburgh. He studied at the University of Edinburgh and then at St John's College, Cambridge, where the influence of the Cambridge geometric tradition — and contact with the Italian school of algebraic geometry — shaped his early interests. After a period at Bristol, he returned to Cambridge, becoming the **Lowndean Professor of Astronomy and Geometry** in 1936 and, later, Master of Pembroke College. He was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1938 and knighted in 1959; he served as a leading figure in British mathematics until his death on 7 July 1975.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Mathematical background and the road to harmonic integrals
text:
Hodge's defining achievement was the **theory of harmonic integrals**, which he developed through the 1930s and presented systematically in his 1941 monograph *The Theory and Applications of Harmonic Integrals*. The core idea fused three strands:

- **Riemann's and Lefschetz's topology of algebraic varieties** — period integrals, the homology of complex manifolds, and Lefschetz's hard and weak theorems on hyperplane sections.
- **De Rham's theorem**, identifying cohomology with closed differential forms modulo exact ones.
- **Elliptic PDE / potential theory** — the recognition that within each de Rham class there is a *unique harmonic representative*, a form annihilated by the Laplace–Beltrami operator of a chosen (Kähler) metric.

On a compact Kähler manifold the Laplacian respects the bidegree $(p,q)$ of forms, and this is precisely what yields the **Hodge decomposition**. The original proofs in the 1941 book contained a gap in the existence theory for harmonic forms; this was repaired rigorously by **Hermann Weyl** (the "method of orthogonal projection") and, in greater generality, by the elliptic theory of **Kodaira** and others. The fully rigorous Hodge theory that resulted became one of the foundational pillars of modern complex and algebraic geometry.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Why he posed the conjecture
text: Hodge understood that his decomposition gave a purely *analytic/topological* invariant — the spaces $H^{p,q}$ — sitting on top of a variety whose origin was *algebraic*. Algebraic subvarieties (cycles) contribute classes living in the diagonal pieces $H^{p,p}$. The natural and ambitious question was whether these geometric cycles **account for all** of the rational $(p,p)$ cohomology. The $p=1$ case was already a theorem (Lefschetz's $(1,1)$-theorem), tying $H^{1,1}\cap H^2(\mathbb{Z})$ to divisors. Hodge's conjecture is the bold extrapolation of that single known case to all codimensions, articulated in his **1950 International Congress of Mathematicians address** in Cambridge, Massachusetts, "The topological invariants of algebraic varieties."

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Historical root vs. modern formulation
text: Hodge's 1950 statement was, as posed, partly incorrect. He included an **integral** version (later disproved by Atiyah–Hirzebruch, 1962) and a "general" form about deeper levels of the Hodge filtration that **Grothendieck (1969)** showed to be false "for trivial reasons," replacing it with the amended general Hodge conjecture. The surviving, sharpened statement — rational coefficients, smooth projective varieties, codimension-$p$ Hodge classes — is the one enshrined as a Clay Millennium Problem.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Legacy
text: Beyond the conjecture, Hodge co-authored with **Daniel Pedoe** the influential three-volume *Methods of Algebraic Geometry*, a bridge between the classical Italian school and modern foundations. **Hodge theory** — Hodge structures, variations of Hodge structure, mixed Hodge structures (Deligne), and the entire industry of period maps — descends directly from his work and underlies much of twentieth- and twenty-first-century algebraic geometry, transcendental number theory, and mathematical physics. The London Mathematical Society's Hodge Prize and the Hodge Institute in Edinburgh both honour his name.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Approaches — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: The Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem and induction by codimension
text: The only fully proved instance is codimension $p=1$: the **Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem** identifies $H^{1,1}(X)\cap H^2(X,\mathbb{Z})$ with the classes of divisors, via the exponential sequence and the Picard group. The natural hope is to climb in codimension. **Hard Lefschetz** and the **Lefschetz hyperplane theorem** let one transfer cohomology between $X$ and its hyperplane sections, and the **Lefschetz standard conjecture** (that the inverse of hard Lefschetz is induced by an algebraic correspondence) would yield large parts of the Hodge picture. *Best result:* hard Lefschetz is a theorem (Hodge theory; Deligne in char $p$), but the standard conjecture itself is open, so this route stalls. *Barrier:* no general mechanism produces cycles in codimension $\ge 2$; products and intersections of divisors generate only a (generally small) subring of $H^{2p,p}$.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Absolute Hodge cycles and the abelian-variety case
text: Deligne's theory of **absolute Hodge cycles** (Deligne–Milne–Ogus–Shih, 1982) provides Hodge classes that are simultaneously Hodge in every $\ell$-adic and de Rham realization. Deligne **proved every Hodge class on an abelian variety is absolutely Hodge**, a major structural result and the strongest unconditional evidence for the conjecture on abelian varieties. *Best result:* the Hodge conjecture is known for abelian varieties whose Hodge classes are generated by divisors (e.g. simple abelian varieties of prime dimension, products of elliptic curves; many cases via the **Hodge group / Mumford–Tate group** being large). *Barrier:* the **Weil and Mumford–Tate abelian varieties** carry "exotic" Hodge classes not built from divisors; for these the conjecture remains open, and absolute-Hodge-ness does not by itself produce a cycle.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Mumford–Tate groups and the André–Deligne machinery
text: One reframes Hodge classes as the invariants of the **Mumford–Tate group** acting on cohomology; algebraicity questions become questions about which invariants are "motivated." André's theory of **motivated cycles** unconditionally realizes many Hodge classes (those obtained from hard Lefschetz and Künneth) as motivated, capturing properties expected of algebraic cycles. *Best result:* the standard conjectures hold for abelian varieties' motivated motives; Künneth and Lefschetz components are motivated. *Barrier:* "motivated" is weaker than "algebraic"; bridging that final gap is exactly the open problem.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Normal functions and Griffiths' infinitesimal method
text: Griffiths revived **Lefschetz's normal functions** as a tool: a Hodge class spreads out over a family and defines a normal function (a section of a family of intermediate Jacobians); the class is algebraic iff a certain singularity / zero-locus condition holds. Green–Griffiths and Brosnan–Fang–Nie–Pearlstein turned this into a program: the Hodge conjecture would follow from the existence of **singularities of normal functions** along the right loci. *Best result:* the **Hodge conjecture is equivalent** to such a singularity statement after spreading out (Green–Griffiths, de Cataldo–Migliorini variants); the algebraicity of the zero locus of admissible normal functions (Brosnan–Pearlstein, building on Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan's algebraicity of Hodge loci) is established. *Barrier:* producing the singularities themselves — i.e. actually constructing the cycle — has not been achieved in general.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan: algebraicity of Hodge loci
text: A landmark *unconditional* result: **Cattani, Deligne, and Kaplan (1995)** proved that the locus where a given Hodge class remains Hodge (the **Hodge locus**) is a countable union of algebraic subvarieties of the base. This is exactly what the Hodge conjecture predicts (Hodge loci should be cut out by the algebraicity of cycles) and is strong evidence, recently reproved and extended by o-minimality / **definability** methods (Bakker–Klingler–Tsimerman, 2020). *Barrier:* algebraicity of the *locus* does not produce an algebraic *cycle* realizing the class.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Hodge theory in characteristic $p$ and the Tate conjecture
text: The **Tate conjecture** is the arithmetic analogue (Galois-invariant $\ell$-adic classes are algebraic). Progress on Tate (e.g. for $K3$ surfaces, abelian varieties by Faltings, Tate, Zarhin) informs Hodge via comparison theorems. *Barrier:* the two conjectures are independent in general; neither is known to imply the other unconditionally.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Negative and limiting results (obstructions)
text:
- **Integral Hodge conjecture is false** (Atiyah–Hirzebruch, 1962; refined by Kollár's degree examples and Totaro): torsion and non-torsion integral classes can be non-algebraic. This forces rational coefficients and is a hard ceiling on naive integral strengthenings.
- **Kähler (non-projective) version is false** (Voisin, 2002): there exist compact Kähler manifolds with Hodge classes not generated by classes of *analytic* cycles, so the conjecture is genuinely tied to **projectivity**.
- **Griffiths' non-finitely-generated / non-algebraic intermediate Jacobians** and **Mumford's infinite-dimensionality** of $0$-cycles show the Chow-group / cycle-class map is far from an isomorphism, dimming hopes for a soft surjectivity argument.
- There is no known **diagonal cycle / motivic** construction outputting codimension-$\ge 2$ cycles from cohomological input — the structural shortage of cycles, not a logical obstruction, is the central difficulty.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Attempts — The Hodge Conjecture
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Established partial results (genuine progress, not full proofs)
text:
- **Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem (Lefschetz, 1924).** The codimension-1 case is a full theorem; it is the historical anchor and the reason the conjecture seemed plausible.
- **Abelian varieties (Deligne, 1982).** Deligne proved every Hodge class on an abelian variety is **absolutely Hodge**. The Hodge conjecture itself is known for many abelian varieties — e.g. when the Hodge ring is generated by divisors, and for various Mumford–Tate configurations — but **not all**; **Weil-type** abelian varieties with exotic Hodge classes remain open.
- **Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan (1995).** Hodge loci are algebraic. Widely viewed as the strongest unconditional structural evidence; it confirms a prediction of the conjecture without producing cycles.
- **$K3$ and abelian surfaces.** The conjecture holds for $K3$ surfaces and abelian surfaces (Hodge classes in $H^2$ are divisor classes by Lefschetz $(1,1)$, and there are no higher non-trivial codimensions to test).
- **Hypersurfaces and complete intersections.** For low degrees/dimensions the Hodge classes are spanned by the hyperplane class and its powers; the conjecture holds trivially or via explicit cycles in many such families.
- **Shioda, Schoen, and others** verified the conjecture for specific families (Fermat hypersurfaces of certain degrees, products of curves, some Calabi–Yau threefolds) by explicitly exhibiting the cycles.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Conditional reductions and equivalences
text:
- **Standard conjectures (Grothendieck).** The Lefschetz standard conjecture would settle major structural pieces; it remains open but is a recognized, respectable line.
- **Normal-function singularity program (Green–Griffiths; Brosnan–Fang–Nie–Pearlstein).** Reduces the Hodge conjecture to the existence of singularities of admissible normal functions. The supporting algebraicity inputs (zero loci of normal functions) were proved, but the reduction does not by itself yield a proof — the predicted singularities have not been produced in general.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Disputed / withdrawn / unverified claimed proofs
text:
The Hodge conjecture, being a Millennium Prize Problem, attracts purported proofs; **none has been accepted by the mathematical community**, and several have been formally withdrawn or refuted. Stated neutrally:

- Numerous **arXiv preprints** claiming a general proof have appeared over the years (e.g. periodically in the math.AG listings). In every case to date, experts have identified gaps — most commonly an unjustified leap from a Hodge class being *cohomologically* or *motivically* distinguished to it being represented by an *actual algebraic cycle* — and the claims have not survived refereeing. Because these are not published, peer-reviewed results and the specific authors' claims have not been validated, the Atlas does **not** endorse any of them.
- The Clay Mathematics Institute requires publication in a refereed venue and a two-year waiting period before a prize claim is considered; **no claim has reached that bar.**

The honest summary: there are deep, genuine partial results and several precise equivalent reformulations, but **no correct proof, and no widely credited near-miss that the community treats as "almost there."** The principal obstruction is not a known logical barrier (unlike, say, the parity barrier in sieve theory or relativization in complexity) but the structural **scarcity of constructions producing high-codimension algebraic cycles** from cohomological data.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits at the intersection of **complex algebraic geometry, Hodge theory, and arithmetic geometry**. Core contemporary figures include Claire Voisin (Collège de France), Pierre Deligne (IAS, emeritus), and the **Hodge-loci / o-minimality** circle — Bruno Klingler, Benjamin Bakker, Jacob Tsimerman, and collaborators — who have reproved and extended Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan using definability (tame geometry). A second cluster works on **normal functions and singularities** (Gregory Pearlstein, Patrick Brosnan, Matt Kerr, Mark Green, and the late–career program of Griffiths). Arithmetic analogues (the **Tate conjecture**, motivated cycles) are pushed by Yves André, and by researchers on $K3$ surfaces and Shimura varieties. Much current activity is *indirect*: rather than attacking the full statement, the field accumulates structural results (algebraicity of Hodge loci, motivic realizations, special families) that the conjecture predicts. The problem is widely regarded as one of the hardest of the Millennium Problems, with no consensus route to resolution.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Status & Frontier — The Hodge Conjecture
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: OPEN.** A Clay Mathematics Institute Millennium Prize Problem (\$1,000,000), unresolved in every codimension $p \ge 2$. No accepted proof or refutation of the rational, smooth-projective statement exists.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Codimension 1 is settled:** the Lefschetz $(1,1)$-theorem realizes every integral $(1,1)$-class as a divisor class. This is the only complete case.
- **Algebraicity of Hodge loci** (Cattani–Deligne–Kaplan, 1995; reproved via tame geometry by Bakker–Klingler–Tsimerman, 2020): the locus where a Hodge class stays Hodge is algebraic — a prediction of the conjecture, confirmed without producing cycles.
- **Abelian varieties:** Deligne proved all Hodge classes there are **absolutely Hodge** (1982); the Hodge conjecture itself holds for many — but not all — abelian varieties (those with Hodge ring generated by divisors; numerous Mumford–Tate cases). Weil-type classes remain open.
- **Special families:** $K3$ and abelian surfaces, low-degree hypersurfaces and complete intersections, Fermat varieties of certain degrees, and various explicit Calabi–Yau threefolds are verified by exhibiting cycles directly.
- **Hard ceilings:** the **integral** Hodge conjecture is false (Atiyah–Hirzebruch, 1962; Kollár; Totaro), and the **Kähler** extension is false (Voisin, 2002) — pinning the live statement to *rational* coefficients on *projective* varieties.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Strongest conditional results
text:
- The **Lefschetz standard conjecture** (Grothendieck) would deliver major structural pieces (algebraicity of Künneth and Lefschetz operators).
- The **normal-function singularity program** (Green–Griffiths; Brosnan–Fang–Nie–Pearlstein) reduces the conjecture to producing singularities of admissible normal functions; the supporting algebraicity (zero loci) is proved, but the construction of cycles is not.
- **Motivated cycles** (André) realize many Hodge classes with cycle-like properties, conditionally bridging part of the gap.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: A proof must supply, for an *arbitrary* Hodge class on an arbitrary smooth projective $X$, an **actual algebraic cycle** (a $\mathbb{Q}$-combination of subvarieties) with that cohomology class. The central difficulty is the **structural scarcity of cycle constructions** in codimension $\ge 2$: cohomology offers no general recipe for producing subvarieties. Unlike sieve theory's parity barrier or complexity's relativization, there is no identified *logical* obstruction — but also no known engine of cycles. A disproof, conversely, would need a *projective* variety with a *rational* Hodge class provably not algebraic; all current counterexamples evade projectivity or rationality.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Tame geometry / o-minimality** pushing beyond algebraicity of Hodge loci toward effective cycle construction.
2. Settling the **standard conjectures** or the Tate conjecture in enough generality to transfer.
3. **Normal functions:** proving the predicted singularities exist.
4. New **motivic or derived-category** constructions producing high-codimension cycles.

title: The Hodge Conjecture
heading: Related problems
text:
- [Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer Conjecture](../birch-swinnerton-dyer/README.md) — sibling Millennium Problem; algebraic cycles / Chow groups and arithmetic of varieties.
- [Riemann Hypothesis](../riemann-hypothesis/README.md) — Millennium Problem; the function-field analogue (Weil conjectures) links to the standard conjectures.
- [P versus NP](../p-versus-np/README.md) — companion Millennium Prize Problem.
- [Yang–Mills Mass Gap](../yang-mills-mass-gap/README.md) — Millennium Problem at the geometry–physics interface.
- [Navier–Stokes Smoothness](../navier-stokes-smoothness/README.md) — Millennium Problem; PDE/analytic methods adjacent to Hodge-theoretic harmonic analysis.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: History — The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The Inscribed Square Problem — often called the **square peg problem** — asks whether every continuous simple closed curve (Jordan curve) in the Euclidean plane $\mathbb{R}^2$ contains four points that are the vertices of a square. The conjecture is that the answer is yes: every Jordan curve inscribes at least one square.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: How the problem arose
text: The problem was posed by **Otto Toeplitz** in 1911, at a meeting of the Deutsche Mathematiker-Vereinigung (German Mathematical Society) in Karlsruhe, where he announced the conjecture and reported partial results. It belongs to a family of "inscribed figure" questions that flourished in the early twentieth century alongside the rigorous development of plane topology, itself driven by Camille Jordan's curve theorem (1887) and the subsequent search for proofs (Veblen, 1905). Toeplitz's formulation is deceptively elementary — it requires no machinery to state — yet it resists elementary resolution because the class of Jordan curves includes wildly non-smooth, nowhere-differentiable, and fractal-like examples (e.g., curves with positive area cannot occur, but Koch-type and Osgood curves do). The central difficulty is precisely this generality: techniques that work for smooth or convex curves break down on rough curves.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Formulation and reformulations
text: The **modern formulation** is: for every injective continuous map $\gamma : S^1 \to \mathbb{R}^2$, the image $\gamma(S^1)$ contains four points forming a (non-degenerate) square. A key topological reformulation, due to the consideration of the configuration space of inscribed squares, recasts existence as a $\mathbb{Z}/4$-equivariant question or a question about the intersection/cobordism of certain surfaces in $\mathbb{R}^4$ — the approach exploited by Vaughan, Stromquist, and later by Hugelmeyer and Greene–Lobb. A natural generalization (the **rectangle peg** and **similar-quadrilateral** problems) asks which quadrilaterals can be inscribed; rectangles of every aspect ratio turn out to be more tractable on smooth curves than squares are on general curves.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1887** — Camille Jordan states the Jordan curve theorem, setting the topological backdrop for inscribed-figure problems.
- **1911** — Otto Toeplitz poses the square peg conjecture and announces partial results for convex curves.
- **1913** — Arnold Emch publishes a proof for smooth convex curves; over 1913–1916 he extends results to certain piecewise-analytic curves.
- **1929** — Lev Schnirelmann proves the conjecture for sufficiently smooth ($C^2$-type) curves; the argument is refined and corrected in later editions.
- **1944** — Ogilvy and others popularize and discuss the problem; partial results accumulate for special curve classes.
- **1961** — R. P. Jerrard establishes inscribed squares for analytic curves with a parity/transversality argument.
- **1989** — Walter Stromquist proves the conjecture for the broad class of "locally monotone" curves (including all piecewise-$C^1$ curves), one of the strongest general results.
- **1995** — H. Vaughan's slick topological argument shows every Jordan curve inscribes a **rectangle** (the Möbius-band/$\mathbb{R}^4$ method), influencing later square results.
- **2014–2017** — Benjamin Matschke writes an influential survey and obtains results for curves satisfying explicit smoothness/"$\varepsilon$" conditions; Terence Tao proves a square-peg variant for curves that are unions of Lipschitz graphs.
- **2020** — Joshua Greene and Andrew Lobb prove that every **smooth** Jordan curve inscribes rectangles of *every* aspect ratio, using symplectic geometry in $\mathbb{R}^4$ (Lagrangian tori); Cole Hugelmeyer obtains related rectangle-inscription results via knot/embedding theory.
- **2021–present** — Greene–Lobb and others extend the smooth rectangle results; the general (continuous, non-smooth) square case remains open. The frontier is bridging from smooth/locally-monotone curves to arbitrary Jordan curves.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Timeline
text: After more than a century, the conjecture is **proved for essentially every reasonable smoothness class** (convex, analytic, piecewise-$C^1$, locally monotone, bounded-variation) but **remains open for the full class of continuous Jordan curves**, where the lack of any smoothness obstructs the limiting and transversality arguments on which all known proofs rely.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Originator(s) — The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
text: _Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Otto Toeplitz (1881–1940)
text: **Otto Toeplitz** was a German mathematician of the early twentieth century, best known today for the Toeplitz matrices and Toeplitz operators that bear his name and form a cornerstone of functional analysis and operator theory. He posed the inscribed square problem in 1911, and although it occupies a small corner of his output, it has become one of the most enduring open questions in elementary plane topology.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Life
text:
Toeplitz was born on 1 August 1881 in Breslau (then in the German Empire, now Wrocław, Poland) into a family of mathematicians — both his father and grandfather were Gymnasium mathematics teachers. He studied at the University of Breslau, earning his doctorate in 1905 with a dissertation in algebraic geometry. He then moved to Göttingen, the world center of mathematics under David Hilbert and Felix Klein, where he was profoundly influenced by Hilbert's program on infinitely many variables and the theory of integral equations. This Göttingen period turned Toeplitz toward the analysis of bilinear and quadratic forms in infinitely many variables — work that produced the "Toeplitz forms" and the spectral theory of what are now called Toeplitz operators.

He held a professorship at the University of Kiel from 1913, then at Bonn from 1928. As a Jewish mathematician, Toeplitz was dismissed from his Bonn chair in 1935 under the Nazi racial laws. He devoted his final years in Germany to supporting Jewish students and to emigration relief efforts before himself emigrating to Palestine in 1939, where he worked in an administrative role at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem. He died in Jerusalem on 15 February 1940.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Mathematical background and motivation
text:
Toeplitz's deepest contributions lie in functional analysis: the Toeplitz–Hausdorff theorem (convexity of the numerical range), Toeplitz operators on Hardy spaces, and summability theory (the Silverman–Toeplitz theorem characterizing regular matrix summation methods). He was also a gifted expositor and historian of mathematics; with Hans Rademacher he wrote the classic popular book *Von Zahlen und Figuren* (1930, translated as *The Enjoyment of Mathematics*), and he championed the "genetic method" of teaching mathematics through its historical development.

It was this taste for vivid, geometrically intuitive problems — and the early-twentieth-century ferment around the Jordan curve theorem and the foundations of plane topology — that led him to the square peg question in 1911. The problem fit a broader contemporary interest in "inscribed configurations": questions about points on curves forced by topology rather than by metric rigidity. Toeplitz announced the conjecture together with a proof for convex curves, signaling from the outset that the hard case would be the wild, non-convex, non-smooth Jordan curves.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Legacy
text: Toeplitz's name is attached to objects used daily across analysis, numerical mathematics (Toeplitz and circulant matrices), signal processing, and probability. The inscribed square problem is a comparatively minor part of that legacy in volume but an outsized one in fame: it is a canonical example of a problem that any schoolchild can understand yet that has defied a century of effort. Its persistence vindicates Toeplitz's instinct that the generality of continuous curves — not their geometry — is where the real difficulty lies. The problem is now a flagship illustration of how genuinely elementary statements can sit just beyond the reach of modern topology and symplectic geometry.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Approaches — The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
text:
_Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

The known proofs all establish the conjecture on restricted classes of curves and then attempt — so far unsuccessfully in full generality — to pass to the limit of arbitrary continuous curves. The strategies fall into a few families.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Smoothing and limiting arguments
text: The core idea: prove inscribed squares for smooth curves, approximate an arbitrary Jordan curve $\gamma$ by smooth curves $\gamma_n \to \gamma$ uniformly, extract inscribed squares $S_n \subset \gamma_n$, and take a limit $S_n \to S$. **Best result:** this yields the conjecture for every curve in a class closed under good approximation and is the engine behind most special-case theorems. **Barrier:** the limit square $S$ may **degenerate to a single point**. A square inscribed in $\gamma_n$ can shrink as $n \to \infty$, so one needs a uniform lower bound on the size of the inscribed square — and no such bound is known for arbitrary continuous curves. Defeating this collapse is *the* central obstruction; it is why roughness, not non-convexity, is the enemy.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Parity / degree-theoretic counting (Emch, Schnirelmann, Jerrard)
text: For smooth or analytic curves one counts inscribed squares (or inscribed rectangles, or midpoint configurations) modulo 2 and shows the count is **odd**, hence nonzero. Emch (1913) used the medians/chords of convex curves; Schnirelmann (1929) and Guggenheimer gave a $C^2$ argument via a transversality/degree count; Jerrard (1961) ran a parity argument for analytic curves. **Best result:** the conjecture holds for $C^2$ (and analytic) curves with an *odd* — therefore stable, generically transverse — number of inscribed squares. **Barrier:** parity arguments need transversality of an auxiliary map, which requires smoothness; they say nothing when the curve is merely continuous, and the odd count can collapse under degeneration.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Locally monotone and bounded-variation curves (Stromquist)
text: Walter Stromquist (1989) proved the conjecture for **locally monotone** curves: curves that are, near each point, a monotone graph in some rotated coordinate frame. This class contains all piecewise-$C^1$ curves and all polygons. The method tracks "inscribed square" configurations through a continuous family and uses a connectedness/winding argument to force one to be a genuine square. **Best result:** one of the strongest *unconditional* general theorems; covers essentially every curve arising in practice. **Barrier:** local monotonicity is exactly the hypothesis that fails for genuinely wild curves (nowhere-monotone, fractal), which is where the open part of the problem lives.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Topology in $\mathbb{R}^4$: the Möbius band and surfaces (Vaughan, Hugelmeyer)
text: Encode an unordered pair of points of $\gamma$ as a point of the configuration space; the space of such pairs is a **Möbius band** whose boundary is $\gamma$. Vaughan (c. 1977, published 1995) used this to show every Jordan curve inscribes a **rectangle**, since the relevant map cannot embed the Möbius band in the plane without a coincidence. Hugelmeyer (2018–2021) lifted these ideas to smooth knot theory, mapping the inscribed-rectangle question to embeddings of tori/Klein bottles in $\mathbb{R}^4$ and recovering rectangle results for many aspect ratios on smooth curves. **Best result:** rectangles (any aspect ratio in the smooth case via later work), and a clean topological proof for *some* rectangles on *all* Jordan curves. **Barrier:** a square is a rectangle of aspect ratio 1 *plus* the extra equation that the diagonals be equal/perpendicular at $90°$ — the topology that forces a rectangle does not, by itself, force the additional square constraint, and the strongest square versions still require smoothness.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Symplectic geometry: Lagrangian tori (Greene–Lobb)
text: The most striking modern advance. Greene and Lobb (2020) reformulate inscribed rectangles for **smooth** Jordan curves as an intersection problem for Lagrangian tori in the symplectic 4-manifold $\mathbb{R}^4 \cong \mathbb{C}^2$. Using results on the non-displaceability/Floer-theoretic structure of monotone Lagrangian tori (à la Polterovich, Cieliebak–Mohnke), they prove that **every smooth Jordan curve inscribes a rectangle of every aspect ratio** — in particular a square. **Best result:** complete resolution of the *smooth* rectangle (hence smooth square) problem, a landmark. **Barrier:** the Lagrangian-torus machinery requires the curve to be smooth (or at least rectifiable enough to define the relevant Lagrangian submanifold); extending to merely continuous curves is open and appears to need genuinely new ideas, since the symplectic objects are not defined for wild curves.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Special quadrilaterals and inscribed-figure variants
text:
A parallel line studies which quadrilaterals are inscribable: every Jordan curve inscribes a rectangle (Vaughan); smooth curves inscribe all aspect-ratio rectangles (Greene–Lobb); cyclic quadrilaterals and other shapes give related theorems. **Best result:** broad families of inscribed shapes for smooth curves. **Barrier:** these results sharpen intuition but the square-on-continuous-curve case is strictly harder, sitting at the intersection of the degeneration problem and the loss of smoothness.

**Summary of the obstruction.** No fundamental impossibility (relativization-style barrier) is known — the conjecture is widely believed true. The single recurring obstacle is the **degeneration of inscribed squares to points under approximation**, which every smooth-case method must control and which no method yet controls for arbitrary continuous curves.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Attempts — The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
text:
_Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

The history of the square peg problem is one of steadily widening special cases rather than dramatic failed proofs. There is no famous retracted "complete solution," but several episodes of imprecision, gaps, and over-claimed generality are worth recording neutrally.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Emch's pioneering proofs (1913–1916)
text: Arnold Emch gave the first rigorous results, proving the conjecture for smooth convex curves and then for a class of piecewise-analytic curves. These are genuine theorems, but Emch's arguments rely on tangency and median-chord constructions that implicitly assume regularity; they do not extend to general curves, and Emch himself framed them as partial. They are best read as foundational special cases, not as attempts at the full conjecture.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Schnirelmann's smoothness hypothesis (1929) and its correction
text: Lev Schnirelmann's celebrated 1929 argument proved the conjecture for sufficiently smooth curves via a parity (mod-2) count of inscribed squares. The original statement and proof contained imprecisions about exactly how much smoothness was required and about the transversality of the auxiliary map. The result was later cleaned up and the smoothness hypothesis made explicit (a $C^2$-type condition suffices, with refinements by Guggenheimer and others). This is a near-miss in the sense that the *method* tantalizingly suggests a count that "should" survive to continuous curves, but the parity is exactly what can collapse under degeneration.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: The persistent degeneration gap
text: The most common error in informal or amateur attempts is to prove inscribed squares for smooth approximations $\gamma_n \to \gamma$ and then assert that the limiting configuration is a non-degenerate square. As emphasized in Stromquist's and Matschke's writings, the inscribed squares $S_n$ can shrink to a point in the limit; without a uniform size bound the argument is incomplete. Many proposed "elementary proofs" of the full conjecture circulating in the literature and online founder precisely here. The neutral statement of the objection is: *uniform non-degeneracy of inscribed squares under approximation has not been established for arbitrary continuous Jordan curves.*

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Stromquist's locally monotone theorem (1989) — a genuine high-water mark
text: Stromquist proved the conjecture for locally monotone curves, covering all piecewise-$C^1$ curves. This is a correct and influential result, not a disputed claim. It is a near-miss only in that the hypothesis (local monotonicity) is the precise feature that wild curves lack — so it brings the smooth/tame world fully into the fold while leaving the fractal frontier untouched.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Modern conditional results (Matschke; Tao)
text: Benjamin Matschke (2014) surveyed the field and obtained results under explicit metric/smoothness conditions, carefully flagging the boundary of what is proved. Terence Tao (2017) proved a square-peg-type theorem for curves that decompose as unions of Lipschitz graphs (a "two graphs" condition), with the honest caveat that the fully general continuous case is not implied. These are correct partial theorems whose authors are explicit about their limitations — exemplary in distinguishing what is proved from what is conjectured.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Greene–Lobb (2020) — resolved the smooth case, not the general one
text:
The symplectic-geometry breakthrough of Greene and Lobb settled inscribed rectangles of every aspect ratio for **smooth** curves. It was widely and correctly reported as a major advance, but some popular coverage blurred "smooth Jordan curve" into "every Jordan curve." For the record: the general continuous (non-smooth) square problem remained open after Greene–Lobb and remains open today.

**Bottom line.** There is no accepted solution to the full problem and no notorious fraudulent proof; the recurring methodological pitfall is the unjustified limiting argument, and the recurring reporting pitfall is conflating the solved smooth case with the open general case.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Notes
text: The active community is small but vibrant and currently centered on the symplectic and topological frontier opened by **Joshua Greene** (Boston College) and **Andrew Lobb** (Durham), whose Lagrangian-torus method settled the smooth rectangular peg problem and triggered a wave of follow-up work on cyclic quadrilaterals and aspect-ratio questions. **Cole Hugelmeyer** advanced the knot-theoretic/$\mathbb{R}^4$-embedding viewpoint, and **Richard Schwartz** has pursued the structure of inscribed rectangles in general (non-smooth) Jordan loops, probing exactly the wild-curve regime where the conjecture remains open. **Benjamin Matschke** and **Terence Tao** remain key reference points for the configuration-space and analytic approaches respectively. The historical pillars — Toeplitz, Emch, Schnirelmann, Jerrard, and Stromquist — define the special-case ladder that any general proof must ultimately subsume. The open problem now is essentially a single hard step: removing all smoothness and controlling the degeneration of inscribed squares for arbitrary continuous curves.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Status & Frontier — The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open (active progress).** The general conjecture — *every continuous simple closed curve in $\mathbb{R}^2$ inscribes a square* — remains **unproved** for arbitrary Jordan curves. It is proved on every well-behaved subclass, and recent symplectic methods have closed the smooth case for the closely related rectangle problem.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Convex curves:** Inscribed squares exist (Emch, 1913). Elementary and complete.
- **Smooth / analytic curves:** Schnirelmann (1929) and Jerrard (1961) prove existence via parity counts; the inscribed-square count is odd for generic smooth curves.
- **Locally monotone curves:** Stromquist (1989) proves the conjecture for all locally monotone curves, which include every piecewise-$C^1$ curve and every polygon. This is the strongest broad unconditional result and covers essentially all curves met in practice.
- **Lipschitz-graph unions:** Tao (2017) proves a square-peg theorem for curves that are unions of two Lipschitz graphs with small constant — an analytic route avoiding heavy topology.
- **Rectangles, all aspect ratios, smooth curves:** Greene and Lobb (2020) prove that **every smooth Jordan curve inscribes a rectangle of every aspect ratio**, in particular a square. This resolves the *smooth* square peg problem and is the headline recent advance.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: What is known (conditional / partial)
text:
- Inscribed **rectangles** (some aspect ratio) exist on *every* Jordan curve, smooth or not (Vaughan's Möbius-band argument). The square is the rectangle constraint *plus* equal diagonals at $90°$, which the pure topology does not by itself force.
- Equivariant-topology and configuration-space methods (Matschke) give existence under explicit smoothness/$\varepsilon$ hypotheses but not for arbitrary continuous curves.
- Richard Schwartz and others have mapped out structural dichotomies for inscribed rectangles in general Jordan loops, isolating the genuinely wild regime.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: What a full resolution requires
text: A complete proof must handle **arbitrary continuous curves with no smoothness**, where the obstruction is uniform: prove that inscribed squares (or the relevant configuration intersections) **do not degenerate to a point** in the limit of a smooth approximation $\gamma_n \to \gamma$. Every special-case method secures a non-degenerate square via transversality, monotonicity, or a Lagrangian non-displaceability result that needs the curve to be smooth or tame. Removing that hypothesis — either by a new compactness/size bound or by a topological invariant defined directly for continuous curves — is the missing step.

title: The Inscribed Square Problem (Toeplitz)
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. **Extend the symplectic method off the smooth class.** Define the relevant Lagrangian objects (or a Floer-theoretic count) for rectifiable or merely continuous curves and recover the square as the aspect-ratio-1 rectangle. This is the most promising current direction but the symplectic data is not yet available for wild curves.
2. **A uniform non-degeneracy bound.** Prove a quantitative lower bound on the size of an inscribed square in terms of the geometry of $\gamma$, making the limiting argument valid for all continuous curves.
3. **Equivariant topology with weaker hypotheses.** Push Matschke-style $\mathbb{Z}/4$ configuration-space arguments down to the continuous category.

The consensus expectation is that the conjecture is **true**; no impossibility or barrier result is known. What is lacking is a way to convert the abundant smooth-case successes into a statement about the full, untamed class of Jordan curves.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Plain statement
text: Does every bounded linear operator on a separable infinite-dimensional complex Hilbert space have a non-trivial closed invariant subspace?

*Originators: John von Neumann (informal).*

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: History — The Invariant Subspace Problem
text:
_Origin, formulation, and timeline._

The invariant subspace problem grew out of the spectral theory of operators that flowered in the 1930s. For a bounded linear operator $T$ on a Banach or Hilbert space $X$, a closed subspace $M \subseteq X$ is **invariant** if $T M \subseteq M$. The subspaces $\{0\}$ and $X$ are always invariant; the question is whether a *non-trivial* one — a closed subspace other than $\{0\}$ and $X$ — must always exist.

In finite dimensions the answer is trivially yes over $\mathbb{C}$: every operator has an eigenvalue, and the corresponding eigenvector spans a one-dimensional invariant subspace. The infinite-dimensional case is radically different, because a bounded operator need not have any eigenvalue at all (the unilateral shift on $\ell^2$ is the canonical example). The problem therefore probes whether the spectral organization familiar from finite-dimensional and compact operators persists for *arbitrary* bounded operators.

**The precise modern formulation** — what is usually meant by "the invariant subspace problem" — is the Hilbert-space case:

> Does every bounded linear operator $T$ on a separable, infinite-dimensional, complex Hilbert space $H$ admit a non-trivial closed invariant subspace?

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: History — The Invariant Subspace Problem
text:
> Does every bounded linear operator $T$ on a separable, infinite-dimensional, complex Hilbert space $H$ admit a non-trivial closed invariant subspace?

Several variants matter historically. The **Banach-space version** asks the same for general separable infinite-dimensional Banach spaces; this is now known to be **false** (Enflo, Read). The **hyperinvariant** variant asks for a subspace invariant under every operator commuting with $T$. A **transitive-algebra** reformulation casts everything in terms of weakly closed subalgebras of $B(H)$ acting transitively on $H$. Separability is essential: on a non-separable space the cyclic subspace $\overline{\{p(T)x : p \text{ polynomial}\}}$ is automatically non-trivial and invariant, so the difficulty is genuinely an infinite-dimensional, separable phenomenon.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Timeline
text:
- **1929–1932** — von Neumann's spectral-theory program (culminating in *Mathematische Grundlagen der Quantenmechanik*, 1932) sets the stage; invariant subspaces are implicit in the structure theory of operator algebras.
- **1935** — John von Neumann reportedly proves, in unpublished work, that every **compact** operator on a Hilbert space has a non-trivial invariant subspace, and is credited with first posing the general problem informally around this date.
- **1950** — Beurling characterizes all invariant subspaces of the unilateral shift on the Hardy space $H^2$ via inner functions — a structural landmark that becomes a template for the field.
- **1954** — Aronszajn and Smith publish a proof that every compact operator on a Banach space has a non-trivial invariant subspace, recovering von Neumann's result in print and extending it beyond Hilbert space.
- **1966–1968** — Bernstein and Robinson prove the polynomially compact case using non-standard analysis; Halmos immediately recasts the argument in standard terms.
- **1973** — **Lomonosov's theorem**: any bounded operator commuting with a non-zero compact operator has a non-trivial *hyperinvariant* subspace. The proof, via Schauder's fixed-point theorem, is short and subsumes essentially all earlier positive results.
- **1975** — Hilden gives an elementary proof of a Lomonosov-type statement avoiding the fixed-point theorem.
- **1975–1987** — **Per Enflo** constructs a bounded operator on a Banach space with *no* non-trivial invariant subspace. The construction circulates from 1975; the definitive paper appears in *Acta Mathematica* in **1987**.
- **1978** — Scott Brown proves that every subnormal operator has a non-trivial invariant subspace, introducing the "Scott Brown technique" of dual-algebra methods.
- **1984–1986** — **Charles Read** produces a much simpler Banach-space counterexample, then a celebrated operator on $\ell^1$ with no non-trivial invariant subspace, settling the Banach-space problem decisively in the negative.
- **1987–1988** — Brown, Chevreau, and Pearcy show that contractions whose spectrum contains the unit circle have invariant subspaces, broadening the dual-algebra reach.
- **1990s–2010s** — Steady positive results for structured classes (subnormal, hyponormal, certain perturbations of normal operators), while the general Hilbert-space case resists every method.
- **2023** — Per Enflo posts a preprint claiming that *every* bounded operator on a separable Hilbert space has a non-trivial invariant subspace. As of this writing the claim is **under scrutiny and not accepted**; no peer-reviewed confirmation exists.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Timeline
text: The Hilbert-space problem remains open. Its defining tension — provably false for Banach spaces, undecided for Hilbert spaces — is what makes it one of operator theory's central questions.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Originator(s) — The Invariant Subspace Problem
text:
_Biography, background, and the ideas that led here._

The problem is universally attributed to **John von Neumann (1903–1957)**, who is credited with posing it informally in the mid-1930s. No single founding paper exists — von Neumann's relevant result on compact operators was never formally published by him — which is itself characteristic of how the question entered the field: as a natural next step in a research program rather than as a dramatic conjecture.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: John von Neumann
text:
Born Neumann János Lajos in Budapest in 1903, von Neumann was a prodigy who absorbed advanced mathematics as a teenager. He took a chemical-engineering diploma in Zurich and a doctorate in mathematics from Pázmány Péter University in Budapest (1926), with a thesis on set theory. After postdoctoral work in Göttingen under Hilbert's influence and lectureships in Berlin and Hamburg, he moved to the United States, joining Princeton in 1930 and becoming one of the first faculty of the Institute for Advanced Study in 1933.

Von Neumann's mathematical range was unmatched in his generation: foundations of set theory, the axiomatization of quantum mechanics, the spectral theory of unbounded operators, ergodic theory, the theory of operator algebras (now "von Neumann algebras"), game theory and economics, numerical analysis, and the logical design of the stored-program computer. The invariant subspace problem sits squarely in his operator-theoretic work of the late 1920s and 1930s.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: The ideas and motivation
text:
The problem is the direct heir of von Neumann's **spectral theorem** program. For self-adjoint and, more generally, normal operators, the spectral theorem furnishes a rich supply of invariant (indeed reducing) subspaces — the spectral projections. For *compact* operators, the Riesz–Schauder theory shows the spectrum away from $0$ consists of eigenvalues with finite-dimensional eigenspaces, again yielding invariant subspaces. Von Neumann's contribution was to confirm that compact operators on Hilbert space always have non-trivial invariant subspaces and to ask the obvious follow-up: must this hold for *every* bounded operator?

The motivation was structural. A central theme of von Neumann's work was the decomposition of operators and algebras into simpler pieces — a program that produced the type classification of von Neumann algebras and the reduction theory of direct integrals. An invariant subspace is the most basic form of such a decomposition: it lets one "triangularize" an operator, restricting it to $M$ and compressing it to $H/M$. The hope was that every bounded operator, however wild, retains at least this much structure. The question also resonates with quantum mechanics, where invariant subspaces correspond to subsystems preserved by a dynamics.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Historical root vs. modern formulation
text: The historical root and the modern formulation differ in scope rather than in spirit. Von Neumann's actual *theorem* concerned compact operators; the *problem* he is credited with raising is the general bounded case. Over the following decades the question was sharpened in two directions. First, the **Banach-space** generalization was studied in parallel and eventually resolved negatively (Enflo 1987, Read 1984–1986), which retroactively clarified that the genuinely open question is the Hilbert-space case, where the geometry is far more rigid. Second, the **hyperinvariant** and **transitive-algebra** reformulations gave the problem its modern operator-algebraic phrasing. The version stated in this dossier's metadata — bounded operators on a separable infinite-dimensional complex Hilbert space — is the one the community now treats as "the" invariant subspace problem.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Legacy
text: Von Neumann died in 1957, decades before the Banach-space case fell and long before the Hilbert-space question reached its current frontier. His broader legacy — operator algebras, spectral theory, the mathematics of quantum mechanics and computation — frames almost the entire toolkit later brought to bear on the problem: dual algebras, functional models, and the structure theory of $B(H)$ all descend from his work. That the question he raised most casually remains open, while so much he built around it has been completed, is a recurring irony noted by operator theorists.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Approaches — The Invariant Subspace Problem
text: _Major strategies, partial results, and barriers._

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Compactness and Lomonosov's theorem
text:
The oldest and most successful line exploits **compactness**. Von Neumann (1935, unpublished) and Aronszajn–Smith (1954) showed every compact operator has a non-trivial invariant subspace. The high-water mark is **Lomonosov's theorem (1973)**: if a bounded operator $T$ commutes with a non-zero compact operator $K$, then $T$ has a non-trivial *hyperinvariant* subspace. The proof is a clever application of Schauder's fixed-point theorem to the algebra generated by $T$. This single result implies almost every prior positive theorem and covers a vast class of operators.

**Barrier.** The method is fundamentally about operators that "see" a compact operator in their commutant. Hadwin, Nordgren, Radjavi, and Rosenthal (1980) exhibited an operator commuting with *no* non-zero compact operator, showing Lomonosov's theorem cannot resolve the general problem. Compactness-based arguments are therefore intrinsically limited to a structured subclass.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Functional models and the Beurling program
text:
Beurling's 1950 theorem describing the shift-invariant subspaces of $H^2$ via inner functions inspired a program of **functional models**: realize an operator concretely (as multiplication, as a shift, as a compression to a model space) and read off its invariant subspaces from analytic data. The Sz.-Nagy–Foiaş model theory for contractions is the mature form. This succeeds spectacularly for shifts, $C_0$ contractions, and operators with rich functional calculus.

**Barrier.** Model theory needs enough analytic structure — a unitary dilation with tractable characteristic function. A generic bounded operator, or even a generic contraction, may have a characteristic function too pathological to analyze, so the models do not reach the full problem.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Dual algebras and the Scott Brown technique
text:
Scott Brown's 1978 proof that **subnormal operators** have invariant subspaces introduced **dual-algebra methods**: one works in the predual of the weak-* closed algebra generated by $T$ and solves systems of equations $\langle T^n x, y\rangle = \langle \mu, z^n\rangle$ using factorization in the predual. Brown, Chevreau, and Pearcy (1988) pushed this to show **contractions whose spectrum contains the unit circle** have invariant subspaces; Apostol, Bercovici, Foiaş, and Pearcy developed the property $(\mathbb{A}_{\aleph_0})$ machinery that systematizes when the technique applies.

**Barrier.** The method requires the predual factorization to succeed, which is governed by the spectral picture and the position of the operator relative to the unit circle. Contractions whose spectrum is a "thin" subset of the disk, and non-power-bounded operators, fall outside its reach. There is no known way to make dual-algebra methods bite on an arbitrary bounded operator.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Hyponormal, subnormal, and structured classes
text:
A long tradition proves the result for ever-larger structured classes: normal operators (trivially, via the spectral theorem), subnormal operators (Scott Brown 1978), hyponormal operators with thick spectrum, quasinilpotent perturbations of normals, operators with non-trivial point or residual spectrum, and so on. Each result chips away at the space of potential counterexamples.

**Barrier.** These are existence theorems for specific niches; they do not aggregate into a general proof. The hard core is the class of operators with no spectral or structural handle — e.g., quasinilpotent operators with trivial commutant — precisely where every structured method fails.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: The Banach-space counterexamples (a negative landmark)
text:
For **Banach spaces** the problem is settled negatively. **Enflo (1976/1987)** constructed a bounded operator on a Banach space with no non-trivial invariant subspace, and **Read (1984–1986)** gave simpler examples, including a celebrated operator on $\ell^1$. Read further built operators that are quasinilpotent with no invariant subspace, sharpening how pathological the constructions are.

**What this tells us about Hilbert space.** The counterexamples rely on geometric freedom unavailable in Hilbert space: bases far from orthonormal, the absence of an inner product, and norms that permit delicate "shifting with feedback" constructions. The proofs use the flexibility of general Banach norms in an essential way, which is exactly why they do not transfer. This is the closest thing the field has to a "barrier result": it shows a purely soft, structure-free approach *can* yield a counterexample in the Banach category, so any proof of the Hilbert-space affirmative must use Hilbert-space-specific rigidity (orthogonality, the parallelogram law, polar decomposition, $C^*$-structure).

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Random, computational, and approximation methods
text:
More recent work probes whether **approximation, perturbation, or quantitative methods** can force invariant subspaces: showing every operator is a small perturbation of one with an invariant subspace, or studying "almost-invariant" subspaces (subspaces $M$ with $\dim P_{M^\perp} T P_M < \infty$). Androulakis, Popov, Tcaciuc, Troitsky and others proved that **every operator on a Banach space has almost-invariant half-spaces**, a striking partial result.

**Barrier.** Almost-invariant is strictly weaker than invariant, and the perturbation arguments do not localize to a fixed operator. Bridging from "almost" to "exactly" invariant remains open and is widely regarded as the crux of any quantitative attack.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Attempts — The Invariant Subspace Problem
text: _Notable attempts, near-misses, retracted proofs._

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: The compact-operator chain (positive near-misses toward generality)
text: The earliest "attempts" were successful for restricted classes and repeatedly raised hopes of a general proof. Von Neumann's unpublished compact-operator theorem (c. 1935), the Aronszajn–Smith published version (1954), and the Bernstein–Robinson polynomially-compact result (1966) — proved with Abraham Robinson's non-standard analysis and immediately translated to standard language by Halmos — each looked like a step toward the full problem. **Lomonosov's theorem (1973)** was the most dramatic: its proof was so short and general that many expected it, or a refinement, to settle everything. The Hadwin–Nordgren–Radjavi–Rosenthal example (1980) of an operator with no compact operator in its commutant showed the Lomonosov route is intrinsically bounded, ending that hope.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: The Banach-space resolution (a celebrated negative result)
text: **Per Enflo's counterexample** is the most famous near-miss in the *opposite* direction. Enflo announced a Banach-space operator with no non-trivial invariant subspace in the mid-1970s; the manuscript was notoriously long and difficult, and its refereeing took over a decade, finally appearing in *Acta Mathematica* in **1987**. Before publication, its correctness was itself a matter of community discussion — an instructive case of a hard construction taking years to be validated. **Charles Read** then produced dramatically simpler counterexamples (1984–1986), including an operator on $\ell^1$ and quasinilpotent examples, which were quickly checked and accepted. These results are not disputed; they definitively answer the Banach-space question in the negative while leaving Hilbert space open.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: The Hilbert-space frontier and the 2023 Enflo claim
text:
For Hilbert space, the strongest accepted positive results stop well short of the general case: subnormal operators (Scott Brown 1978), contractions with spectrum meeting the unit circle (Brown–Chevreau–Pearcy 1988), and various hyponormal and perturbation classes. None has been extended to all bounded operators despite sustained effort.

The most prominent recent development is a **2023 preprint by Per Enflo** claiming a proof that *every* bounded operator on a separable Hilbert space has a non-trivial invariant subspace. The claim drew wide attention given Enflo's stature and his earlier Banach-space work in the same area. **The dispute, stated neutrally:** as of this writing the proof has **not** been peer-reviewed, published, or accepted by the operator-theory community. Experts have noted that the manuscript is intricate and that independent verification of its central construction has not been completed; no specific, agreed-upon error has been published, but neither has the proof been confirmed correct. The standard interpretation is therefore that the problem **remains open** and the claim is unverified. A separate 2023 preprint by another author also circulated; it likewise lacks accepted verification. Readers should treat both as claimed-but-unconfirmed until peer review concludes.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Other partial advances often cited
text:
- **Almost-invariant subspaces** (Androulakis, Popov, Tcaciuc, Troitsky, and collaborators, late 2000s onward): every operator on an infinite-dimensional Banach space has almost-invariant half-spaces. A genuine theorem, but "almost" cannot yet be upgraded to "invariant."
- **Quasitriangularity and Apostol–Foiaş–Voiculescu theory** (1970s–80s): deep structural results on approximation of operators that constrain where counterexamples could live, without producing invariant subspaces directly.

No retracted *published* proof of the general Hilbert-space case is on record; the pattern instead is a series of correct partial results and, recently, a high-profile unverified claim. The honest summary: the affirmative Hilbert-space statement has never been established to the community's satisfaction.

No identifier has been invented; every "—" reflects a deliberate omission rather than a fabricated number. The 2023 Enflo preprint (#25) is listed as a notable development, not as an accepted resolution — see status.md.

| 6 | Scott W. Brown | 20th–21st c. | breakthrough | Proved subnormal operators have invariant subspaces (1978), introducing the dual-algebra "Scott Brown technique." |
| 7 | Per Enflo | b. 1944 | breakthrough | Constructed the first Banach-space operator with no non-trivial invariant subspace (1976/1987); author of the disputed 2023 Hilbert-space claim. |
| 8 | Charles J. Read | 1958–2015 | breakthrough | Gave simpler Banach-space counterexamples and the $\ell^1$ example (1984–1986), plus quasinilpotent counterexamples (1997). |
| 9 | Carl Pearcy | b. 1935 | frontier | With Brown, Chevreau, Bercovici, and Foiaş, developed dual-algebra theory and the contraction results extending the positive frontier. |

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Notes
text: The active community sits within operator theory and functional analysis, clustered around dual-algebra methods, model theory of contractions (Sz.-Nagy–Foiaş lineage), and concrete operator theory on function spaces. Schools associated with Bercovici, Foiaş, and Pearcy (dual algebras), and with workers on subnormal and hyponormal operators, remain central; surveys by Chalendar and Partington reflect the concrete-operator viewpoint. The 2023 Enflo preprint reinvigorated attention, prompting renewed scrutiny of the Hilbert-space case, though no resolution is accepted. Adjacent activity on almost-invariant subspaces (Androulakis, Popov, Troitsky, Tcaciuc and collaborators) represents the most active quantitative front. The field is small but durable, with progress measured in ever-larger structured operator classes rather than a single decisive advance.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Status & Frontier — The Invariant Subspace Problem
text:
_Where the problem stands and what a resolution would require._

**Status: open (active progress).** The Hilbert-space invariant subspace problem — does every bounded operator on a separable, infinite-dimensional, complex Hilbert space have a non-trivial closed invariant subspace? — has **not** been resolved to the satisfaction of the operator-theory community.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: What is known (unconditional)
text:
- **Compact and Lomonosov class.** Every compact operator has a non-trivial invariant subspace (von Neumann; Aronszajn–Smith 1954). More generally, any operator commuting with a non-zero compact operator has a non-trivial *hyperinvariant* subspace (Lomonosov 1973). This is the broadest general positive theorem.
- **Normal and subnormal operators.** Normal operators have invariant subspaces via the spectral theorem; subnormal operators do too (Scott Brown 1978).
- **Contractions with thick spectrum.** Contractions whose spectrum contains the unit circle have invariant subspaces (Brown–Chevreau–Pearcy 1988); various hyponormal and dual-algebra classes are covered by the property-$(\mathbb{A})$ machinery.
- **Spectral handles.** Any operator with a non-trivial point spectrum, residual spectrum, or a disconnected spectrum yields invariant subspaces via the Riesz functional calculus.
- **Almost-invariant subspaces.** Every operator on an infinite-dimensional Banach space has almost-invariant half-spaces (Androulakis–Popov–Tcaciuc–Troitsky and collaborators) — a genuine but strictly weaker result.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: What is known (the negative side, other categories)
text: - **Banach spaces: false.** Enflo (1976/1987) and Read (1984–1986, including an operator on $\ell^1$) constructed bounded operators with *no* non-trivial invariant subspace. Read (1997) gave quasinilpotent counterexamples. These use Banach-space geometric freedom unavailable in Hilbert space.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: The disputed 2023 claim
text: In 2023, **Per Enflo** posted a preprint, *On the invariant subspace problem in Hilbert spaces* (arXiv:2305.15442), claiming that every bounded operator on a separable Hilbert space has a non-trivial invariant subspace. The claim attracted significant attention but, as of this writing, has **not** been peer-reviewed, published, or accepted; independent verification of the central construction is incomplete, and no agreed-upon error has been published either. The community's working position remains that the problem is open. This dossier records the claim as a notable development, not a resolution. (A separate, unrelated 2023 preprint by another author also circulated without accepted verification.)

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: What a full resolution would require
text: An **affirmative** proof must produce a non-trivial invariant subspace for *arbitrary* bounded $T$, including the hardest case: operators with trivial commutant, no compact operators nearby, quasinilpotent spectrum (so no spectral handle), and no usable functional model. Because the Banach-space counterexamples show a structure-free construction can defeat the affirmative, any successful Hilbert-space proof must exploit **Hilbert-space-specific rigidity** — orthogonality, the parallelogram law, polar decomposition, or $C^*$-algebraic structure — in an essential way. A **negative** resolution would require a Hilbert-space operator provably lacking invariant subspaces, which decades of effort and the rigidity of the inner-product geometry have so far prevented.

title: The Invariant Subspace Problem
heading: Plausible routes
text:
1. Upgrading **almost-invariant** to invariant via a quantitative or fixed-point argument tailored to Hilbert space.
2. Extending **dual-algebra / Scott Brown** methods past the unit-circle-spectrum barrier to general contractions.
3. New **functional models** for quasinilpotent or trivial-commutant operators.
4. A genuinely new structural invariant detecting hidden invariant subspaces — or, conversely, a Read-style construction adapted to the Hilbert norm.

title: The Jacobian Conjecture
heading: Plain statement
text: A polynomial map from C^n to C^n with constant nonzero Jacobian determinant has a polynomial inverse.
